At that ball Rosalie placed in the Duchess’s hand all of Albert’s letters, the Abbe de Grancey’s letter to Leopold Hannequin, the notary’s reply, and Rosalie’s own confession to the Abbe.

“I will not be the only sufferer,” she told her rival, “for one has been as ruthless as the other.” Having savored the dismay on the Duchess’s face, Rosalie withdrew from public view for a time and then returned with her mother to Besancon.

Thus the sequence of events—an arrival of a mysterious stranger that precipitated Savaron’s flight, the clerical and legal scramble that followed, Rosalie’s secret forgery and subsequent confession, the Baron’s death, Albert’s renunciation and retreat to the Grand Chartreuse, and Rosalie’s bitter, strategized confrontation with the woman she had wronged—closed with Rosalie socially elevated and wealthy but inwardly burdened. Her outward victory in forcing the Duchess to see the truth of her acts was matched by the eternal solitude Albert chose and that Rosalie had ultimately helped bring about.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone at Les Rouxey—riding, hunting, rejecting two or three offers a year, wintering often in Besançon and improving her land. Regarded as eccentric, she was a celebrity of the Eastern provinces.

Madame de Soulas has two children, a boy and a girl, and seems younger; Monsieur de Soulas has aged noticeably.

“My fortune has cost me dear,” he said to young Chavoncourt. “Really to know a bigot it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”

Mademoiselle de Watteville behaved extraordinarily—“she has vagaries,” people said. Each year she went to gaze at the walls of the Grande Chartreuse; perhaps she fancied, like her grand-uncle Watteville—who once broke through monastery walls to regain his liberty—that she might force the monastery's walls to find a husband.

She left Besançon in 1841, purportedly to marry, but returned in a state that forbids her appearing in society. By the chance the Abbé de Grancey mentioned, she was on a Loire steamboat whose boiler burst. She was so severely injured that she lost her right arm and her left leg; her face bears fearful scars that have taken her beauty; her health is shattered and she has few days free from suffering. In short, she never leaves the Chartreuse of Les Rouxey, where she lives wholly devoted to religious practices.

PARIS, May 1842.

Produced by John Bickers and Dagny

LETTERS OF TWO BRIDES

By Honoré de Balzac

Translated by R. S. Scott

DEDICATION

To George Sand

Your name, dear George, lends a radiance my book cannot increase. I place it here not from vanity or diffidence but to testify to a solid friendship that has outlasted wanderings and malignity. The friendly names attached to my works console me amid the vexations of an ever-growing output. Each new book brings fresh annoyance—accusations of undue prolixity, as if my pen vied with the world for fertility! Would it not be fine if an antiquarian of dead literatures found in this list only great names and generous hearts, friends bound by pure ties? I would rather pride myself on such possession than on an unstable popularity. For him who knows you well, it is happiness to sign himself,

Your friend,
DE BALZAC.

PARIS, June 1840.

LETTERS OF TWO BRIDES

FIRST PART

I. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENÉE DE MAUCOMBE. PARIS, September.

Sweetheart, I too am free! Unless you have already written to Blois, I am the first. Keep those great black eyes of yours fixed: I save the account of my first love for another letter. By the way, why always “first”? Is there such a thing as a second?

Don't urge me on; tell me how you escaped the convent. As for me, my deliverance was prosaic: alarmed conscience overcame strict policy. After you left, a heavy melancholy seized me; my aunt did not wish me to pine away and my mother, whose one cure was a novitiate, gave way.

So I am in Paris, thanks to you, my love! When we parted you must have been proud of the impression you left on me. We had lived so constantly together—sharing dreams, letting fancy range—that our souls seemed welded; the Carmelite routine that crushes interest had become for us a world of imagination. Without you I became a true Carmelite; the sombre regularity closed in on me. My aunt, content in her tiny convent paradise, could not understand my revolt. A religious life for young girls demands either extreme simplicity or ardour for sacrifice—qualities she saw only in relation to family, not to unknown ideals.

For a fortnight I stifled words and stored up things to tell you; letter-writing was my sole remedy. Then one morning—a red-letter day—arrived a lady companion and Philippe, my grandmother’s last valet, to carry me off. My aunt kissed my forehead and spoke of God’s elect and of passion; her kiss burned with that consuming fire which has clouded her life. I kissed her hands and promised not to be made a victim of a broken heart. She smiled and escorted me to the carriage.

At Beaugency night found me stunned by parting words and wondering what awaited in the world I had hungered for. No one received me: my mother was at the Bois de Boulogne, my father at the Council, my brother seldom home. Miss Griffith and Philippe took me to my rooms—the suite of my beloved grandmother, the Princess de Vauremont, whose fortune bequeathed me some provision. Entering the rooms where she had died revived all the memories; I wept as if she had gone but yesterday. Many objects remained, showing the indifference of those busy with state. Philippe said the furniture was left to me and that my father postponed restoring the larger suites, hoping for a law to restore property values—an architect estimated three hundred thousand livres’ damage. The thought that my father might have spared money to restore the house rather than arrange my marriage angered me.

Alone, I found consolation in my grandmother’s presence. Her rooms—bedroom, morning-room, drawing-room—were old-world, yellow damask, Savonnerie tapestry from Louis XV., a timepiece from Maréchal de Saxe, china from Maréchal de Richelieu. Her portrait at twenty-five faced the King’s; the Prince, her husband, was conspicuous by absence—an honest negligence characteristic of her. I remember her as a coquette to the end, arranging herself as if her portrait were constantly painted. She had a subtle, concise wit and free-thinking views that shaped me. I had been abruptly sent away to the convent in 1816; she had warned she would prevent me being made a victim, and six months later she died. Her will had been entrusted to Prince de Talleyrand, who intimated to me that the Princess forbade my taking the vows. I hope to learn more from him.

Thus, if no fleshly person received me at first, the Princess’s shade did. I began our journal, resolved to report even trifles. Send me a faithful picture of your household; tell me everything, even the sunset among your tall trees.

October 19th.

I arrived at three; my mother returned by five. Her suite is directly below mine and we share a secret stair. I wondered how she, who had written me only twice in eight years, would greet me. She put aside pretence and spoke as a kind and sincere friend, kissing my forehead.

“My dear little one,” she said, “if you were to die at the convent it is better to live with your family. You frustrate our plans, but blind obedience is past. I wish to be a sister to you and counsel you.”

She was charming and at thirty-eight exquisitely beautiful—dark-blue eyes, smooth forehead, delightful hands and form. I responded simply and genuinely; her pride in me touched her and she promised we would remain friends. She advised that I defer my first appearance in society until dressed, and after seeing my father and brother I could retire and prepare.

My father entered, affectionate, playing his part so well I believed his heart was involved. He kissed me like a lover and spoke of watching my success in society to compensate for disappointment at my change of vocation. He presented me to my brother Alphonse, the Duc de Rhetore, who kissed my hand. He took my side against his father; though I noted he might have come to Blois when his regiment was at Orléans.

Fearing strangers, I withdrew to arrange my rooms and begin writing to you. Thus ended the return of an eighteen-year-old after nine years away to the bosom of one of the noblest families. Tired, I slept early in my grandmother’s bed, finding even a little Saxe service preserved there.

II. THE SAME TO THE SAME November 25th.

Next day the rooms were dusted and Philippe put flowers in the vases. But a Carmelite’s appetite surprised the household; Rose had trouble serving breakfast. At one o’clock my father found me writing to you and handed me a purse.

“My dear,” he said, “you will need clothes and comfort. In this purse are twelve thousand francs yearly for your expenses. Arrange about a governess with your mother. A carriage and man-servant shall be at your disposal.”

“Let me keep Philippe,” I begged.

“So be it,” he replied. “You have your own money; your grandmother left you five hundred thousand francs in government stock yielding about forty thousand francs a year. I had intended it for your second brother, but you upset my plans. It shall be for you; I have confidence in your good sense.”

His frankness pleased me though I wondered who had relented after nine years. Philippe dashed off to arrange my transformation. Victorine the dressmaker, the corset-maker, the shoemaker came; tradespeople take time. Janssen declared I had my mother’s foot. A glovemaker measured my hand. The morning passed in these serious concerns. My mother told me we were to go to the milliner; this new independence dazzled me. I felt like a blind man restored to sight. I began to grasp the gulf between a Carmelite sister and a society girl.

At lunch my father seemed absent—deep in the King’s confidence—and I saw how little my parents shared besides name and rank. The essence of their lives seemed lived outside the family. My mother’s days are fuller than I imagined—frequent toilettes, driving, receptions, theatres and balls; she leaves little time to herself. We meet at lunch and dinner but often dine separately. Paris life, with its nightly whirl, fosters separations even in a household. In this style of life members of the same family can share no interior knowledge of each other.

I had not yet set foot in the streets—ignorant and waiting until I cease to be a country cousin and have suitable dress and deportment. The Italian opera opens soon; my mother has a box and I am mad with delight. Already I drop convent habits: evenings I write to you until ten, the hour my mother leaves for the theatre. There are twelve theatres in Paris.

I read indiscriminately and learn slowly. Modern novels I have read focus on love—our old subject—but how inferior they are to our little dialogues, Renée! Two books pleased me—Corinne and Adolphe. I once asked my father if Mme. de Staël could be seen; the family laughed: “She’s dead,” my mother said gently. Miss Griffith, the governess I chose—an English clergyman’s daughter, thirty-six, Scotch, proud but kind—sleeps in Rose’s room and will be my chaperon. She has discerned I am not a mere ignoramus and warns me not to be dazzled by society, always to be on guard—especially against what attracts most. Her warnings are monotonous but sincere.

III. THE SAME TO THE SAME December.

My darling—today I make my bow to the world. To prepare I rehearsed all the follies I could imagine before settling into society. This morning I examined myself—stays, shoes, curls, dress, ornaments—alone, with Griffith as spectator. I discovered a victorious air likely to carry all before it. Know thyself! I must tell you my portrait: formerly disguised as a Carmelite, now a frivolous society girl, I am one of the greatest beauties—Provence excepted.

I confess weak points. After admiring my mother’s exquisite arms, I regret my angular elbows. There is consolation in a fine wrist and well-modeled hollows; shoulders are bony but the contours are bold and delicate, with bright health and blue blood showing under transparent skin. I have the foot of a gazelle, finely turned joints, features of Greek correctness; a tropical blonde, more apt to strike than to blush. The hairdresser wanted to set my hair in medieval bands with a pearl chain, but I found that aged. My nose is imperious and mocking but delicately cut; ears dainty; the neck long and dignified; the mouth a little large but expressive; teeth pretty. There is harmony—gait and voice recall my grandmother; a mood can make my eyes melt or harden. I can laugh or adopt a Madonna face; versatility in coquetry is an advantage.

My grandmother’s manner and voice live in me; my father’s subtlety shows in my character. My mother is imposing and grave; she cannot readily heal wounds. We are unlike and thus not rivals, except perhaps in our extremities, which resemble. I feel as if I had left the convent for the first time—the idea of society thrills me. I still belong only to myself, a flower unseen.

Regret for the past and anxiety about the future fleetingly touched me as I paced the drawing-room and saw the convent garments. Yet strange, fantastic visions arose which I suppressed. I have a regular trousseau laid away—ribbons, shoes, gloves, toilette things—perfumed in cedar drawers. My father gave me gewgaws a girl loves: dressing-case, toilet set, scent-box, fan, sunshade, prayer-book, gold chain, cashmere shawl; riding lessons and dancing will follow. Tomorrow evening I come out in white muslin with a garland of white roses in Greek style—I mean to play the simpleton and win the women’s support. My mother thinks me almost mindless; my brother affects profound contempt.

Alphonse is handsome but melancholy and jealous of his father, with no post at Court. In a household where father and mother are absorbed in society, a young convent-bred girl can quickly learn domestic details they miss. There is a mystery in how Paris keeps people out from nine to two or three in the morning and drains them of intimacy; I must explore it.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
IV. THE SAME TO THE SAME December 15th.

Yesterday at two I went to drive in the Champs-Élysées and the Bois de Boulogne. It was one of those autumn days we used to find so beautiful on the Loire. So I have seen Paris at last! The Place Louis XV is certainly fine, but its beauty is that of man's handiwork.

I was dressed to perfection, pensive, with set face (though inwardly much tempted to laugh), under a lovely hat, my arms crossed. Would you believe it? Not a single smile was thrown at me, not one youth was struck motionless as I passed, not a soul turned to look again; nevertheless the carriage proceeded with a deliberation worthy of my pose.

No, I am wrong—there was one—a duke, and a charming man—who suddenly reined in as we went by. The individual who thus saved appearances for me was my father, and he declared himself highly gratified. I met my mother also, who sent me a butterfly kiss from her fingertips. The worthy Griffith, who fears no man, cast her glances hither and thither without discrimination. In my judgment, a young woman should always know exactly what her eye is resting on.

I was mad with rage. One man actually inspected my carriage without noticing me. This flattering homage probably came from a carriage-maker. I have been quite out in the reckoning of my forces. Plainly, beauty, that rare gift from heaven, is commoner in Paris than I thought. I saw hats doffed with deference to simpering fools; a purple face called forth murmurs of "It is she!" My mother received an immense amount of admiration. There is an answer to this problem, and I mean to find it.

The men, my dear, seemed generally very ugly. The few exceptions are bad copies of us. Heaven knows what evil genius has inspired their costume; it is amazingly inelegant compared with former generations. It has no distinction, color, or romance; it appeals neither to sense nor mind, and must be very uncomfortable. It is meagre and stunted. The hat, above all, struck me; it is a sort of truncated column, and does not adapt itself to the shape of the head; but I am told it is easier to bring about a revolution than to invent a graceful hat. Courage in Paris recoils before the thought of appearing in a round felt; and for lack of one day's daring, men stick all their lives to this ridiculous headpiece. And yet Frenchmen are said to be fickle!

The men are hideous anyway, whatever they put on their heads. I have seen nothing but worn, hard faces, with no calm or peace in the expression; the harsh lines and furrows speak of foiled ambition and smarting vanity. A fine forehead is rarely seen.

"And these are the product of Paris!" I said to Miss Griffith.

"Most cultivated and pleasant men," she replied.

I was silent. The heart of a spinster of thirty-six is a well of tolerance.

In the evening I went to the ball, where I kept close to my mother's side. She gave me her arm with a devotion which did not miss its reward. All the honors were for her; I was made the pretext for charming compliments. She was clever enough to find me fools for my partners, who one and all expatiated on the heat and the beauty of the ball, till you might suppose I was freezing and blind. Not one failed to enlarge on the strange, unheard-of, extraordinary fact—that he saw me for the first time.

My dress, which had dazzled me as I paraded alone in my white-and-gold drawing-room, was barely noticeable amidst the gorgeous finery of most of the married women. Each had her band of faithful followers, and they all watched each other askance. A few were radiant in triumphant beauty, and amongst these was my mother. A girl at a ball is a mere dancing-machine—a thing of no consequence whatever.

The men, with rare exceptions, did not impress me more favorably there than at the Champs-Élysées. They have a used-up look; their features are meaningless, or rather they all have the same expression. The proud, stalwart bearing we find in the portraits of our ancestors—men who joined moral to physical vigor—has disappeared. Yet in this gathering there was one man of remarkable ability, who stood out by the beauty of his face. But even he did not rouse in me the feeling I should have expected. He is a man of no family whose works I do not know. Whatever the genius and merits of a plebeian, he could never stir my blood. Besides, this man was obviously so much more taken up with himself than with anybody else, that I could not but think these great brain-workers must look on us as things rather than persons. When men of intellectual power love, they ought to give up writing; otherwise their love is not the real thing. The lady of their heart does not come first in all their thoughts. I seemed to read this in his bearing. They say he is a professor, orator, and author, whose ambition makes him the slave of every bigwig.

My mind was made up on the spot. It was unworthy of me, I determined, to quarrel with society for not being impressed by my merits, and I gave myself up to the simple pleasure of dancing, which I thoroughly enjoyed. I heard a great deal of inept gossip about people of whom I know nothing; perhaps my ignorance prevents me from appreciating it, since most men and women took a lively pleasure in certain remarks, whether from their own lips or those of others. Society bristles with enigmas and is a perfect maze of intrigue. Yet I am fairly quick of sight and hearing, and as to my wits, Mlle. de Maucombe does not need to be told!

I returned home tired with a pleasant sort of fatigue, and in all innocence began describing my sensations to my mother, who was with me. She checked me with the warning that I must never say such things to anyone but her.

"My dear child," she added, "it needs as much tact to know when to be silent as when to speak."

This advice brought home to me the nature of the sensations which ought to be concealed from everyone, not excepting perhaps even a mother. At a glance I measured the vast field of feminine duplicity. I can assure you, sweetheart, that we, in our unabashed simplicity, would pass for two very wide-awake little scandal-mongers. What lessons may be conveyed in a finger on the lips, in a word, a look! All in a moment I was seized with excessive shyness. What! may I never again speak of the natural pleasure I feel in the exercise of dancing? "How then," I said to myself, "about the deeper feelings?"

I went to bed sorrowful, and I still suffer from the shock of this first collision of my frank, joyous nature with the harsh laws of society. Already the highway hedges are flecked with my white wool! Farewell, beloved.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
How deeply your letter moved me; above all, when I compare our widely different destinies! How brilliant is the world you are entering, how peaceful the retreat where I shall end my modest career!

In the Castle of Maucombe, which you know well by description, I found my room much as I left it; only now I can enjoy the splendid view it gives of the Gemenos valley, which my childish eyes once saw without comprehending. A fortnight after my arrival, my father and mother took me, with my two brothers, to dine with a neighbor, M. de l'Estorade, an elderly gentleman of good family who, after the provincial fashion, has grown rich by scraping and paring.

M. de l'Estorade was unable to save his only son from Bonaparte; after evading conscription he had to send him to the army in 1813 to join the Emperor's bodyguard. After Leipzig no more was heard of him. M. de Montriveau, interviewed by the father in 1814, said he had seen him taken by the Russians. Mme. de l'Estorade died of grief while a vain search was made in Russia. The Baron is a very pious man who practises that hopeful theology we used to cultivate at Blois—Hope! Hope made him see his son in dreams. He hoarded his income and guarded carefully the portion inherited from the late Mme. de l'Estorade; no one ventured to ridicule him.

At last it dawned upon me that the unexpected return of this son was the cause of my own. Who could have imagined, while fancy led us a giddy dance, that my destined husband was slowly traveling on foot through Russia, Poland, and Germany? His bad luck left him at Berlin, where the French Minister helped him return home. M. de l'Estorade, the father, who is a small landed proprietor in Provence with an income of about ten thousand livres, has not sufficient fame to interest the world in the wandering Chevalier de l'Estorade, whose name now recalls his adventures.

The accumulated income of twelve thousand livres from Mme. de l'Estorade's property, plus the father's savings, provides the poor guard of honor with about two hundred and fifty thousand livres, not counting house and lands—quite a considerable fortune in Provence. His worthy father had just bought, on the eve of the Chevalier's return, a fine but badly managed estate where he intends to plant ten thousand mulberry trees, raised in his nursery for this acquisition. Having found his long-lost son, the Baron now thinks only of marrying him well.

My parents agreed with their neighbor's design as soon as they discovered that Renée de Maucombe would be acceptable without a dowry, and that the money I should inherit from my parents would be acknowledged as mine in the contract. In a similar way, my younger brother Jean, on coming of age, signed a document acknowledging an advance upon the estate equal to one-third of the whole. This device is how the nobles of Provence elude the Civil Code of M. de Bonaparte—a code which will drive as many girls of good family into convents as it will find husbands for. From what I have learned, the French nobility seem divided on these matters.

The dinner, darling, was the first meeting between your sweetheart and the exile. The Comte de Maucombe's servants donned their old laced liveries and hats, the coachman his great top-boots; we sat five in the antiquated carriage, and arrived in state about two o'clock—the dinner was for three—at the grange, which is the dwelling of the Baron de l'Estorade.

My future father-in-law has, you see, no castle, only a simple country house beneath one of our hills at the entrance of the noble valley, whose pride is the Castle of Maucombe. The building is unpretentious: four pebble walls washed yellow and roofed with hollow red tiles. The rafters bend under the weight of this roof. The windows are inserted without symmetry, and have enormous yellow shutters. The garden is a Provencal one, enclosed by low walls of big round pebbles, set according to the mason's taste. The mud in which they are set is falling away in places.

Thanks to an iron railing at the entrance facing the road, this simple farm has a certain air of being a country-seat. The railing, long sought with tears, is so emaciated that it recalled Sister Angélique to me. A flight of stone steps leads to a door protected by a pent-house roof such as no peasant on the Loire would tolerate for his coquettish white-stone house with its blue roof. The garden and walks are horribly dusty, the trees seem burned. It is easy to see that for years the Baron's life has been a mere rising and going to bed again, day after day, without thought beyond piling up coppers. He eats the same food as his two servants, a Provencal lad and the old woman who used to wait on his wife. The rooms are scantily furnished.

Nevertheless, the house of l'Estorade had done its best; the cupboards had been ransacked, and its last man beaten up for the dinner, which was served on old silver dishes, blackened and battered. The exile, my darling, is like the railing, emaciated! He is pale and silent, bearing traces of suffering. At thirty-seven he might be fifty. The once-ebon locks of youth are streaked with white like a lark's wing. His fine blue eyes are cavernous; he is a little deaf, reminding one of the Knight of the Sorrowful Countenance.

Despite all this, I have graciously consented to become Mme. de l'Estorade and to receive a dowry of two hundred and fifty thousand livres, but only on the express condition of being allowed to work my will upon the grange and make a park there. I have demanded from my father, in set terms, a grant of water, which can be brought from Maucombe. In a month I shall be Mme. de l'Estorade; for, dear, I have made a good impression. After the snows of Siberia a man is ready enough to see merit in those black eyes which, according to you, used to ripen fruit with a look. Louis de l'Estorade seems well content to marry the fair Renée de Maucombe—such is your friend's splendid title.

Whilst you prepare to reap the joys of the many-sided existence that awaits a Chaulieu lady and to queen it in Paris, your poor little sweetheart, Renée, that child of the desert, has fallen from the empyrean where we once soared into the vulgar realities of a life as homely as a daisy's. I have vowed to comfort this young man, who never knew youth but passed straight from his mother's arms to war, and from the joys of his country home to the frosts and forced labor of Siberia.

Humble country pleasures will enliven the monotony of my future. It shall be my ambition to enlarge the oasis around my house and to give it the lordly shade of fine trees. My turf, though Provencal, shall be always green. I shall carry my park up the hillside and plant on the highest point some pretty kiosk, whence, perhaps, my eyes may catch the shimmer of the Mediterranean. Orange and lemon trees, and all choicest things that grow, shall embellish my retreat; and there will I be a mother among my children. The poetry of Nature, which nothing can destroy, shall hedge us round; and standing loyally at the post of duty, we need fear no danger. My religious feelings are shared by my father-in-law and by the Chevalier.

Ah! darling, my life unrolls before my eyes like one of the great highways of France, level and easy, shaded with evergreen trees. This century will not see another Bonaparte; and my children, if I have any, will not be rent from me. They will be mine to train and make men of—the joy of my life. If you also are true to your destiny, you who ought to find your mate among the great, the children of your Renée will not lack a zealous protectress.

Farewell, then, for me at least, to the romances and thrilling adventures in which we used to play heroines. The whole story of my life lies before me now; its great crises will be the teething and nourishment of the young Masters de l'Estorade, and the mischief they do to my shrubs and me. To embroider their caps, to be loved and admired by a sickly man at the mouth of the Gemenos valley—there are my pleasures. Perhaps some day the country dame may spend a winter in Marseilles; but danger does not haunt the purlieus of a narrow provincial stage. There will be nothing to fear, not even admiration that could make a woman proud. We shall take an interest in the silkworms for whose benefit our mulberry leaves will be sold! We shall know the strange vicissitudes of life in Provence and the storms that may attack even a peaceful household. Quarrels will be impossible, for M. de l'Estorade has formally announced that he will leave the reins in his wife's hands; and as I shall do nothing to remind him of this wise resolve, he is likely to persevere in it.

You, my dear Louise, will supply the romance of my life. So you must narrate to me in full all your adventures, describe your balls and parties, tell me what you wear, what flowers crown your lovely golden locks, and what are the words and manners of the men you meet. Your other self will be always there—listening, dancing, feeling her fingertips pressed—with you. If only I could have some fun in Paris now and then, while you played the house-mother at La Crampade! such is the name of our grange. Poor M. de l'Estorade, who fancies he is marrying one woman! Will he find out there are two?

I am writing nonsense now, and as henceforth I can only be foolish by proxy, I had better stop. One kiss, then, on each cheek—my lips are still virginal; he has only dared to take my hand. Oh! our deference and propriety are quite disquieting, I assure you. There, I am off again.... Good-bye, dear.

P.S.—I have just opened your third letter. My dear, I have about one thousand livres to dispose of; spend them for me on pretty things such as we can't find here, nor even at Marseilles. While speeding on your own business, give a thought to the recluse of La Crampade. Remember that on neither side have the heads of the family any people of taste in Paris to make their purchases. I shall reply to your letter later.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
VI. DON FELIPE HENAREZ TO DON FERNAND PARIS, September.

The address of this letter, brother, shows that the head of your house is beyond danger. The massacre of our ancestors in the Court of Lions made Spaniards and Christians of us against our will, but left us a legacy of Arab cunning; perhaps my safety is owed to the Abencerrages' blood still in my veins.

Ferdinand's acting frightened Valdez into believing him; without that, the poor Admiral would have been done for. Nothing will teach the Liberals what a king is. This Bourbon has long been known to me; the more he assured me of protection, the more suspicious I grew. A true Spaniard does not repeat a promise; a flood of words is a sign of duplicity.

Valdez took ship on an English vessel. As soon as I saw the cause of my beloved Spain wrecked in Andalusia, I had written to my steward in Sardinia to prepare my escape. Coral fishers waited at a point on the coast; when Ferdinand urged the French to seize me, I was already in my barony of Macumer, among brigands who defy law and avengers.

The last Hispano‑Moorish family of Granada has found again the shelter of an African desert and even a Saracen horse, on an estate that comes to it from Saracens. How their eyes—men who yesterday feared my authority—sparkled with savage joy and pride when they found they were protecting against the King of Spain the Duc de Soria, their master and a Henarez—the first to visit them since the island belonged to the Moors. More than a score of rifles were ready to point at Ferdinand of Bourbon, a line of kings unknown when the Abencerrages arrived as conquerors on the Loire's banks.

I had intended to live off these large estates, sadly neglected; but my stay showed me Queverdo's reports were too true. The steward had twenty‑two lives at my disposal and not a single real; prairies of twenty thousand acres and no house; virgin forests and no furniture. A million piastres and a resident master for half a century would be needed to make them pay. I must see to this.

The conquered have time during flight to ponder their case and that of their vanquished party. Seeing my noble country, a corpse for monks to prey on, I wept; I read there the presage of Spain's gloomy future.

At Marseilles I heard of Riego's end. It struck me deeply that my life would henceforth be a protracted, unnoticed martyrdom. Is existence worthy when a man can neither die for his country nor live for a woman? To love and to conquer were the law graven on our sabres, whispered by the water rising and falling in our marble fountains. But now the sabre is broken, the palace in ashes, the living spring sucked up by barren sand.

Here, then, is my last will.

Don Fernand, you will now understand why I checked your ardor and bade you remain faithful to the rey netto. As brother and friend I implore you; as master I command. Go to the King and ask him to grant you my dignities, property, office, and titles. He may hesitate and frown; say you are loved by Marie Heredia and that Marie can marry none but a Duc de Soria. That will charm him. Marie Heredia's immense fortune alone stood between him and my ruin; your proposal will seem to deprive me of a last resource, and he will gladly hand you my spoils.

Then marry Marie. The secret mutual love you two pretended against was no secret to me; I arranged the old Count to see you take my place. Marie and I were merely fulfilling our positions and our fathers' wishes; everything else favors you. You are beautiful as a child of love and possess Marie's heart. I am an ill‑favored grandee she dislikes. Any reluctance because of my misfortunes you will soon overcome.

Duc de Soria, your predecessor will neither cost you a regret nor rob you of a maravedi. I keep my mother's diamonds to make myself independent; the gap they cause in the estate can be filled by Marie's jewels. Send those by my nurse Urraca—the only servant I wish to retain; no one prepares my chocolate as she does.

During our brief revolution my unremitting toil was reduced to basics, and my salary sufficed. You will find the income of the last two years with your steward. This sum is mine, but a Duc de Soria cannot marry without expense; we will divide it. You will not refuse this wedding‑present from your brigand brother.

The barony of Macumer, not being Spanish territory, remains mine. Thus I have still a country and a name, should I wish to reenter the world.

Thank Heaven, this finishes our business—the house of Soria is saved!

At the moment I drop into simple Baron de Macumer, the French cannon announce the Duc d'Angouleme's arrival. You will understand why I break off.

October.

When I reached here I had not ten doubloons. A poor leader who, amid calamities he could not avert, fattened himself would be vile. For the vanquished Moor there remains a horse and the desert; for the foiled Christian, the cloister and a few gold pieces.

But my present resignation is mere weariness. I am not yet near the monastery nor have abandoned all thoughts of life. Ozalga gave me letters of introduction for emergencies, among them to a bookseller who with our fellow‑countrymen takes Galignani's place with the English in Paris. He found me eight pupils at three francs a lesson. I go to my pupils every other day, so I have four lessons daily and earn twelve francs—more than I require. When Urraca comes, I shall make some Spanish exile happy by passing on my connection.

I lodge in Rue Hillerin‑Bertin with a poor widow who takes boarders. My room faces south to a little garden. It is quiet; I have green trees and spend one piastre a day. I marvel at the calm, pure pleasure of this life, like Dionysius at Corinth. From sunrise until ten I smoke and take chocolate at my window, contemplating two Spanish plants, a broom rising from jessamine—gold on white—colors that thrill any scion of the Moors. At ten I go to lessons until four, return for dinner, then read and smoke until bed.

I can long endure such a life of work and meditation, solitude and society. Be happy, Fernand; my abdication brings no regrets; I have no Charles V. longings or Napoleon's impulse to try again. Five days and nights since I wrote my will might have been five centuries. Honor, titles, wealth are as though they had never existed.

Now that the conventional barrier of respect around me has fallen, I can open my heart. Though cased in gravity, it is full of tenderness and devotion, which found no object and which no woman divined—even she destined from the cradle to be my bride. In this lies the secret of my political enthusiasm: Spain took the place of a mistress and received my homage. And now Spain is gone! Beggared of all, I can gaze upon my ruin and ponder my being.

Why did life animate this carcass, and when will it depart? Why has that chivalrous race breathed its tropical love and fiery poetry into this last offshoot, if the seed was never to burst, no stem to spring, no radiant flower to scatter Eastern perfumes? Of what crime am I guilty before birth that I can inspire no love? Did fate from infancy doom me to be a stranded hulk on some barren shore? I find in my soul the image of deserts where my fathers ranged, a scorching sun that shrivels life. Proud remnant of a fallen race, vain force, love run to waste—an old man in youth—here better than elsewhere shall I await death's last grace. Under this murky sky no spark will kindle these ashes again. Thus my last words might be "My God, Thou hast forsaken me!"—a cry of agony no mortal has ventured to explore.

You can now see what joy it is for me to live afresh in you and Marie. I shall watch you with the pride of a creator satisfied in his work. Love one another well; any discord would hurt me more than it would you.

Our mother had a presentiment that events would one day serve her wishes; perhaps a mother's longing makes a pact with God. Was she not one of those who can converse with Heaven and bring back visions of the future? How often have I read in her forehead that she coveted for Fernand the honors and wealth of Felipe! When I said so, she would weep and display a heart torn between both sons, yet yielding by an irresistible passion to you alone.

Her spirit will hover joyfully above you as you bow at the altar. My mother, have you not a caress for Felipe now that he has yielded to your favorite the girl you regretfully thrust into his arms? What I have done pleases womankind, the dead, and the King; it is God's will. Make no difficulty, Fernand; obey and be silent.

P.S. Tell Urraca to call me only M. Henarez. Say nothing of me to Marie. You must be the single living soul to know the secrets of the last Christianized Moor, whose veins run with a great family's blood that rose in the desert and is now about to die out in a solitary exile.

Farewell.

VII. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBE

WHAT! Married so soon? Unheard of. After a month you become engaged to a stranger of whom you know nothing—he may be deaf, sickly, tiresome. Don't you see why they want you? To carry on the illustrious l'Estorades stock; you will be buried in the provinces. Were these the promises we made? If I were you I'd sooner set off to Hyères in a caique to be captured by an Algerian corsair and sold to the Grand Turk—then perhaps I'd be a Sultana and make a stir in the harem.

You leave one convent for another. You are a coward and will submit lamblike to family life. But I will direct you: come to Paris. There we'll drive men wild and hold a court like queens. Your husband may be in the Chamber in three years; I know members and will instruct you. You can live in Paris and become, as my mother calls it, a woman of fashion. I won't leave you to your grange!

Monday.

For a fortnight I've lived the social life: Italiens, Grand Opera, and always a ball. Society is witching. The Opera enchants; while my soul is in divine pleasure I am admired and the focus of opera‑glasses. A single glance will make the boldest youth drop his eyes.

I've seen charming young men; yet none have roused the emotion I feel when listening to Garcia in the duet with Pellegrini in Otello. How jealous Rossini must have been! I'm speaking Greek to you, for you never heard Garcia, but you know how jealous I am!

What a wretched dramatist Shakespeare is! Othello is in love with glory; he wins battles and struts while Desdemona sits meekly at home. Such a sheep deserves slaughter. Let any man I deign to love beware how he thinks of anything but loving me!

I like long trials of old chivalry. That lout of a young lord, offended because his sovereign‑lady sent him among lions to fetch her glove, was impertinent and a fool. The lady had some exquisite flower of love in reserve which he lost, deservedly.

But here I run on. My father will represent our master the King at Madrid. I say our master because I shall be part of the embassy; my mother prefers to remain here, and my father will take me as a woman with him.

This seems simple but hides horrors: in a fortnight I have probed the house's secrets. My mother would go if my father took M. de Canalis as secretary, but the King appoints secretaries; the Duke won't oppose the King nor vex my mother, so he leaves the Duchess here. M. de Canalis—our day's great poet—cultivates my mother's society and likely studies diplomacy with her. Diplomacy must be a fine subject, for he is as regular as a Stock Exchange gambler.

The Duc de Rhetore, our elder brother, solemn and whimsical, would be crushed by his father in Madrid, so he remains in Paris. Miss Griffith discovered that Alphonse is in love with a ballet‑girl at the Opera—how fall in love with legs and pirouettes? He comes only when Tullia dances; he applauds and then goes out. Two ballet‑girls in a family are, I fancy, more destructive than the plague. My second brother is with his regiment; I haven't seen him. Thus I act as Antigone of His Majesty's ambassador. Perhaps I will marry in Spain, perhaps a dowryless marriage like yours. My father offered me the use of his Spanish master.

"Spain, the country for castles in the air!" I cried. "Perhaps you hope it means marriages for me!"

He answered with a meaning look and has amused himself teasing me at lunch; he watches and I dissemble. I have played cruelly with him. He asked my opinion of young men and girls; I replied inanely on hair color and figures. He seemed disappointed and blamed himself.

"Still, father," I added, "do not think I say what I really think: mother made me afraid I'd spoken too frankly."

"With your family you can speak freely," my mother replied.

"Very well, then," I continued. "The young men so far seem too self‑centered; they are more taken up with themselves than with company. They put on expressions to talk and drop them, satisfied we don't use our eyes. The man speaking is the lover; silent, he is the husband. The girls are so artificial you cannot know them, except by their dancing; figures and movements alone are not sham. What alarms most is society's brutality. Small incidents at supper give some idea—by small things—of what a popular rising might be. Courtesy is a thin veneer over selfishness. Women count for little—perhaps a survival of Bonapartist ideas."

"Armande is coming on extraordinarily," said my mother.

"Mother, did you think I should never get beyond asking to see Mme. de Stael?"

My father smiled and rose.

Saturday.

One thing I left out: the tidbit. The love we pictured must be extremely hidden; I've seen no trace. A quick glance now and then, but how colorless! Love, as we imagined—wonders, dreams, sorrows, smiles that make sunshine—does not exist. Magick words, constant interchange of happiness, misery of absence, joy at presence—where are they? What soil produces these flowers? Which is wrong, we or the world?

I've seen hundreds of men; not one stirred me. No proof of admiration—even a sword drawn—would move me. Love is the product of rare conditions; one may live a lifetime without meeting the being nature endowed to make one happy. The thought makes one shudder; if found too late, what then?

I've begun to tremble at women's destiny and to understand why many wear a sad face under society's artificial flush. Marriage is chance. Look at yours. A storm of wild thoughts has passed over me. To be loved daily the same yet with difference, to be loved as much after ten years as on the first day—such love needs years. The lover must languish, curiosity be piqued and answered; feeling must be roused and returned.

Is there a law for the inner fruits of the heart as for visible fruits? Can joy be made lasting? How should love mingle tears with pleasures? The convent's cold routine now seems the only real life; the splendor and delight of equal, shared love a mere idle vision.

I see no room in this city for gentle love: shady walks, moonlight on water, the suppliant pleading—all absent. Rich, young, and beautiful, I would make love my life, yet in three months nothing has appealed in the keen, covetous eyes around me. No voice thrills, no glance makes the world brighter.

Music alone filled my soul; sometimes at night I linger by my window, gazing into the garden, summoning the future. Other days I walk the Champs‑Élysées, imagine the man to wake my slumbering soul following me, but meet only mountebanks, gingerbread vendors, jugglers, passersby, or secret lovers. I am tempted to stop and ask, "You who are happy, tell me what is love," but I refrain and return to my carriage, swearing to die an old maid. Love is an incarnation; how many conditions must be met! We are not certain of never quarreling with ourselves, much less with another. This problem God alone can solve.

I may return to the convent. If I remain in society, I shall do things that look like follies, for I cannot reconcile myself to what I see. I am wounded in delicacy, principles, and secret thoughts.

Ah! my mother is happiest, adored by Canalis, her great little man. Sometimes I crave to know what goes on between them. Griffith tells me that women have raged like this: longed to fly at the happy, blacken their characters, tear them to pieces. According to her, virtue is burying savage instincts in the heart. But then what of the heart? It becomes the sink of all that is worst.

It is humiliating that no adorer has appeared for me. I am marriageable, but have brothers and family sensitive about honor. If that keeps men back, they are poltroons.

The part of Chimène in Le Cid delights me. What a marvelous play! Farewell.

VIII. THE SAME TO THE SAME, January.

Our master is a poor refugee, forced into hiding for his part in the revolution the Duc d'Angoulême quelled—a triumph that brought splendid fêtes. Though a Liberal and likely a man of the people, he has awakened my interest: I fancy he was condemned to death. I make him talk to find his secret; but he is of a Castilian taciturnity, proud as Gonsalvo di Córdoba, and yet angelic in patience and gentleness. His pride is not irritable like Miss Griffith's; it is intrinsic, forcing civility by the perfection of his manners and keeping us at a distance by the respect he shows. My father says there is much nobleman in Señor Henarez, whom we call in fun Don Henarez.

A few days ago I addressed him thus. He raised his eyes—usually bent on the ground—and flashed a look that abashed me; he certainly has the most beautiful eyes imaginable. I asked if I had offended him; he replied in grand Spanish, "I am here only to teach you Spanish." I blushed and felt snubbed. I was about to retort, but remembered our dear mother's words and instead said, "It would be a kindness to tell me if you have anything to complain of."

A tremor passed through him; blood rose in his olive cheeks; he answered with emotion: "Religion must have taught you better than I can to respect the unhappy. Had I been a don in Spain and lost everything in Ferdinand VII.'s triumph, your witticism would be unkind; but if I am only a poor teacher of languages, is it not a heartless satire? Neither is worthy of a young lady of rank."

I took his hand and begged pardon in religion's name. He bowed, opened my Don Quixote, and sat down.

This incident disturbed me more than compliments on my successful evening. During the lesson I watched him, safely, since he never looks at me.

From my observations, the tutor we took for forty is under thirty. My governess noted the beauty of his black hair and pearly teeth. His eyes are velvet and fire; otherwise he is plain and insignificant. Though Spaniards are described as uncleanly, he is carefully dressed and his hands whiter than his face. He stoops a little, has an odd, large head; smallpox scars seam his face. His prominent forehead and meeting eyebrows give a repellent harshness. There is a frowning, plaintive look like that of a sickly child kept alive by superhuman care. As my father observed, his features are a shrunken likeness of Cardinal Ximenes. His natural dignity seems to disconcert the dear Duke, who dislikes him and is never at ease with superiority.

When my father knows enough Spanish, we will start for Madrid. When Henarez returned two days after reproving me, I remarked, to show gratitude, "I suppose you left Spain because of political events. If my father is sent there, we might help you, perhaps obtain a pardon if you are condemned."

"It is impossible for anyone to help me," he replied.

"But," I pressed, "is that because you refuse help, or because it is impossible?"

"Both," he said, bowing, in a tone that forbade further questioning.

My father's blood chafed; I was offended by this haughty manner and promptly dropped Señor Henarez.

Yet there was something noble in this refusal. "He would not accept even our friendship," I thought while conjugating a verb. Suddenly I spoke my mind in Spanish. Henarez replied politely that equality of sentiment was necessary between friends, which did not exist here, so it was useless to consider it.

"Do you mean equality of feeling on both sides, or equality in rank?" I persisted to shake him from his gravity.

He raised those awe‑inspiring eyes; mine fell beneath them. He seemed to ask whether my words meant love; the mixture of joy, pride, and agonized doubt in his glance went to my heart. It was clear that advances that would be taken for what they are in France might land me in difficulties with a Spaniard, and I withdrew into my shell, feeling foolish.

When the lesson ended he bowed; his eyes pleaded humbly, "Don't trifle with a poor wretch."

This sudden contrast to his usual grave dignity made a great impression. It may seem horrible to say, but I cannot help believing there are treasures of affection in that man.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
All is over, my child, and it is Mme. de l'Estorade who writes to you. Between us there is no change; it is only a girl the less.

Do not be troubled; I did not give my consent rashly. My life is henceforth mapped out, and the freedom from uncertainty suits me. A great moral power has intervened and swept chance from my fate. We have property to develop, a home to beautify; I shall direct and sweeten a household and try to reconcile a husband to life. In all probability I shall have a family to look after and children to educate.

What would you have? Everyday life cannot be cast in heroic mould. At first sight there may seem no place in this scheme for that longing after the infinite which enlarges the mind and soul. But what prevents me from launching our familiar craft on that boundless sea? And do not suppose the humble duties I take up leave no scope for passion. To restore faith in happiness to one who has been the sport of adverse circumstances is a noble task and may well relieve the monotony of my existence. I see little room for suffering now, and a great deal of good to be done.

I need not hide from you that the love I bear Louis de l'Estorade is not the sort that makes the heart leap at a step or thrill at a glance; yet there is nothing in him that offends me.

You will ask what becomes of that instinct for the great and noble, those energies which linked us and which we still possess. This thought has troubled me. But are these faculties less ours because we keep them concealed, using them in secret for the family's welfare, as instruments to produce the happiness of those entrusted to our care, to whom we owe ourselves without reserve? The time during which a woman may seek admiration is short; it will soon be past. If my life is not a great one, it will be calm, tranquil, and free from shocks.

Nature has favored our sex by giving us a choice between love and motherhood. I have made mine. My children shall be my gods, and this spot of earth my Eldorado.

I can say no more today. Thank you for all you have sent me; look at my wants on the enclosed list. I am resolved to live in an atmosphere of refinement and luxury, taking from provincial life only what adds charm. In solitude a woman is not vulgarized—she remains herself. I count on your kindness to keep me up to the fashion. My father-in-law is so delighted he refuses me nothing; he is turning his house upside down. We are bringing workpeople from Paris and renovating everything.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
X. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE January.

Oh! Renée, you have distressed me for days. That uncommon person—beauty, wit, sensibility, rare powers—threatened with the dullness of an ordinary marriage! How can such treasures perish in tedium? I hate the children you may bear before they exist!

So you know what lies before you—nothing left to hope or fear? Suppose, though, that some glorious morning brings the man destined to rouse you from that sleep… I shudder!

At least you will have a friend. You will play guardian angel to your valley, learning its moods; the slow growth of nature will become part of you; the ever-changing landscape will show you the sameness of the soul. But I shall be there, Renée, a heart unsullied by petty society, all your own.

Monday.

My Spaniard fascinates me—melancholy, calm, severe, mysterious; his solemn silence irritates and intrigues me. He is like a fallen king. Griffith and I puzzle over him as a riddle.

How odd that a language-master should captivate me so! I have considered all sorts of men—nobles, generals, ambassadors—but his coldness provokes me. He wraps himself in pride and mystery; where he hangs back I am bold. It amuses me, this trifling battle. What is a man, a Spaniard, a teacher of languages to me? I make light of any man. If Napoleon had loved me, I think I should have made a slave of him—if he had loved me, he would have felt my whip!

Yesterday I struck at M. Henarez; he made no reply, and his look said he would never return. Perhaps that is for the best; there would be an ominous imitation of Rousseau's Nouvelle Héloïse. Rousseau has left me with distaste for love that moralizes—passion that argues is detestable.

Clarissa flatters itself with its long letters; Richardson paints English women well. Rousseau’s is a sermon in letters. Love, as I conceive it, is purely subjective, a poem. In books about it nothing is both false and true. So, Renée, since you will be mistress of conjugal love, it seems my duty, for both our experiences, to remain unmarried and have a grand passion to enlarge my life.

Tell me every detail of your first days with that strange animal called husband. I promise to do the same if ever I am loved.

Farewell, poor martyred darling.

XI. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU La Crampade.

Your Spaniard makes me shudder. Dismiss him. Your description fits dangerous adventurers who have nothing to lose and will risk everything. He cannot be your husband or your lover. I will write more about married life when my heart is less anxious from your last letter.

XII. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE February.

At nine my father came and sat solemn by the fire. He called me quick-witted and—after half an hour of a remarkable confession—asked a sacrifice. This was the first time I saw his inner thought.

He flattered me, then launched into a political plea: France is in crisis, only the King and a few minds perceive the danger. The Revolution has destroyed family authority, creating weak individuals and empowering masses. We're poised between founding the State on family or on individual interest—between aristocracy and democracy, obedience and free discussion, Catholicism and indifference. He opposes what is called the people for the people's true interest. He fears the decay of art, identity, and strength if families vanish. The country needs wealthy families defending a common treasure; scattered individuals make a bankrupt nation, soon prey to foreign invasion.

He charged me, if I had any sense of service, to use my inheritance to buy an estate for my younger brother, to preserve the family’s rank. He begged me to consider, six months if needed. I amused him by jesting; we laughed together. He reminded me I should not have money until my brother's future was secured; he wanted to use my fortune for him. If I consented to leave him my fortune, he promised to let me live as I pleased so long as it did not conflict with family honor. I yielded with kisses.

Your warning had already made me consider this abyss. I had thought of having Henarez sent away; his looks, disquieting and inspiring dread, made me hesitate. He is ugly and fascinating.

For two days I would have him gone; yet the sensation he causes has become necessary to me. "Once more, and then I will speak," I told myself. His voice is a thrilling music; his manners are simple and unpretending. Once, as he left, he seemed about to kiss my hand, checked himself, and in his embarrassed gesture there was something pathetically sincere. My smile emboldened him—then he looked up; his eyes were wet. I, moved, put my hand out for him to kiss. Perhaps that was to tell him that love might bridge our distance. His lips, moist with tears, burned me; I lay dreaming, happy like a poet. I was ashamed next day at my condescension, but he had fascinated me.

Friday.

He came one morning quietly, and revealed a new world—eloquence, wit, the force of Bossuet—when he explained Spanish and the soul of language. He told me the story of a valet who gave his life for a queen’s glance—what else but death? His look thrilled me.

At a ball I got M. de Vandenesse, speaking with Talleyrand, to ask if he recognized any Henarez among guests at the Duke’s country house in 1809. Vandenesse told me Henarez is the Moorish name of the Soria family, descended from Abencerrages, converted to Christianity. The late Duke and his sons had been with the King; the eldest, the present Duc de Soria, had lost property under Ferdinand. The duke escaped Cádiz before the Duc d'Angoulême's arrival. This made me reflect. I examined Henarez to discern whether he was duke or commoner. He seemed to enjoy frustrating my guesses. Finally, in Spanish, I accused him as Duc de Soria; he answered sadly, "Unhappily, I am not the Duc de Soria," and his eyes dropped.

Talleyrand had said any Henarez must be the late Duc or a lackey. He merely replied, "My father was indeed the servant of the King of Spain." His reserve provoked me. In sport I gave him to translate a letter I had written—an ideal portrait of the lover I desired. He read it in agitation, then begged to keep it in memory of me, saying he had received from his family a sum sufficient and would send some poor Spaniard in his place. He rose with dignity and left to speak with my father.

At dinner my father laughed: "Louise, you have been learning Spanish from an ex-minister and a man condemned to death." I answered, "The Duc de Soria." He repeated, "Duke!" then said the man calls himself Baron de Macumer, from property in Sardinia. Pride kept my eyes on the table.

A fortnight has passed since I last saw the man who loves me. What is he about? If only I were a fly to spy on him! A man exists who would die for me, and he would. He has become necessary—without him I cannot live. You married, and I—am in love! Four months and those two proud doves have fluttered into reality.

Sunday.

At the Italian Opera he found the one spot where he could see me and sat there, not averting his eyes. For love he has genius.

XIII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU La Crampade, February.

My dear Louise—I waited before writing that you might see more clearly. The difference between girl and married woman is vast; girls do not understand it.

I chose to marry Louis de l'Estorade rather than return to the convent; once I saw the convent was the only alternative, I submitted and resolved to make the best of marriage.

Marriage concerns the whole of life; love seeks only pleasure. Marriage persists when passions vanish; it creates interests more precious than the couple’s immediate sensations. Might friendship be the basis for a happy marriage—a friendship blind to many flaws? Between Louis and me there could be such friendship. Having renounced transports of love in marriage, I sought a calm. "If I cannot find love, why not seek happiness?" I thought. I am loved, and will accept the love offered. My married life will be no slavery but a reign.

A pact such as I sought could be made only between Louis and me, hence we hesitated. I asked whether suffering had softened or hardened him; I found him broken by misfortune, fearing our age difference and my superiority. He feared he would not make me happy; his tears moved me.

"Louis," I said, "whether this marriage becomes dear to me rests with you. Can you rise to the unselfishness of friendship and comradeship? Leave me free. I will not hinder you trying to inspire me with love; but I will be yours only as my free gift. If passion comes, I will meet it. Above all, conceal our mutual dependence. A husband submitted to a wife's yoke is ridiculed; a woman's rule must be unobtrusive. If I have made his courage rise, let all seem to spring from him."

He promised solemnly. We were married at the end of the week. Secure in my freedom, I threw myself into the ceremony and was myself. I studied the small things—attentions, management—that contribute more to love than is supposed. Though cold at first, Louis grew bold; the refinement I introduced was mirrored in his person. He regained youth; I had become mistress of a living man. By discreet influence I restored brightness to his gifts.

A secret joy is that I brought him new life before bearing him children. He is attractive now; gratitude and love blend. This conviction fortifies the soul more than passion. I have become the spring of this family life.

I insisted on keeping our domestic influence concealed; to be the cause of his transformation and let the credit appear his is my proudest task. This secret fills my life with interest, known only to God and you.

I am almost happy, but I need a confidante. How reveal my heart to him without wounding his sensitive nature? For three months we lived as before marriage; I worked to give variety and maintain his interest. I aim to have him study for local office; under penalty of losing my esteem, he must stand for deputy. His ardor will show his affection.

Here is an extract from a letter he wrote from Marseilles, expressing intense admiration for me—my beauty, nobility, freedom from pettiness, and his joy at my supposed blessing in making him a father. He vows to take a leading part in public life for my sake.

So much for my pupil! He fancies himself the chosen of my heart, though we have only external attraction to bind us. Still, Louis is lovable—calm, capable of acts many men would boast of. If I do not love him now, I shall at least be good to him.

We have improved the household: English horses, a coupe, better servants' livery. I manage economy and pleasure. I have the family’s influence behind his political ambitions. If we have children, I wish them prosperity and positions. Why should we not, ten years hence, be laughing in Paris sometimes, while I keep my oasis in Provence? Oh, Louise, don't spoil our splendid future! Marry some graybeard in the Chamber of Peers if you must.

XIV. THE DUC DE SORIA TO THE BARON DE MACUMER MADRID.

My dear brother,—I made you Duc de Soria so your actions should match the name. I cannot stand your wandering in discomfort. Neither Marie nor I will marry till we hear you have accepted Urraca’s money. The two millions are fruits of your savings and Marie's. We pray for your happiness. Marie read your letter with tears. I consent, for the family. The King has justified your hopes. Oh! that I might avenge you by showing the King your scorn.

I have taken my own happiness: I have taken Marie. I shall always be beholden to you. A glorious day for us will be when your heart has found its mate. Live for us, and we live for you.

Write safely via the Nuncio at Rome; the French ambassador will forward. Farewell, dear exile.

XV. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE March.

Ah, my love, marriage is making a philosopher of you! Your cold, practical view of married life shocked me. You would convert me to domestic economy with calendars of pleasures? We were too instructed as girls; but philosophy without love is the worst conjugal hypocrisy. Your arithmetic of thinning pleasures to preserve household interest seems vile. I prefer to perish in the violence of the heart rather than live in the arid air of your prudence!

You would act for duty; I act from impulse. Perhaps you have justified your marriage by cleverness; but a husband who discovers his wife’s duplicity will feel less gratitude. Love, to me, is the first principle of all virtue—the Infinite in us, not arithmetic. You may be right, but I mourn the loss of romantic faith.

Monday.

Last night I sat at the window to watch the stars. In the silence I heard a breath and, by starlight, saw my Spaniard perched in an elm, surely admiring my window. At first I trembled, then felt immense joy. He risked the watch—what heroic charm! He slid down and vanished after blowing a letter through a hollow cane.

I burned it unread, thinking I should not be one to read a secret. But I regretted it. At the Opera he took his post where he could see me; he looked sad. Through a secretary at the Spanish embassy I learned, as Duc de Soria, he had renounced a rich heiress for love of a younger son and allowed himself to be despoiled by the King. "He would perform such heroism simply," I said. He holds a Sardinian title, and his house is known; he has a palace at Sassari and feudal rights. If Ferdinand died, perhaps Macumer would enter diplomacy. He is young but distinguished.

I regretted burning his letter—what words could such a man write? He loves me. To be adored in secret by a man connected with great men in Paris, unknown to all! Now I understood Paris, its balls and gaieties: love wants society for its stage.

The Duchess whispered, "Little rogue!" My mother had seen my hidden joy; that two-word scold taught me more worldliness than a year of study.

In March the opera season ends—how will life be without music when my heart is full? I waited for a shower of rain—heroic acts charm us. He sent another letter. This time I did not burn it. Here it is:

"Louise, it is not for your beauty nor mind nor pride I love you, though you combine pride with charity. Louise, I love you because for the sake of a poor exile you unbent loftiness; by a gesture, a glance, you comforted a man beneath you. You are the one woman whose eyes shone tenderer toward me. Because you let fall this glance, I long to tell you how dear you are. Know, then, idol of my heaven, that an offshoot of the Saracen race gives his life to you, will receive your orders as a slave, deem it honor to execute them. I have given myself to you absolutely, for a glance, for a touch you once offered your Spanish master. I am your servitor, Louise; I ask no more. No, I dare not think I could be loved; perhaps I shall win toleration. Since that morning, I find you in the sunshine of my home, in flowers, in sleep. One thought troubles me—your ignorance of this devotion—so I contrive a way you may answer without danger. At the opera, if you wear one red and one white camellia, that will be my answer. Thenceforth, whatever you demand shall be done, so far as a man can, by your happy servant,

"FELIPE HENAREZ."

P.S.—Great lords know how to love! What restrained fire! What loyalty! He puts to flight calculation. He is sublime, touching, childlike and great. Love here is sweep and simplicity.

I writhe like a thread in the fire. To accept or refuse? I must test him.

XVI. THE SAME TO THE SAME March.

I am dressed in white—white camellias in my hair and another in hand. My mother has red camellias; I toy with suspense: will I take one? I long to make him pay by suspense.

At the opera everyone admired me; only one adored. He hung his head when I arrived with a white camellia, then turned pale when I took a red from my mother. My deliberate action was reply enough. The duet of Romeo and Juliet was perfect for two neophytes listening.

At home I counted steps, my heart on fire. Is he devoted as he writes? How be sure he is not merely obeying? I am harrowed by casuistry. How can I command him to write details of his day? He is my slave whom I must busy.

Sunday Morning.

I had Griffith deliver a note to Rue Hillerin-Bertin, asking Baron de Macumer to return a letter of mine he had carried away. Griffith returned with an envelope containing my portrait stained with tears. He had obeyed; the portrait moved me. Another man might have refused. The Saracen fulfilled his promise.

XVII. THE SAME TO THE SAME April 2nd.

Yesterday was splendid; my father lent me the prettiest turnout—two dapple-grays and a barouche—and I drove out like a flower. On the Champs-Élysées I saw him approach on an extraordinarily beautiful horse. He bowed; he slackened to speak when I signaled. I teased him: "Your horse makes you an object of interest." He said his steward in Sardinia sent it—of Arab blood. The Duc d'Angoulême has bought Macumer's horse; my slave understood he should not attract notice with horses. A man must be noticed for his nature, not his trappings. Vanity in a poor exile is excusable; I forgave him his beauty.

He always wears my livery—a red camellia in his buttonhole while I hold a white. Love's light clarifies everything. I know Paris now, transfused with meaning. I could never flirt with a fool; only men of distinction enter our feelings. Forgive me, I forgot our l'Estorade—didn't you mean to make a genius of him? Good-bye; I am a little off my head.

XVIII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE CHAULIEU April.

My angel—or my imp—you have grieved me. You have never realized the force of indissoluble marriage. I do not wish to quarrel with philosophers, but making marriage irrevocable makes each union unique. The laws of conjugal life differ for country and town; character matters. The wife of a leader may resign to his guidance; the wife of a fool must take the rein.

You call prudence vice; reason may produce what we call vice. Passion is vicious when it reasons, noble only when it springs from the heart in sublime impulses that defy selfishness. Sooner or later you too will find dissimulation the necessary armor of a woman—if dissimulation means courage to bear in silence and prudence to foresee. Every married woman learns social rules often at odds with nature. To protect the family from ruin, a wife may be forced to calculation. Would you call such prudence vice?

You will face such calculations when you are the proud and happy wife of a man who adores you; perhaps he will take on the burden himself. When that day comes, we shall meet in Paris and you may say I erred, that M. de l'Estorade is a man of genius.

As for brave love—night watches, trembling idolatry—I knew it was not for me. Enlarge the borders of your brilliant life; mine is hemmed to La Crampade.

You accuse me of jealous care to nurse a modest shoot into lasting happiness. I believed I had found how to adapt a mistress's charm to a wife’s role, and your letters made me blush. Who knows which of us is right? Perhaps both. Society exacts a heavy price for our furbelows, titles, and children.

I, too, have red camellias—but they bloom in smiles for my double charge, father and son, whose slave and mistress I am. Your letters taught me what a woman sacrifices in marrying—one look at wild plateaus of your youth made me weep. Regret is not remorse.

"Marriage has made you a philosopher!" Your words stung. I wept to think of you swept away in love’s torrent. My father made me read Bonald and he showed me the family in its strength; according to him, your father was right.

Farewell, my dear fancy, my friend, my wild other self.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Well, my Renée, you are a love of a woman, and I quite agree now that we can only be virtuous by cheating. Will that satisfy you? Moreover, the man who loves us is our property; we can make a fool or a genius of him as we please—though the former happens more often. You will make yours a genius and won't tell the secret—two heroic actions, if you will!

Ah! if there were no future life, how nicely you would be sold: this is martyrdom you plunge into of your own accord. You want to make him ambitious and keep him in love! Child, the last alone is sufficient.

Tell me, to what point is calculation a virtue, or virtue calculation? You won't say? Well, we won't quarrel—Bonald can decide. We are, and intend to remain, virtuous; nevertheless at this moment I believe you, with all your pretty little knavery, are a better woman than I am.

Yes, I am deceitful. I love Felipe, and I conceal it from him with an odious hypocrisy. I long to see him leap from his tree to the top of the wall, and from the wall to my balcony—and if he did, how I should wither him with my scorn! You see, I am frank enough with you.

What restrains me? What mysterious power prevents me from telling Felipe, dear fellow, how supremely happy he has made me by the outpouring of his love—so pure, so absolute, so boundless, so unobtrusive, and so overflowing?

Mme. de Mirbel is painting my portrait, and I intend to give it to him, my dear. What surprises me more every day is the animation which love puts into life. How full of interest is every hour, every action, every trifle! What amazing confusion between past, future, and present! One lives in three tenses at once. Is it still so after the heights of happiness are reached? Oh, tell me, I implore you, what is happiness? Does it soothe, or does it excite? I am horribly restless; I seem to have lost all my bearings; a force in my heart drags me to him, despite reason and propriety. There is this gain, that I am better able to enter into your feelings.

Felipe's happiness consists in feeling himself mine; the aloofness of his love, his strict obedience, irritate me, just as his profound respect provoked me when he was only my Spanish master. I am tempted to cry out to him as he passes, "Fool, if you love me so much as a picture, what will it be when you know the real me?"

Oh, Renée, you burn my letters, don't you? I will burn yours. If other eyes than ours were to read these thoughts which pass from heart to heart, I should send Felipe to put them out, and perhaps to kill the owners, by way of additional security.

Monday.

Oh, Renée, how is it possible to fathom the heart of man? My father ought to introduce me to M. Bonald, since he is so learned; I would ask him. I envy the privilege of God, who can read the undercurrents of the heart.

Does he still worship? That is the whole question.

If ever, in gesture, glance, or tone, I were to detect the slightest falling off in the respect he used to show me in the days when he was my instructor in Spanish, I feel that I should have strength to put the whole thing from me. "Why these fine words, these grand resolutions?" you will say. Dear, I will tell you.

My fascinating father, who treats me with the devotion of an Italian cavalier servente for his lady, had my portrait painted, as I told you, by Mme. de Mirbel. I had a copy made, good enough for the Duke, and sent the original to Felipe, with these lines:

  "Don Felipe, your single-hearted devotion is met by a blind
  confidence. Time will show whether this is not to treat a man as
  more than human."

It was a big reward. It looked like a promise and—dreadful to say—a challenge; and worse—I intended it to suggest both without actually committing myself. If in his reply there is "Dear Louise!" or even "Louise," he is done for!

Tuesday.

No, he is not done for. The constitutional minister is perfect as a lover. Here is his letter:—

  "Every moment passed away from your sight has been filled by me
  with ideal pictures of you; my eyes closed to the outside world
  and fixed in meditation on your image. Henceforth my gaze will
  rest upon this wondrous ivory—this talisman—since your blue eyes
  sparkle with life as I look, and paint passes into flesh and blood.
  If I have delayed writing, it is because I could not tear myself
  away from your presence, which wrung from me all that I was bound
  to keep most secret.

  "Yes, closeted with you all last night and to-day, I have, for the
  first time in my life, given myself up to full, complete, and
  boundless happiness. Could you but see yourself where I have
  placed you, between the Virgin and God, you might have some idea
  of the agony in which the night has passed. But I would not offend
  you by speaking of it; for one glance from your eyes, robbed of
  the tender sweetness which is my life, would be full of torture
  for me, and I implore your clemency therefore in advance. Queen of
  my life and of my soul, oh! that you could grant me but one-
  thousandth part of the love I bear you!

  "This was the burden of my prayer; doubt worked havoc in my soul
  as I oscillated between belief and despair, between life and
  death, darkness and light. A criminal whose verdict hangs in the
