thousand, rare in any age, head and shoulders above the rest in
everything. The patient resignation of my Felipe was angelic. The sight
of his brother and Marie gave him a moment's pleasure and easing of
pain.

“Darling,” he said to me with his simple frankness, “I had almost gone
from life without leaving to Fernand the Barony of Macumer; I must make
a new will. My brother will forgive me; he knows what it is to love!”

I owe my life to my brother-in-law and his wife; they want to carry me
off to Spain!

Ah! Renée, to no one but you can I speak freely of my grief. A sense of
my faults weighs me to the ground, and there is bitter solace in
pouring them out to you, poor, unheeded Cassandra. The exactions, the
preposterous jealousy, the nagging unrest of my passion wore him to
death. My love was dangerous because we had both exquisitely sensitive
natures; we spoke the same language, nothing was lost on him, and often
the mocking shaft, so carelessly discharged, went straight to his heart.
You can have no idea of the point to which he carried submissiveness.
I had only to tell him to go and leave me alone, and the caprice,
however wounding, would be obeyed without a murmur. His last breath
was spent in blessing me and repeating that a single morning alone with
me was more precious to him than a lifetime with another woman, even
were she the Marie of his youth. My tears fall as I write.

This is my life now. I rise at midday and go to bed at seven; I linger
absurdly long over meals; I saunter slowly, standing motionless an hour
before a single plant; I gaze into the leafy trees; I take a sober
interest in mere nothings; I long for shade, silence, and night; in a
word, I fight through each hour and take a gloomy pleasure in adding it
to the heap of the vanquished. My peaceful park gives the company I
care for; everything there is full of glorious images of my vanished
joy, invisible to others but eloquent to me.

“I cannot away with you Spaniards!” I exclaimed one morning, as my
sister-in-law flung herself on my neck. “You have some nobility we lack.”

If I still live, it is doubtless because Heaven tempers affliction to
the strength of those who must bear it. Only a woman can know what it is
to lose a love springing from the heart and genuine, a passion satisfying
spirit and flesh. How rare to find a man so gifted that to worship him
brings no degradation! If such supreme fortune befall us once, we cannot
hope for it again. Men of true greatness, whose strength and worth are
veiled by poetic grace and who charm by high spiritual power, men made
to be adored—beware of love! Love will ruin you, and ruin the woman of
your heart. This is my cry as I pace my woodland walks.

And he has left me no child! That love so rich in smiles, which rained
perpetual flowers and joy, has left no fruit. I am a thing accursed.
Can it be that, even as polar ice and torrid sand alike repel life, so
the purity and vehemence of a single-hearted passion render it barren
as hate? Is it only a marriage of reason, such as yours, which is
blessed with a family? Can Heaven be jealous of our passions? These are
wild words.

You are, I believe, the one friend whose company I could endure. Come
to me, then; none but Renée should be with Louise in her sombre garb.
What a day when I first put on my widow’s bonnet! When I saw myself in
black I fell back and wept till night; and I weep again as I recall that
moment.

Good-bye. Writing tires me; thoughts crowd fast, but I have no heart to
put them into words. Bring your children; you can nurse baby here without
making me jealous; all that is gone, he is not here, and I shall be
glad to see my godson. Felipe used to wish for a child like little
Armand. Come, then, come and help me bear my woe.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By the time you read this I shall already be near; I leave a few minutes after sending it. We shall be alone together. Louis must remain in Provence; with elections near, Liberals plot against his re‑election.

I do not come to comfort you; I bring my heart to beat with yours and help you bear life. I bid you weep — only tears can win the joy of meeting him again. He travels toward Heaven; each step and duty brings you nearer and breaks the chain that parts you.

Louise, in my arms you'll once more lift your head and go to him—pure, noble, cleansed of baseless errors, bearing the harvest of good deeds you'll do here in his name.

I scribble these hasty lines amid preparations, interrupted by the babies and Armand, who keeps calling, "Godmother! I want to see her," making me almost jealous. He might be your child!

SECOND PART
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Yes, Renée, it is true; you have been told. I have sold my house, Chantepleurs, and the farms in Seine-et-Marne—but no more; I am neither mad nor ruined.

Let us be practical. When all was wound up, I had left of poor Macumer's fortune about twelve hundred thousand francs. I will account, as to a practical sister, for every franc.

I put a million into the Three per Cents when they were at fifty; I now receive sixty thousand francs a year instead of the thirty thousand the property yielded. Consider my former life: six months a year in the country renewing leases, listening to farmers' complaints who paid when they pleased, bored like a sportsman in wet weather; produce to sell, always at a loss. In Paris my house represented a rental of ten thousand francs; I had to invest money at the notaries, wait for interest, sue for repayments, and study mortgage law. In short, business in Nivernais, Seine-et-Marne, and Paris—a burden and a vexing, losing game for a widow of twenty-seven!

Now my fortune is secured on the Budget. Instead of paying taxes to the State, I receive, every half-year, thirty thousand francs in thirty notes, handed over at the Treasury by a dapper clerk who smiles when he sees me.

Supposing the nation went bankrupt? Well: 'Tis not mine to see trouble so far from my door. At worst the nation would cut me at most in half; I should still be as well off as before, and I shall draw a double income until any catastrophe arrives. A nation becomes bankrupt at most once a century; I shall have time to save.

And finally, is not the Comte de l'Estorade a peer of this July semi-republic? Is he not one of those pillars of royalty offered by the "people" to the King? How can I fear a friend at Court, a great financier, head of the Audit Department? I defy you to arraign my sanity! I am almost as good at sums as your citizen king.

Do you know what inspires a woman with all this arithmetic? Love, my dear!

Alas, the time has come to unfold the motives of my conduct, which baffled even your insight. I am to be married in a country village near Paris. I love and am loved. I love as much as a woman who knows love well can love, and I am loved as a woman ought to be by the man she adores.

Forgive me, Renée, for keeping this secret from you and everyone. If your friend eludes all spies and misleads curiosity, you must admit that my feeling for poor Macumer justified some dissimulation. Besides, de l'Estorade and you would have deafened me with remonstrances and plagued me with misgivings, and the facts might have seemed to support them. You know to what pitch my jealousy can go; all that would only have been useless torture. I was determined to carry out, on my own responsibility, what you will call my insane project, and to take counsel only with my head and heart—like a schoolgirl slipping away from watchful parents.

The man I love has nothing but thirty thousand francs' worth of debts, which I have paid. What a theme for comment here! You would have tried to make Gaston an adventurer; your husband would have set detectives on the dear boy. I preferred to sift him myself. He has been wooing me close on two years. I am twenty-seven, he is twenty-three—the difference, I admit, is huge on the wrong side. Another source of lamentation!

Lastly, he is a poet, who has lived by his trade—that is, on next to nothing. Being a poet, he has spent more time weaving day-dreams and basking in the sun than scribing in a garret. Practical people tar authors, artists, and all men who live by their brains with the same brush of inconstancy. Their nimble wit lays them open to the charge of equal agility in matters of the heart.

In spite of the debts, the age, and the poetry, an end is near to a heroic resistance of more than nine months, during which he has not been allowed even to kiss my hand; so ends the season of our sweet, pure love-making. This is not the surrender of a raw, curious girl as eight years ago; the gift is deliberate, and my lover awaits it with such loyal patience that, if I pleased, I could postpone the marriage for a year. There is no servility in this; love's slave he may be, but the heart is not slavish. Never have I seen a man of nobler feeling, or one whose tenderness was so rich in fancy and stamped with the impress of his soul. The art of love is his by heritage. A few words will tell his story.

My friend is called Marie Gaston. He is the illegitimate son of the beautiful Lady Brandon, whose sad fate you may know—she died broken-hearted, a victim to Lady Dudley's vengeance—a ghastly story of which the dear boy knows nothing. Marie Gaston was placed by his brother Louis in a boarding-school at Tours, where he remained until 1827. Louis sailed a few days later for foreign parts "to seek his fortune," as an old woman who played Providence to him said. This brother, turned sailor, used to write him fatherly letters and breathed a noble spirit; but a struggling life never allowed him to return. His last letter told Marie that he had been appointed Captain in the navy of some American republic and urged him to hope.

Alas, three years have passed, and my poor poet has heard nothing more. He loved his brother so dearly that he would have gone to look for him but for Daniel d'Arthez, the well-known author, who took a generous interest in Marie Gaston and prevented his mad impulse. Nor was this all; often he gave him a crust and a corner, as the poet puts it.

In truth, the poor lad was in terrible straits; he was innocent enough to believe—astonishing as it seems—that genius was the shortest road to fortune, and from 1828 to 1833 his aim was to make a name in letters. His life was a frightful tissue of toil and hardships, hope and despair. d'Arthez's good advice could not prevail against ambition, and his debts grew like a snowball. Still he was beginning to come into notice when I met him at Mme. d'Espard's. At first sight he inspired me, unconsciously to himself, with the most vivid sympathy. How did it happen that this virgin heart was left for me? He combines genius and shrewdness, passion and pride; women fear greatness without a weak side. How many victories were needed before Josephine could see the great Napoleon in the little Bonaparte she married.

Poor Gaston is innocent enough to think he knows the measure of my love! He simply has not the idea of it, but to you I must make it clear; this letter, Renée, is something like a last will. Weigh well what I say.

At this moment I am confident of being loved as perhaps no other woman is, nor have I a shadow of doubt as to the perfect happiness of our wedded life, to which I bring a feeling hitherto unknown to me. Yes, for the first time I know the delight of being swayed by passion. That which every woman seeks in love will be mine in marriage. As poor Felipe once adored me, so do I now adore Gaston. I have lost control of myself; I tremble before this boy as the Arab hero did before me. In a word, the balance of love is on my side, and this makes me timid. I am full of absurd terrors: afraid of being deserted, of growing old and ugly while Gaston keeps youth, afraid of falling short of his hopes!

And yet I believe I have in me sufficient devotion not only to keep alive the flame of his love in our solitary life, but even to make it burn stronger. If I am mistaken, if this idyl in hiding must end—what am I saying?—if I find Gaston's love one day less intense than the night before, be sure, Renée, I shall visit my failure only on myself; no blame shall attach to him. I tell you now it would mean my death. Not even children could make me live on those terms, for I know myself—I am the lover rather than the mother. Therefore, before taking this vow, I implore you, Renée, if this disaster befall me, to take the place of mother to my children; let them be my legacy to you! All I know of you—your blind attachment to duty, your rare gifts, your love of children, your affection for me—would help to make my death, I dare not say easy, at least less bitter.

This compact I have made with myself adds a vague terror to the solemnity of my marriage. For this reason I wish no one I know present; it will be performed in secret. Let my heart fail me if it will, but I shall not read anxiety in your dear eyes; I alone shall know that this marriage-contract may be my death warrant.

I will not again refer to this agreement between my present self and the self I am to be. I have confided it to you so you might know the full extent of your responsibilities. In marrying I retain full control of my property; and Gaston, while aware that I have enough to secure a comfortable life for both, is ignorant of its amount. Within twenty-four hours I shall dispose of it as I please; and to save him from a humiliating position, I shall have stock bringing him twelve thousand francs a year assigned. He will find this in his desk on the eve of our wedding. If he declined, I should break off the whole thing. I had to threaten rupture to get his permission to pay his debts.

This confession tires me. I shall finish it the day after tomorrow; I must spend tomorrow in the country.

October 20th.

I will tell you now the steps I have taken to ensure secrecy. My object has been to ward off every incitement to my ever-wakeful jealousy, in imitation of the Italian princess who, like a lioness carrying off her prey, retired to some Swiss town to devour it in peace. I confide my plans to you because I beg another favor: respect our solitude and never come uninvited.

Two years ago I bought a small property overlooking the ponds of Ville d'Avray, on the road to Versailles. It consists of twenty acres of meadow, the skirts of a wood, and a fine fruit garden. Below the meadows the land was excavated to make a lakelet of about three acres with a charming little island. The small valley is shut in by two graceful, thickly wooded slopes, where spring-water is led by channels to the park and then into the royal ponds, glimpsed in the distance. My park has been laid out with hedges, walls, and ha-has so that no point of view is lost.

A chalet has been built half-way up the hillside with a charming exposure: woods on either side and a grassy slope down to the lake. Externally it is a copy of the chalets admired by travelers from Sion to Brieg; the interior decorations match the best of the kind.

A hundred paces from this rustic dwelling stands a charming ornamental house communicating with it by a subterranean passage. This contains kitchen and servants' rooms, stables, and coach-houses; of all these brick buildings the facade alone is visible, graceful against shrubbery. Another building lodges the gardeners and masks the entrance to the orchards and kitchen-gardens.

The entrance gate is hidden in the wall dividing the park from the wood; the plantations, already well grown, will in two or three years completely hide the buildings, so that, except in winter, no trace of habitation will appear outside, save smoke on neighboring hills.

The surroundings of my chalet are modeled on the King's Garden at Versailles, with an outlook on my lake and island. The hills everywhere display abundant foliage—those splendid trees for which your new civil list has so well cared. My gardeners are to cultivate sweet-scented flowers only, so our home will be a fragrant emerald. The chalet, roof covered in wild vine, is embedded in climbing plants—hops, clematis, jasmine, azalea. It will be a sharp eye that descry our windows!

The chalet is solid, with heating and all conveniences of modern architecture. It contains a suite for Gaston and another for me. The ground floor has an ante-room, parlor, and dining room; above are three rooms for nurseries. I have five first-rate horses, a small light coupe, and a two-horse cabriolet. We are only a forty-minute drive from Paris; we can go to an opera after dinner and return the same night. The road passes under the shade of our green wall.

My servants—cook, coachman, groom, gardeners, and my maid—are respectable people whom I spent six months assembling; they will be overseen by my old Philippe. Confident of their loyalty, I have cultivated self-interest: wages are small but they will receive an annual New Year's present. They know that the slightest fault or a whisper of gossip may cost them a capital place. Lovers are indulgent to servants and therefore I reckon on my household.

All that was choice in my Rue du Bac house has been moved to the chalet. The Rembrandt hangs on the staircase, the Hobbema faces the Rubens in his study; the Titian Mary sent from Madrid adorns the boudoir. The fine furniture Felipe picked up looks well in the parlor, which the architect decorated tastefully. Everything at the chalet is charmingly simple with a simplicity that costs at least a hundred thousand francs. Our ground-floor rests on cellars of millstone embedded in concrete; it is almost completely buried in flowers and shrubs, deliciously cool without damp. To complete the picture, a fleet of white swans sail over my lake!

Oh, Renée, the silence in this valley would bring joy to the dead! One is awakened by birds or the breeze in the poplars. A little spring discovered while digging the foundations trickles down the hillside over silvery sand to the lake between banks of water-cress at the edge of the woods. I know nothing money can buy to equal it.

May not Gaston come to loathe this too perfect bliss? I shudder at its completeness; the ripest fruits harbor worms, the most gorgeous flowers attract insects. I have learned an unseen jealous power attacks happiness when it reaches perfection. That is the moral of your preaching, and you have been proved a prophet.

When I went the day before yesterday to see whether my last whim had been carried out, tears rose to my eyes; to my architect's surprise I passed his account for payment.

"But, madame," he exclaimed, "your man of business will refuse; it is three hundred thousand francs." My reply was only, "To be paid without question," with the bearing of a seventeenth-century Chaulieu.

"But," I added, "on one condition: no human being must hear from you of the park and buildings. Promise me on your honor not to breathe the proprietor's name."

Now can you understand my sudden journeys, my mysterious comings and goings? Do you know whither those beautiful things supposed to be sold have flown? Do you perceive the motive of my change of investment? Love, my dear, is a vast business, and they who would succeed in it should have no other. Henceforth I shall have no more trouble from money matters; I have taken all thorns out of my life and done my housekeeping with a vengeance, so as never to be troubled with it again, except during the ten minutes daily I shall devote to old Philippe. I have made a study of life and its sharp curves; death gave me harsh lessons. Now I want to turn all to account. My one occupation will be to please him and love him, to brighten with variety what to others is dull monotony.

Gaston is still in ignorance. At my request he, like myself, has taken up quarters at Ville d'Avray; tomorrow we start for the chalet. Our life will cost little; but if I told you the sum I set aside for my toilet, you would cry madness—and with reason. I intend to take as much trouble to make myself beautiful for him every day as other women do for society. My country dress, year in, year out, will cost twenty-four thousand francs, and most of this will not be for day costumes. As for him, he may wear a blouse! Don't suppose I will turn our life into an amorous duel and wear myself out in devices for feeding passion; all I want is a conscience free from reproach. Thirteen years still lie before me as a pretty woman, and I am determined to be loved on the last day of the thirteenth more fondly than on the morrow of our nuptials. This time no cutting words shall mar my grateful content. I will be servant where mistress thrived ill before.

Ah, Renée, if Gaston has sounded, as I have, the heights and depths of love, my happiness is assured! Nature at the chalet wears her fairest face. The woods are charming; each step opens some fresh vista of cool greenery that delights the soul with sweet thoughts. They breathe of love. If only this be not the gorgeous theatre dressed by my hand for my own martyrdom!

In two days I shall be Mme. Gaston. My God! is it fitting a Christian so to love a mortal?

"Well, at least you have the law with you," said my man of business, who is to be a witness; on discovering why my property was realized he exclaimed, "I am losing a client!"

And you, my sweetheart (whom I dare no longer call my loved one), may you not cry, "I am losing a sister?"

My sweet, address in future to Mme. Gaston, Poste Restante, Versailles. We shall send there every day for letters. I don't want to be known to the country people; we shall get provisions from Paris. In this way I hope to guard the secret of our lives. Nobody has been seen here during the years of preparing our retreat; the purchase was made in the troubled period after the revolution of July. The only person who has shown himself here is the architect; he alone is known and will not return.

Farewell. As I write I know not whether my heart is fuller of grief or joy. That proves, does it not, that the pain of losing you equals my love for Gaston?
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
My Dear Daniel,—I need two witnesses for my marriage. Come tomorrow evening with our worthy friend Joseph Bridau. She who will be my wife, with an instinctive knowledge of my wishes, has resolved to live in complete retirement. You, who have often helped me in my poverty, have been kept ignorant of my love because absolute secrecy was necessary.

This explains our long estrangement over the past year. After the wedding we shall be separated for a long time; but you, Daniel, can understand me. Friendship survives absence. There will be moments when I shall long for you, yet I shall not receive you in my own house. She has anticipated even our wishes: she has given up the intimacy of a childhood friend, who had been as a sister. For her sake I must relinquish my comrade.

From this you may infer that ours is no brief infatuation, but absolute, perfect love, grounded in full knowledge and sympathy. It is a perennial source of delight.

Yet nature allows no joy without alloy; in my heart there is one pang I hide from her. You know how often you have come to my aid in desperate poverty; without your encouragement I should have lacked courage to struggle after repeated disappointments. Briefly, my friend, she freed me from that crushing load of debt, of which you were no stranger. She is wealthy; I am penniless. Many a time, in idle fits, I had cried, "Oh for some great heiress to cast her eye on me!" Now that it has happened, the boyish jest and cynical outcast’s bravado have vanished, replaced by a bitter humiliation that neither her considerate tenderness nor my conviction of her nobility can dispel. What better proof of my love could there be than this shame, which I have not flinched from even when I could not overcome it? The fact remains that there are moments when, far from protecting, I am protected.

This is the pain I confide to you.

Except for this, my fondest dreams are realized. She is the fairest and noblest of women; such a bride could raise a man to heights of bliss. Her ways are gentle, seasoned with wit; her love is continuously fresh and charming; her mind is informed and quick; in person she is fair and lovely, with a rounded slimness, as if Raphael and Rubens had conspired to make a woman. I do not think I could have worshiped with such fervor a dark beauty; a brunette seems to me unfinished. She is a widow, childless, twenty-seven, full of life though sometimes dreamily melancholy. These gifts accompany a proud, aristocratic bearing and a fine presence.

She belongs to one of those old families that make rank a fetish, yet loves me enough to disregard my misfortune of birth. Our secret passion has long endured; we have tested each other and found ourselves alike in jealousy; our thoughts respond to the same shock. We both love for the first time; this bewitching spring has filled our days with all the delights fancy can paint. Our path has been strewn with tender imaginings. Each hour brought its own wealth, and when we parted we set our thoughts to verse. Not for a moment did I think to sully such brightness by indulgence of sensual passion, though it might have chafed within. She, a widow and free, understood the homage implied by constant self-restraint, which often moved her to tears. Does not this reveal a noble soul? In mutual fear we avoided even the first kiss.

"We have each a wrong to reproach ourselves with," she said one day.
"Where is yours?" I asked.
"My marriage," she answered.

Daniel, you are a giant among us and love, perhaps, one of the most gifted women of the aristocracy that produced my Armande; what more need I say? Such an answer lays bare a woman's heart and the happiness awaiting your friend, MARIE GASTON.

L. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MME. DE MACUMER

Louise, can it be that, with all your discernment about passions—even within marriage—you plan to live wedded solitude? Having sacrificed your first husband during a fashionable career, would you now flee to the desert to consume a second? You are piling up misery.

But I see you have set about it decisively. The man who has overcome your aversion to a second marriage must possess rare magic of mind and heart; you are left to your illusions. Have you forgotten your former criticism of young men, that each has visited places of shame? Where is the change — in them or in you?

You are fortunate to be able to believe in happiness. I cannot blame you, though affection urges me to dissuade you. Nature and society conspire against absolute happiness: such a condition defies their laws, and perhaps Heaven is jealous. My love for you forebodes some disaster I cannot define; excess of joy may overwhelm you more than any sorrow.

Against him I have no complaint. You love him, and I probably never saw him; but one day, in an idle mood, send me some sketch of this rare, fine creature.

If I seem resigned and cheerful, it is because I am convinced that once the honeymoon is over, you will both fall back into the usual course. Some day, two years hence, strolling along that road, you will exclaim, "There is the chalet that was to be my home forever!" and laugh your dear laugh.

I have said nothing to Louis yet; it would give him too fine an opening for ridicule. I shall tell him simply that you are to be married and desire secrecy. Unfortunately, you will need neither mother nor sister for your bridal evening. It is October; like a brave woman, you are grappling with winter first. Were this not a marriage, one would say you are taking the bull by the horns. In any case you have from me the most discreet and intelligent of friends. That mysterious region called the center of Africa has swallowed many travelers; you seem to launch on an expedition in sentiment analogous to those where explorers perish in sands or at natives’ hands. Your desert is happily only two leagues from Paris, so I can wish you "A safe journey and speedy return."

LI. THE COMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE TO MME. MARIE GASTON 1835.

What has become of you, my dear? After a two-year silence, Renee has reason to worry. So this is love! It scatters to the winds a friendship like ours! Devoted as I am to my children—perhaps more than you are to your Gaston—a mother's love is expansive and does not steal from other affections. I miss your letters and your face. Oh Louise, my heart feeds on conjecture!

I will tell you briefly about us.

In your last letter you had stinging comments about our political position. You mocked our keeping the Audit Department post, which—like the title of Count—Louis owed to Charles X. But how could we, on an income of forty thousand livres, with thirty thousand tied up in entailed property, give Armand, Athenais, and little Rene a proper start except by living on our salary and allowing the estate income to accumulate? In twenty years this will amount to about six hundred thousand francs to provide portions for my daughter and for Rene, whom I plan for the navy. The poor little chap will then have an income of ten thousand livres and perhaps enough cash to bring his portion up to his sister's.

When he is Captain, my beggar may make a wealthy marriage and take a position as good as his elder brother's.

These considerations led us to accept the new order. The new dynasty naturally raised Louis to the Peerage and made him a grand officer of the Legion of Honor. Once the oath was taken, l'Estorade could not be half-hearted; he has since been useful in the Chamber. His present position lets him rest on his oars for life. He is adroit in business, not an orator but speaks pleasantly, which suffices in politics. His shrewdness and administrative knowledge make him indispensable across parties. He was offered an embassy, which I would not permit him to accept. I am tied to Paris by Armand and Athenais's education—now thirteen and nearly eleven—and I will not leave until little Rene finishes his, which has just begun.

We could not return to country life loyal to the elder branch of the dynasty without harming our children's education and prospects. A mother is hardly called on to be a Decius, especially when such types are rare. In fifteen years l'Estorade may retire to La Crampade on a good pension, having secured a place as referendary for Armand in the Audit Department.

As for Rene, the navy may make him a diplomatist. The little rogue, at seven, has all the cunning of an old Cardinal.

Oh, Louise, I am indeed a happy mother. My children are endless joy.

Senza brama sicura ricchezza.

Armand is a day scholar at Henry IV's. I wanted him to have public-school training yet could not bear to part with him, so I compromised as the Duc d'Orleans did before becoming—or so as to become—Louis Philippe. Every morning Lucas, the old servant you remember, takes Armand to school and brings him home at half-past four. At home we have a private tutor who helps him in the evening and calls him in the morning for school hours. Lucas brings him lunch at midday. Thus I dine with him and am with him until he goes to bed.

Armand is the same charming, feeling, unselfish boy you loved; his tutor is pleased with him. I still have Nais and the baby—two restless little creatures—but I remain as childlike as they. I could not give up the sweet caresses of my darlings. I could not live without flying to Armand's bedside or seizing a kiss.

Home education has drawbacks. Society, like nature, is jealous and claims to regulate conduct. Children brought up at home see the world's passions early and learn its subterfuges; the finer distinctions of mature life escape them, and they rely on feeling and passion rather than societal codes. The gay trappings that attract favor blind them to sober virtues. A child of fifteen with the assurance of a man of the world is unnatural; at twenty-five he will be prematurely old, ill-fitted for the deeper study on which solid ability rests. Society is a long comedy; its actors reflect impressions that rarely penetrate below the surface. A mother who will not part from her children must resolve they shall not enter the gay world; she must resist their inclinations and her own, keeping them in the background. Cornelia kept her jewels under lock and key; shall I do less for the children who are my world?

Now that I am thirty, the heat of the day is over; the hardest part of the road lies behind me. In a few years I shall seem old, and the sense of duty fulfilled is a great encouragement. It seems my trio can read my thoughts and shape themselves accordingly. A mysterious bond unites me to these children who never left my side. If they knew the void they fill, they could not be more lavish of solace.

Armand, who was dull and dreamy in his first three school years and caused me anxiety, has taken a sudden start. He seems to have grasped the aim of this preparatory work: sharpening intelligence, cultivating habits of application, and accustoming him to obedience—the fundamental principle of society. A few days ago I had the proud joy of seeing Armand crowned at the interscholastic competition in the Sorbonne; your godson received the first prize for translation, and at the school distribution he gained two first prizes—one for verse and one for an essay. I went white when his name was called and longed to shout, "I am his mother!" Little Nais squeezed my hand till it hurt. Ah, Louise, a day like this outweighs many a dream of love!

His brother's triumphs have spurred little Rene, who now wants to go to school too. Sometimes the three children make such racket I wonder how my head stands it. I am always with them; no one else, not even Mary, is allowed to take care of my children. But motherhood, though taxing, has compensating joys. To see a child leave play and run to hug you from the fullness of its heart—what could be sweeter?

Being constantly with them is the only way to study their characters. It is a mother's duty, not delegable, to decipher tastes, temper, and aptitudes from infancy. Home-bred children gain ease of manner and tact—acquired qualities that may supply for lack of natural ability—while no natural ability can replace this early training. I already recognize this tone in men I meet and trace the hand of a woman in the making of a young man's manners. How could any woman deprive her children of such a possession? See what rewards attend my tasks!

Armand, I am certain, will make an admirable judge—upright public servant, devoted deputy. And Rene? Where will you find a sailor bolder, more adventurous, more astute than he will be? The little rascal has iron will; whatever he wants he contrives to obtain. He will try a thousand circuitous ways to reach his end, and if not successful, will invent a thousand and first. Where Armand quietly resigns and seeks reasons, Rene storms, puzzles, chatters, and at last finds a chink; if a knife's blade can enter, his little carriage rides through in triumph.

And Nais? She is so completely a second self I can hardly see her as separate from my flesh and blood. What a darling! How I love to make a little lady of her, to dress her curls, thinking tenderly with every touch! I must have her happy; I will give her only to the man she loves and who loves her. But when I let her wear little ornaments, a cherry ribbon, or tiny shoes, a sickening thought comes over me: how can one order a girl's destiny? Who can say she will not love a scoundrel or a man indifferent to her? Tears often rise as I watch this lovely creature, this flower in my heart, to be handed to a man who might tear it from the stem. Louise—who in two years has not written three words of herself—you alone have recalled the terrifying possibilities of marriage, so full of anguish for a mother wrapped up in her child. Farewell, for truly you do not deserve my friendship now, and I hardly know how to write. Oh! answer me, dear Louise.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
So, after two years' silence, you wonder why I have not written. My dear Renée, words, images, language fail to express my happiness. That we have strength to bear it sums up all I could say. It costs us no effort, for we are in perfect sympathy. The whole two years have known no discord, no jarring word between us, no difference in our slightest wishes. Not one day has failed to bear its own peculiar fruit; no moment has passed without being enriched by the play of fancy. We are so far from fearing monotony that our only anxiety is lest life be too short to contain all the poetic creations of a love as rich and varied as Nature herself. Of disappointment there is not a trace. We find more pleasure in being together than on the first day, and each hour discloses fresh reasons for our love. Every evening after dinner, as we stroll, we tell each other we must go and see what is doing in Paris, just as one might talk of going to Switzerland.

"Only think," Gaston will say, "such and such a boulevard is being made, the Madeleine is finished. We ought to see it. Let us go tomorrow."

And tomorrow comes; we are in no hurry to rise, we breakfast in our bedroom. Midday is on us and it is too hot; a siesta seems appropriate. Gaston looks at me and gazes as though my face were a picture, losing himself in contemplation, which, as you may suppose, is not one-sided. Tears rise to both our eyes as we think of our love and tremble. I am still the mistress, pretending, that is, to give less than I receive, and I revel in that deception. What can be sweeter to a woman than to see passion held in check by tenderness, and the man who is her master stayed, like a timid suitor, by a word from her within the limits she chooses?

You asked me to describe him; but, Renée, one cannot truly portrait the man we love. How could the heart be excluded? Moreover, civilization often alters the social man until he bears little likeness to the natural man of feeling. The gentleman who assumes the most graceful operatic poses by the fire may be utterly destitute of the intimate charms a woman values; conversely, an ugly, boorish man may possess the genius of love and the perfect mastery of situations that bewilder us more than superficial graces do. Our ideal is a man whose conventional aspect matches his inner nature, who, in the intimacy of wedded love, has an inborn grace neither given nor acquired—the careless innocence of ancient poets which, even in frank undress, veils the soul with modesty. To find this ideal in life haunts every woman's imagination—in Gaston I have found it.

Ah! dear, I did not know what love could be when joined to youth, talent, and beauty. Gaston has no affectations; he moves with instinctive, unstudied grace. When we walk alone in the woods, his arm round my waist, mine on his shoulder, our bodies fitting, heads touching, our step so even and gentle that passers-by at night take the vision for a single figure gliding over the graveled paths, like one of Homer's immortals. A like harmony exists in our desires, thoughts, words. More than once on evenings when a shower has left the leaves glistening and the grass a vivid green, we have ended our walk without a word, listening to the patter and feasting our eyes on the scarlet sunset, flaring on the hilltops or warming the gray of the trunks.

Beyond a doubt our thoughts then rose to Heaven in silent prayer, pleading for our happiness. At times a cry escaped us when some bend in the path opened fresh beauty. What words can tell how sweet and full of meaning is a kiss half-timidly exchanged within the sanctuary of nature—it is as though God had created us to worship in this fashion.

And we return home, each more deeply in love than ever.

A love so passionate between older married people would outrage Parisian society; only in the heart of the woods, like lovers, can we give it full scope.

To particulars: Gaston is of middle height—the height proper to men of purpose. Neither stout nor thin, his figure is admirably made, ample in proportion, and every motion agile; he leaps a ditch with the easy grace of a wild animal. Whatever his attitude, he shows an instinctive sense of balance, rare in thoughtful men. Though dark, he has an extraordinarily fair complexion; his jet-black hair contrasts finely with the pale tones of neck and forehead. He has the tragic head of Louis XIII. His moustache and tuft have been allowed to grow, but I made him shave the whiskers and beard, which were becoming too common. An honorable poverty was his safeguard, leaving him unsoiled by the loose life that ruins many young men. His teeth are magnificent, and he has iron health. His keen blue eyes, full of tenderness for me, can flash like lightning at any rousing thought.

Like all men of powerful mind, he has an admirable temper; its evenness would surprise you, as it did me. I have heard many a woman's tale of home troubles; men's moods, depressions, men who carry the rankling memory of youthful excess, whose veins run poison and whose eyes are never frankly happy—men who cloak suspicion under bad temper and make their women pay for an hour's peace by a morning of annoyance, who take vengeance on us for beauty they no longer have—all these horrors are unknown here. They are the penalty of unequal unions. Oh, my dear, whatever you do, don't marry Athénaïs to an old man!

But his smile—how I feast on it! A smile always present yet fresh through subtle fancy, a speaking smile storing thoughts of love and unspoken gratitude, linking present joys to past. Nothing is allowed to drop out of our common life. The smallest works of nature have become part of our joy. In these delightful woods everything is alive and eloquent of ourselves. An old mossy oak near the woodsman's house reminds us how we sat there, wearied under its shade, while Gaston taught me the story of the mosses at our feet, and, gradually ascending from science to science, we touched the very confines of creation.

There is something so kindred in our minds that they seem like two editions of the same book. You see what a literary tendency I have developed! We both have the habit, or gift, of looking at every subject broadly, taking in all its points of view, and the proof we constantly give ourselves of the singleness of our inward vision is an ever-new pleasure. We have come to see this community of mind as a pledge of love; if it failed, it would mean as much to us as a breach of fidelity in an ordinary home.

My life, full as it is of pleasures, would seem to you nevertheless extremely laborious. To begin with, my dear, you must know that Louise-Armande-Marie de Chaulieu does her own room. I could not bear that a hired menial, some woman or girl from outside, should be initiated—literary phrase—into the secrets of my bedroom. The veriest trifles connected with the worship of my heart partake of its sacred character. This is not jealousy; it is self-respect. Thus my room is done with all the care a young girl in love bestows on her person, and with the precision of an old maid. My dressing-room is no chaos; on the contrary, it is a charming boudoir. My keen eye foresees all contingencies. At whatever hour the lord and master enters, he will find nothing to distress, surprise, or shock him; he is greeted by flowers, scents, and everything that can please.

I rise at dawn, while he still sleeps, and without disturbing him pass into the dressing-room, where, profiting by my mother's experience, I remove the traces of sleep by bathing in cold water. During sleep the skin, being less active, does not perform its functions; it becomes warm and covered with a sticky mist. From a sponge-bath a woman issues ten years younger; perhaps this is the interpretation of the myth of Venus rising from the sea. The cold water restores to me the saucy charm of dawn, and, having combed and scented my hair and made a most fastidious toilette, I glide back, snake-like, so my master finds me, dainty as a spring morning, at his wakening. He is charmed with this freshness, as of a newly opened flower, without having the least idea how it is produced.

The regular toilet of the day is for my maid, and this takes place later in a larger room set aside for the purpose. As you may suppose, there is also a toilette for going to bed. Three times a day, or perhaps four, do I array myself for the delight of my husband; which, again, dear one, is suggestive of certain ancient myths.

But our life is not all play. We take great interest in our flowers, the hothouse beauties, and our trees. We give ourselves seriously to horticulture and embosom the chalet in flowers, of which we are passionately fond. Our lawns are always green, our shrubberies as well tended as those of a millionaire. Nothing, I assure you, can match the beauty of our walled garden. We are gluttons over our fruit and watch with tender interest our Montreuil peaches, our hotbeds, our laden trellises, and pyramidal pear-trees.

But lest these rural pursuits fail to satisfy my beloved's mind, I have advised him to finish, in the quiet of this retreat, some plays begun in his starving days, which are really very fine. This is the only kind of literary work that can be done in odd moments, for it requires long intervals of reflection and does not demand the elaborate pruning essential to a finished style. One cannot make task-work of dialogue; there must be biting touches, summings-up, and flashes of wit—the blossoms of the mind—which come rather by inspiration than reflection. This sort of intellectual sport suits me. I assist Gaston in his work, and so accompany him even in the boldest flights of his imagination. Do you see now how my winter evenings never drag?

Our servants have such an easy time that since our marriage we have never had to reprimand any of them. When questioned about us, they have had wit enough to invent an account, calling us the companion and secretary of a lady and gentleman supposed to be traveling. They never go out without permission, which they know will not be refused; they are contented, and see plainly it would be their own fault if things changed for the worse. The gardeners are allowed to sell the surplus of our fruits and vegetables. The dairymaid sells milk, cream, and butter on condition that the best is reserved for us. They take pleasure in their profits, and we are delighted with an abundance that no money in terrible Paris could procure, where a single fine peach costs a hundred francs.

All this has its meaning, my dear. I wish to fill the place of society to my husband; society is amusing, and his solitude must not pall. I believed myself jealous in the old days when I merely allowed myself to be loved; now I know real jealousy, the jealousy of the lover. A single indifferent glance unnerves me. From time to time I say, "Suppose he ceased to love me!" and a shudder runs through me. I tremble before him, as the Christian before his God.

Alas, Renée, I am still without a child. The time will surely come—it must—when our hermitage will need a father's and mother's care, when we shall both pine to see little frocks and pelisses, brown or golden heads leaping and running through our shrubberies and flowery paths. Oh, it is a cruel jest of Nature—a flowering tree that bears no fruit. The thought of your lovely children goes through me like a knife. My life has grown narrower while yours has expanded and shed its rays afar. Passion is essentially selfish, while motherhood widens the circle of feeling. How well I felt this when I read your kind, tender letter! To see you thus living in three hearts roused my envy. Yes, you are happy; you have had the wisdom to obey social laws, whilst I stand outside, an alien.

Children, dear, loving children, can alone console a woman for the loss of her beauty. I shall soon be thirty, and at that age the dirge within begins. What though I am still beautiful, the limits of my woman's reign are in sight. When they are reached, what then? I shall be forty before he is; I shall be old while he is still young. When this thought strikes, I lie at his feet for an hour, making him swear to tell me instantly if ever he feels his love diminishing.

But he is a child. He swears as though the suggestion were absurd, and he is so beautiful that—Renée, you understand—I believe him.

Good-bye, sweet one. Shall we ever again let years pass without writing? Happiness is a monotonous theme, and perhaps that is why, to souls who love, Dante seems greater in the Paradiso than in the Inferno. I am not Dante; I am only your friend, and I don't want to bore you. You can write, for in your children you have an ever-growing, ever-varying source of happiness, while mine... No more of this. A thousand loves.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
LIII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MME. GASTON

My dear Louise,—I have read your letter repeatedly; the more I enter
into its mood the more certain I am that it is the cry of a child, not
a woman. You forget what I used to tell you: the first passion belongs
to a state of nature and is fleeting; civilization must educate and
temper it. As you admit, love thus transformed ceases to be purely
natural.

Society, which abhors sterility, has invented the family to replace the
mere frenzy of nature with a lasting sentiment. For that the individual
is sacrificed: a married man devotes his energy, power, and possessions
to his wife; she reaps the comfort, luxury and position his care
provides.

You have taken the wrong turning again. Being adored is the dream of
youth; it cannot be the aim of a mature woman, a wife and mother. Vanity
may be content with knowing she could be adored, but if you would live
the life of wife and mother, return to Paris. It is not misfortune but
happiness you should fear: life consists in balanced movements; excess
produces fever of soul—pain or pleasure—that nature cannot long
sustain. To make life one long excess is to choose sickness. You are
sick because you keep at the passion-temperature a feeling marriage
ought to steady and purify.

The glory of a home is its calm, intimacy and shared responsibility,
qualities the frivolous deride. The Duchesse de Sully, when told of her
husband's mistress, answered: "What of that? I represent the honor of
the house." You, Louise—more passionate than tender—would be at once
wife and mistress. With Heloise's soul and Saint Theresa's passions you
let loose all impulses when law sanctions them; you degrade the
marriage rite. The reproaches you once flung at me for seizing early
happiness could be turned on you for grasping at anything that serves
your appetite. Must nature and society be in bondage to your caprice?
You are the old Louise: self-willed, unchanged; you bring into your love
the keenest and most mercenary calculations. Are you sure the price you
demand for your toilettes is not too high? Such precautions suggest
mistrust.

If you knew the sweetness of a mother disciplining herself in kindness
and gentleness! My proud, self-sufficient temper melted into a soft
melancholy, now swallowed up by the delights of motherhood. If the
early hours were toilsome, the evening is tranquil. My dread is that
your day should take the opposite course.

When I read your letter I prayed God to send you among us for a day, so
you might see what real family life is and learn those lasting joys
which are simple, natural and inexhaustible. Chance and your own
happiness, however, have proved my arguments vain. I hoped a few months
of honeymoon would restore you to reason, but there seems no limit to
your appetite; having killed a man who loved you, you will not cease
until you have killed love itself. Farewell, dear misguided friend. I am
in despair that the portrait of my happiness should have provoked only
a paean of selfishness. Yes, your love is selfish; you love Gaston less
for himself than for what he is to you.

LIV. MME. GASTON TO THE COMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE May 20th.

Renee, calamity for me is doubt; certainty would be death.

The day before yesterday, after my first toilette, I looked for Gaston
before lunch and found his mare Fedelta in a foam; the groom was
scraping mud from her legs.

"Who has put Fedelta in such a state?" I asked.

"Master," replied the lad.

Paris mud on the mare struck me like lightning: he had been to Paris
without telling me, at the hour when I leave him alone—suspicion seized
me. When Gaston found me pale and fluttered he embraced me and carried
me to the parlor; I wept in my room while he waited two hours, patient.
At last I said I would see him when I could speak without tears.

He left angry at my words but returned, promising explanation tomorrow.
All that day we spent together and I realized how closely my life
clung to him. When a man's return fills you with trembling and delight,
you live in him, not in yourself. I hated that mare and thought mad
things—lame Gaston, imprison him—because if love is not the cage, what
else can hold a man who wants to be free?

"Do I bore you?" I asked.

"What needless torture you give yourself!" he cried. "Never have I
loved you so deeply."

"If that is true, sell Fedelta," I said.

"Sell her, by all means!"

His answer crushed me; I thought it a covert taunt at my wealth. I left
him and that night the gardens seemed a precipice; such a night ages a
woman. Next morning I urged him to go to Paris alone; he read me and
went. He left at seven and returned at eleven—he must have spent hours
in town. He came back radiant with a new whip, an india-rubber lash
with a gold handle and a scent-box—exactly the sort I had wanted for a
fortnight. I admired it, but saw Verdier's name on the handle; Gaston
blushed like a child. I made much of him and pretended all was
forgotten.

May 25th.

Next morning I rode to Verdier's; a servant recognized the whip. My
heart tore. I confronted Gaston by the lake with the whip; he turned
white, fumbled, sought excuses. Jealousy had led to war between us; it
has neither sense nor decency.

I resolved to suffer in silence while watching until doubt was
resolved. There was a secret involving a woman. Is it a youthful
escapade? The question "what?" rose in letters of fire before me:
every sight, sound and word seemed to cry it. I, who had been devout in
love, found myself dragged into this duel.

May 30th.

Since that day Gaston no longer idles; he now sets tasks and devotes
hours to finishing his plays.

"He wants money!" a voice whispered. But why? We keep no secrets; his
study is open to me. He spends little; I know he once had thirty
thousand francs scattered in a drawer. At midnight, while he slept, I
went and found the drawer empty.

That week I saw he went to Sèvres to fetch—and perhaps tear up—letters;
I followed him to the post-office. He was appalled to be discovered
on horseback holding a letter; it was from Nathan telling him a play
was accepted and enclosing a box-ticket. This should have cleared my
doubts, yet an inner fiend kept whispering, "Where are the thirty
thousand francs?" The thought came close to speech but my dignity
forbade asking; a woman would rather fling herself into the lake.

I could have searched his drawer and found the missing money, but that
felt like seeking my own ruin. Still, the play's success—though credit
went to Nathan—was in part ours. I saw the first night from a stage
box.

July 1st.

Gaston continues his work and Paris visits. Three plays accepted, two
more commissioned. I am lost in darkness. Does he hesitate to take from
me because of pride? If so, it is but a scruple—yet if he needs such
sums to spend on a woman—then he deserts me for her. We have one
hundred thousand francs; if he feels ashamed to touch them, is it
because he loves another?

I explored all possibilities and became convinced I have a rival. I must
see the unknown.

July 10th.

Light has come, and it is over with me. At thirty, in the perfection of
my beauty, I am betrayed—and for whom? A large-boned Englishwoman with
big feet and a thick waist! I will tell you the last days.

Worn out with suspicions fed by Gaston's silence, his need of money and
frequent Paris trips, I discovered that when in Paris Gaston visits a
house in the Rue de la Ville-l'Évêque where he keeps a mistress with
mysterious jealousy. The porter was surly but gave enough to end hope.
I went to Paris, took rooms opposite the house, and saw him enter the
courtyard on horseback. I soon saw that this Englishwoman, about
thirty-six and known as Mme. Gaston, was with him; worse, I saw him
walking in the Tuileries with two children, living images of Gaston,
handsomely dressed in English clothes. She is the children's mother.
This discovery was my deathblow.

I returned home almost lifeless, and the next day extracted from the
porter that Mme. Gaston was legally married.

July 15th.

My love for Gaston is stronger than ever since that morning; he seems
more in love. He spoke of consulting doctors and advised travel for
months. "Travel" pierced me. I arranged for an easy death and made my
will. You can come to me now; the prohibition is removed. Come and see
me die. If passion has not been ours in intensity, we have at least
been spared its venom. Farewell.

LV. THE COMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE TO MME. GASTON July 16th.

My dear Louise,—I send this express before hastening to the chalet.
Take courage. Your last letter was so frantic I thought it right to
confide all to Louis; it was a question of saving you from yourself.
If our means were drastic, the result will satisfy you. I even set the
police to work, but the affair is kept among the prefect, ourselves,
and you.

In one word, Gaston is a jewel. The facts are these. His brother Louis
Gaston died in Calcutta while in mercantile service, on the point of
returning to France rich and married; he had received a large fortune
with his wife. For ten years he had worked to send home enough to
support his brother, concealing his trials in letters. Then the great
Halmer house failed; the widow was ruined and the shock affected Louis'
brain; he died in Bengal trying to realize the remnants of his wife's
property.

Louis had deposited with a banker three hundred thousand francs to go
to his brother, but the banker failed in the Halmer crash. Louis'
widow—the woman you took for your rival—arrived in Paris with two
children—your nephews—and an empty purse; her mother's jewels barely
paid for their passage. The instructions Louis had given the banker
enabled the widow to find Gaston's former home. As Gaston had
disappeared without leaving an address, Mme. Louis Gaston was directed
to d'Arthez, the only person who could help.

D'Arthez, ready to relieve her, had earlier been consulted by Louis on
the best means of transferring the money. He had written to Gaston to
tell him of the widow's needs and of Louis' intentions. Gaston, who
had saved fifty thousand francs out of the income you obligingly let
him take, hurried to Paris. He invested the fifty thousand in public
funds under the names of his two nephews, securing each about twelve
hundred francs a year. He furnished his sister-in-law's rooms and
promised her a quarterly allowance of three thousand francs. This
explains his rides to Paris and his dramatic labors, and the pleasure
he took in his play's success.

Mme. Gaston is therefore no rival and has every right to her name. A
man of Gaston's delicacy would keep the affair secret from you, knowing
your generous nature; he does not regard what you give him as his own.
D'Arthez read me Gaston's letter asking him to be a witness at the
marriage and confessing that except for poverty and indebtedness to
you his happiness would have been perfect. A sensitive soul makes such
scruples. Nothing is more natural than his wish to provide secretly for
his brother's widow, who had herself put aside one hundred thousand
francs for him. She is handsome, warm-hearted and well-bred, a mother;
I lost my heart to her when I saw her with one child in her arms and
the other dressed like a little lord. The children are everything in
her house.

Far from being angry with your husband, you should see in this fresh
reason to love him. I have met him and think him delightful; his soul
is in his face. If I were you, I would settle the widow and her
children at the chalet in a pretty cottage and adopt the boys.

Be at peace, dear soul, and plan this little surprise for Gaston.

LVI. MME. GASTON TO THE COMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE

Ah, my dear friend, what can I say but Lafayette's cruel "It is too
late"? Oh, my life, what physician will restore it? My own hand struck
the deathblow; I was a will-o'-the-wisp destined to perish before a
flame. My eyes rain torrents of tears—yet they must not fall when I am
with him. I fly to him and he seeks me. My despair is all within.
This torture Dante forgot to place in his Inferno. Come and see me die.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
THE CHALET,
August 7th.

My love,—Take the children to Provence without me; I remain with Louise, who has only a few days to live. I cannot leave her or her husband, for whose sake I fear.

You know the scrap of a letter that sent me flying to Ville d'Avray, picking up the doctors on my way. Since then I haven't left my darling friend; I have sat up every night for a fortnight.

When I arrived she and Gaston were in full dress, laughing and beautiful — a heroic falsehood. They seemed like two children in restored confidence. For a moment I was deceived, but Louise pressed my hand and whispered, "He must not know; I am dying."

I felt her burning hand and saw the red spots on her cheeks. I had left the doctors in the wood, thinking to summon them if needed.

"Leave us for a little," she said to Gaston. "Two women who have not met for five years have plenty of secrets to talk over." Once alone she threw herself into my arms and wept.

"I've brought, in case of need, the best surgeon and physician from the hospital, and Bianchon; there are four in all," I said.

"Have them in at once if they can save me, if there is still time. The passion which hurried me to death now cries for life!"

"But what have you done to yourself?" I cried.

"In a few days I've brought myself to the last stage of consumption."

"How?"

"I perspired at night, then ran out and lay by the lake in the dew. Gaston thinks I have a cold, and I am dying!"

I hurried for the doctors. After consultation none of them gave hope; they all believed Louise would die by the fall of the leaves. Her constitution predisposed her to consumption, and a few days' folly had made the case desperate.

I cannot tell you what I felt. You know I have lived in Louise as much as in my own life. Crushed, I could not even escort the doctors to the door. Tears ran down my cheeks until she roused me with the clear words, "So there is no hope for me!" She made me get up and, carrying me to her small drawing-room, besought me to stay to the end. She would not have doleful faces around her and above all must keep the truth from Gaston. "I know I have the strength," she said, "I am full of youth and spirit, and can die standing! For myself, I have no regrets. I am dying as I wished to die, still young and beautiful, in the perfection of my womanhood."

"As for him," she went on, "I can see very well now that I should have made his life miserable. Passion has me in its grips; my groundless jealousy has already wounded him sorely. Had my suspicions met only indifference, that would have been my death. I have had my share of life. Some people live sixty years and never have two years of real life; my thirty are doubled by the intensity of my love.

"Thus for him, as well as for me, the close is a happy one. But between us, dear Renée, it is different. You lose a loving sister, and that is a loss which nothing can repair. You alone here have the right to mourn my death."

After a long pause she continued, with that gentle voice you know: the moral of her death was cruel—her doctor in petticoats was right; marriage cannot rest on passion alone, nor even on love. She praised my steady married life and contrasted it with her own passionate eagerness, which by nature must diminish. Twice she had gone astray and twice Death had threatened her happiness—the first time taking the noblest and most devoted of men; now the monster would tear her from the arms of her poet husband. Yet she would not complain: she had known two noble men—Felipe, where soul dominated body, and Gaston, in whom heart, mind, and beauty were indissoluble. She died adored—what more could she wish? Perhaps time to draw nearer God; she hoped her spirit might one day be reunited to the two who had made her life a heaven.

"To others my example would be fatal, for mine was no common lot. To meet a Felipe or a Gaston is more than mortals can expect; therefore social doctrine accords with natural law. Woman is weak, and in marrying she ought to make an entire sacrifice of her will to the man who, in return, should lay his selfishness at her feet. The stir created by women's whining and insubordination is ridiculous and only shows how well we deserve philosophers' epithet of children."

She talked on in that grave, pretty manner until Gaston entered, bringing with him his sister-in-law, the two children, and the English nurse he had fetched from Paris at Louise's request.

"Here are the pretty instruments of my torture," she said as her nephews approached. "Was not the mistake excusable? What a wonderful likeness to their uncle!"

She was most friendly to Mme. Gaston the elder and begged that she look upon the chalet as her home; in short, she played hostess in her best de Chaulieu manner.

I wrote at once to the Duc and Duchesse de Chaulieu, the Duc de Rhetore, the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, and to Madeleine. It was time. The next day Louise, worn out, could only get up for dinner. Madeleine de Lenoncourt, her two brothers, and her mother arrived that evening. The coolness caused by Louise's second marriage vanished. Since then her father and both brothers rode over each morning, and the two duchesses spent every evening at the chalet. Death unites as well as separates; it silences paltry feeling.

Louise retained to the last her perfection of charm, grace, good sense, wit, and tenderness, and lavished on us the treasures of her brilliant mind. "I should like to look well even in my coffin," she said with that matchless smile as she lay down to linger for a fortnight. Her room had nothing of a sick-chamber; medicines and nursing apparatus were carefully concealed. "Is not my deathbed pretty!" she said to the Sèvres priest who came to confess her.

We gloated over her like misers. The anxiety and sudden truths prepared Gaston for the worst. He is full of courage, but the blow has gone home; it would not surprise me to see him follow his wife in the natural course. Yesterday, walking round the lake, he said to me, "I must be a father to those two children," and pointed to his sister-in-law taking the boys for a walk. "Though I shall do nothing to hasten my end, I want your promise that you will be a second mother to them, and will persuade your husband to accept the office of guardian, which I shall depute to him in conjunction with my sister-in-law."

He said this simply, like a man who knows he is not long for this world. He keeps smiles to meet Louise's, deceiving only me. He is a mate for her in courage.

Louise wished to see her godson, but I am not sorry he is in Provence; she might want to remember him generously, and I should be in great difficulty.

Good-bye, my love.

August 25th (her birthday).

Yesterday evening Louise was delirious for a short time; but even her delirium was charming: in a thread of a voice she sang Italian airs from I Puritani, La Sonnambula, Moise, while we stood around in silence. Not one of us, not even the Duc de Rhetore, had dry eyes — it was clear her soul was passing from us. She could no longer see us; yet she lingered in the faint melody, sweetness not of this earth.

During the night the death agony began. At seven in the morning I raised her from bed. Some flicker of strength revived; she wished to sit by her window and asked for Gaston's hand. Then, my love, the sweetest spirit whom we shall ever see on this earth departed, leaving the empty shell.

The last sacrament had been administered the evening before, unknown to Gaston, who had taken a snatch of sleep during this agonizing ceremony; after she was moved to the window she asked me to read her the De Profundis in French, while she faced the lovely scene, her handiwork. She repeated the words after me to herself and pressed the hands of her husband, who knelt on the other side of the chair.

August 26th.

My heart is broken. I have just seen her in her winding-sheet; her face is pale with purple shadows. "Oh! I want my children! my children! Bring me my children!" she cried in her last struggles.

THE END
