############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
MIDDLE OF THE STORY — MIDPOINT SCENE.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In the early spring of 1822 Parisian doctors sent a young man convalescing from an inflammatory complaint, brought on by overstudy or excess, to Lower Normandy. Recovery required rest, light diet, bracing air, and freedom from excitement; the fat lands of Bessin seemed to offer these. To Bayeux, a picturesque town six miles from the sea, the patient went and was welcomed with the cordiality of relatives who live retired lives and regard a new arrival as a godsend.

Small towns resemble one another except in local costume. After two or three evenings at his cousin’s house with Mme. de Sainte-Severe’s circle, M. le Baron Gaston de Nueil quickly met the handful of people whom that exclusive society called “the whole town.” He recognized the stock characters common to every provincial capital.

First is the family whose claim to ancient nobility is unquestioned locally though unknown fifty leagues away. This miniature royal family is distantly connected to old names; its head is inevitably a determined sportsman with no manners, crushing others by nominal superiority, tolerating the sub-prefect as he submits to taxes, and refusing to acknowledge nineteenth‑century innovations as political monstrosities. His wife speaks loud, has had admirers, takes the sacrament at Easter, brings up her daughters poorly, and believes their name will secure them. Neither cares for modern luxury; they keep a livery fit for the stage and cling to old plate, furniture, equipages, language, and manner. This antique state suits provincial thrift; they are lords of a bygone manor without quitrents or hounds, full of honor among themselves and devoted to distant princes. Among them one finds an uncle or brother, a lieutenant‑general or old courtier wearing the red ribbon of Saint‑Louis, like a stray leaf from Louis XV’s court.

This fossil greatness meets a rival in a wealthier but less ancient house. Husband and wife spend months in Paris and bring back its frivolity and brief fashions. Madame is a woman of fashion, conscious of clothes and often behind the mode; she scoffs at her neighbors’ affected ignorance. Her plate is modern; she keeps grooms and a valet. The eldest son drives a tilbury and does nothing (the estate is entailed on him); the younger is an auditor at the Council of State. The father knows official scandals and anecdotes of Louis XVIII and Madame du Cayla; he invests in five per cents, avoids cider talk, but sometimes speculates about local fortunes. A member of the Departmental Council, he buys Paris clothes and wears the Legion of Honor. He has grasped the Restoration’s significance and profits at the Chamber, though his Royalism is less pure than his rival’s; he reads the Gazette and the Débats, while the other family reads the Quotidienne.

Between these powers stands the Bishop, once Vicar‑General, who vacillates and receives the respect due to religion while sometimes being reminded of La Fontaine’s ass laden with relics. His origin is distinctly plebeian.

Next come men of family with modest incomes—captains in navy or cavalry, or idlers—who rank halfway on the roads between the cure with sacraments and the tax collector. Nearly all have been Pages or in Household Troops and now end their days in a faisance‑valoir, more interested in felling timber and cider prospects than politics. Still they talk of the Charter and Liberals while the cards make or backgammon settles. Their women assume Court airs in basket chaises, wrap in shawls and caps as full dress, and twice a year, after deliberation, receive a new bonnet from Paris. They are mostly exemplary and garrulous wives.

These are the principal elements of aristocratic gentility, with a few outlying old maids of good family—spinsters who have solved the problem of remaining absolutely stationary. They might be sealed up in their houses; their faces and dresses are fixtures in the town and province. They are its tradition, its memory, the genius loci incarnate, frigid and monumental; they know when to laugh and when to shake their heads, and occasionally utter remarks that pass as witticisms.

A few rich townspeople have crept into this miniature Faubourg Saint‑Germain by money or aristocratic leanings. Despite being forty, the circle will say of them, “Young So‑and‑so has sound opinions,” and make such men deputies. Elderly spinsters are often their patronesses, not without comment.

Finally, the set includes two or three ecclesiastics, admitted for the sake of their cloth or wit; these great nobles find their own society dull and introduce the bourgeois element into their drawing‑rooms as a baker puts leaven into dough.

The total of minds produces a stock of antiquated notions with a few new inflections brewed at evening gatherings. Like sea‑water in a creek, the phrases representing these ideas surge daily, obeying conversational tides; you hear the hollow echo of yesterday, today, and forever. They pass immutable judgments forming a tradition into which wit or sense seldom penetrates. Their lives revolve like clockwork in an orbit of use and wont, admitting no more deviation than their fixed opinions on religion, politics, morals, or letters.

If a stranger is admitted to the cenacle, each member will say with a trace of irony, “You will not find the brilliancy of your Parisian society here,” and then criticize others as if he alone had vainly tried to enlighten them. But any stranger who echoes their criticisms is pronounced ill‑natured, a heathen, an outlaw, a Parisian “as Parisians mostly are.”

Before Gaston de Nueil appeared in this world of strict etiquette—where every detail is part of a whole, and everything is known—he had been weighed in Bayeusaine judgment. His cousin, Mme. de Sainte‑Severe, had already declared the amount of his fortune and expectations, produced the family tree, and praised the talents, breeding, and modesty of this branch. He received precisely the attentions due him; accepted as a worthy scion of good stock and, at twenty‑three, made welcome without ceremony, though some young ladies and mothers of daughters regarded him with favor.

He had an income of eighteen thousand livres from land in the valley of the Auge; sooner or later his father would, as duty required, leave him the chateau of Manerville with its lands. No one questioned his education, political prospects, or qualities. His land was undeniable: rentals steady, plantations excellent, tenants paying repairs, rates, and taxes; apple‑trees thirty‑eight years old; and his father negotiating for two hundred acres of woodland to enclose with walls. No earthly hopes of a political career or fame could compare with such advantages.

Whether from malice or design, Mme. de Sainte‑Severe omitted that Gaston had an elder brother; nor did Gaston himself mention him. The brother was consumptive and seemed likely to die soon, lamented and forgotten.

At first Gaston amused himself at the circle’s expense. He drew portraits for his mental album of their angular, wrinkled faces, hooked noses, crotchets, and eccentricities of dress—portraits full of racy truth. He delighted in their “Normanisms,” in the primitive quaintness of their ideas and characters. For a short time he threw himself into their squirrel’s life of busy gyrations in a cage. Then he began to feel the want of variety and grew tired. It was like a life of the cloister cut short before it had well begun. He drifted on until he reached a crisis that was neither mere spleen nor mere disgust but a mixture of both. When a human being is transplanted into uncongenial soil and leads a starved, stunted existence, discomfort at the transition is certain. Gradually, if nothing removes him, he grows accustomed to surroundings and adapts to the vacuity that renders him powerless. Even now Gaston’s lungs were used to the air; he was willing to discern a kind of vegetable happiness in days with no mental exertion and no responsibilities. The constant stirring of life’s sap, the fertilizing influences of mind on mind he had sought so eagerly in Paris, began to fade from his memory, and he was in fair way to become a fossil with these fossils, ending his days among them, content, like Ulysses’ companions, in his gross envelope.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One evening Gaston de Nueil sat between a dowager and a vicar-general in a gray-paneled drawing-room with large white tiles. Family portraits watched four card-tables where some sixteen people chatted over whist. Digesting one of those provincial dinners he had anticipated all day, Gaston found himself justifying the country customs.

He began to see why these good folk still played with yesterday’s cards on a threadbare tablecloth and had ceased to dress for themselves or others. He perceived a philosophy in the even tenor of their perpetual round: the calm of methodical monotony, their ignorance of luxury’s refinements. He almost concluded that luxury profited little; Paris, with its passions and pleasures, had become scarcely more than a childhood memory.

He admired the red hands and shy, bashful manner of a young lady who at first seemed an awkward simpleton, unattractive and ridiculous. His fate was sealed. From the provinces to Paris and back again—he would have sunk into provincial life but for a chance remark that sent through him an emotion like a fine strain of music amid a tedious opera.

An elderly lady asked the head of the local royal family if he had called on Mme. de Beauseant. “I went this morning. She was so poorly and depressed that I could not persuade her to dine with us tomorrow,” he replied. When the dowager exclaimed about Mme. de Champignelles, he added casually, “With my wife. Mme. de Beauseant is descended from the House of Burgundy, on the spindle side, ’tis true, but the name atones for everything. My wife is attached to the Vicomtesse, and the poor lady has lived alone so long—”

The Marquis de Champignelles’ cool unconcern made it impossible to tell whether he pitied Mme. de Beauseant or was paying homage to her birth, whether he felt flattered to receive her or proud to force the country families to meet her. The women exchanged a glance; silence and disapproval fell.

“Is this Mme. de Beauseant the lady whose adventure with M. d’Ajuda-Pinto made so much noise?” Gaston asked his neighbor.

“The very same,” came the reply. “She came to Courcelles after the Marquis d’Ajuda’s marriage; nobody visits her. She has too much sense to ignore that she is in a false position, so she has made no attempt to receive. M. de Champignelles and a few gentlemen called on her, but she would see no one but M. de Champignelles, perhaps because he is a connection. They are related through the Beauseants; the father of the present Vicomte married a Mlle. de Champignelles of the older branch. But though the Vicomtesse de Beauseant is supposed to be of Burgundian descent, you can understand we could not admit a wife separated from her husband into our society here. We cling to old ideas. There was less excuse for the Vicomtesse, because M. de Beauseant is a well-bred man who would have listened to reason. But his wife is quite mad—”

M. de Nueil heard the words but retained little of their meaning; his mind was full of thick-coming fancies. What else could one call the alluring charm of an adventure that tempts the imagination and sets vague hopes springing up—sensations of coming events, mysterious felicity, and fear where no facts yet exist? Over these fancies thought hovers, conceiving impossible projects and giving, in the germ, all the joys of love. Perhaps all passion is contained in that thought-germ, as a flower’s beauty and fragrance are packed in the seed.

Gaston did not yet know that Mme. de Beauseant had taken refuge in Normandy after a notoriety women both envy and condemn, especially when youth and beauty excuse the transgression. Any kind of celebrity bestows prestige: for women, as for families, the glory of the crime can efface the stain. A noble house may be proud of tales of executed ancestors; a pretty young woman becomes more interesting through dubious renown—a happy love or a scandalous desertion makes her more compelling. We pity or admire what rises above the commonplace. The mob gives involuntary respect to anyone who lifts themselves above it and is not particular about the means.

Some such motives may have influenced Gaston, or perhaps curiosity, a craving for interest, or that crowd of inexplicable impulses we call “fatality,” drew him to Mme. de Beauseant.

Her figure rose before him with gracious associations. She was a new world—a world of fears and hopes to fight for and conquer. He felt the contrast between this vision and the shabby room; she was a woman unlike any he had seen in the dull little world of calculation and formal courtesy, where the simplest ideas were alarming. Her name revived a young man’s dreams and awakened urgent desires that had lain dormant.

Gaston was absent-minded the rest of the evening. He pondered how to gain access to Mme. de Beauseant, a task no easy matter. She was believed clever. If gifted people may be captivated by something subtle or eccentric, they are also exacting and can read beneath the surface; after the first step, success and failure are equally possible. Besides, the Vicomtesse had the dignity of her name; her seclusion was another barrier. For these reasons it seemed unlikely a stranger could hope for admittance. Yet the next morning found M. de Nueil walking toward Courcelles, a dupe of illusions natural to his age. Several times he circled the garden walls, peering through gaps at closed shutters or open windows, hoping for some romantic chance on which to found a scheme of introduction, utterly ignoring impracticability. Morning after morning he walked thus; the vision of a woman living apart, love’s martyr, loomed larger and was enshrined in his soul. A gardener’s heavy footstep would set his heart beating with hope.

He thought of writing her but, on reflection, what could one say to a woman one had never seen? Gaston had little self-confidence; like many young people with many illusions, he feared mortifying silence and shuddered at sending a first tender epistle into that void. Distracted by conflicting ideas, inventing chimeras, weaving romances, and beating his brains, he at last hit upon a stratagem: a means that would make clear to any woman that a man took a fervent interest in her. Social conventions place as many barriers between lovers as any fairy tale; in real life, as in stories, the woman belongs to the man who can reach and set her free. Distance lent enchantment.

Confident in his inspiration, he resolved to employ speech rather than letter, to engage feminine curiosity and plead his case in person. He went to M. de Champignelles, proposing to use the Marquis in his enterprise. He explained that he had been entrusted with a delicate commission concerning the Vicomtesse and feared she would not trust a stranger’s letter. Would the Marquis, on his next visit, ask if she would consent to receive M. de Nueil? While asking the Marquis to keep the secret in case of refusal, Gaston insinuated reasons for his admission—implying the right sort of motive. Love lends a young man the self-possession and craft of an old ambassador; the Marquis’ harmless vanities were gratified and he was completely duped. He tried to fathom Gaston’s secret, but the young man turned aside his questions with Norman shrewdness until the Marquis, as a gallant, complimented him on his discretion.

M. de Champignelles hurried to Courcelles, eager to serve a pretty woman, and in the Vicomtesse’s position such a message was likely to excite curiosity. Though her memory supplied no reason for receiving M. de Nueil, she saw no objection—after prudent inquiries about his family and condition. At first she refused, then, after discussion with the Marquis and guided by his discretion, she revoked her negative answer. The discussion and the Marquis’ reserve had piqued her curiosity.

The Marquis would not appear ridiculous; he professed the air of a man who can keep counsel, insisting that the Vicomtesse must know the visitor’s purpose. Mme. de Beauseant, in sincerity, had no idea. She connected Gaston with people he had never met, chased wild conjectures, and asked herself whether she had seen him before. No love-letter, however skilfully indited, could have produced so much effect as this riddle. Again and again she puzzled over it.

When Gaston learned he might call, his rapture was mingled with singular embarrassment. How contrive a suitable sequel to his stratagem?

“Bah! I shall see her,” he kept telling himself as he dressed. “See her, and that is everything!”

He hoped that once across Courcelles’ threshold an expedient would reveal itself. There are believers in necessity’s omnipotence who never turn back; the nearness of danger inspires them. Gaston was one of these.

He took pains with his dress, imagining, as youth does, that success depends on a curl, forgetting that youth itself is charming. In any case, women like Mme. de Beauseant are chiefly attracted by wit or uncommon character. Greatness flatters their vanity and seems to promise a great passion; wit amuses and answers their subtlety, making them feel understood. And what do women want but to be amused, understood, or adored? Only after long reflection do we comprehend the coquetry of neglecting dress and reserve at a first interview; by the time we learn the strategy, we are often too old to profit.

While Gaston borrowed charms from his clothes, Mme. de Beauseant was also attending to her toilette. “I would rather not frighten people,” she said as she arranged her hair.

M. de Nueil had an unconscious originality that lent flavor to his words and deeds and absolved them. He was highly cultivated, had a keen brain, and a face as mobile as his nature, winning goodwill. The promise of passion in his bright eyes matched a kindly heart. His resolution as he entered Courcelles fitted his frank nature and ardent imagination. Bold in love, his heart nevertheless beat violently as he crossed the great court laid out like an English garden. The man-servant, who had taken his name to the Vicomtesse, returned to say she would receive him.

“M. le Baron de Nueil.”

Gaston entered slowly but with sufficient ease; it is harder, it is said, to enter a room where there is only one woman than a room that holds a score.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A great fire burned on the hearth; by the soft candlelight he saw a young woman sitting on a low bergère. The seat let her move her head freely; every turn was graceful, whether she bent or raised it, crossed her feet and let them appear, or drew them back under the folds of a long black dress.

The Vicomtesse moved to lay down the book she was reading; the volume fell to the floor between the small stand and the sofa. Unperturbed, she looked up and inclined her head to his greeting without rising. Bending forward, she stirred the fire, stooped for a fallen glove and drew it over her left hand while her eyes sought the other. The glance stopped when she stretched out a thin, white, almost transparent right hand with slender, ringless fingers and pointed to a chair, inviting Gaston to sit. No words could describe the subtle, gracious inquiry in that gesture; deliberate kindness, the ease of a woman trained in life’s graces. Her swift, deft movements blended a pretty woman’s fastidious carelessness with the noble manner of a great lady.

Mme. de Beauseant contrasted strongly with the automatons among whom he had spent two months of exile in that remote Normandy district. She seemed the realization of his romantic dreams and defied comparison with other women he had admired. In her presence, in a drawing-room like a Faubourg Saint-Germain salon, full of costly trifles, flowers, and books, he felt transported back to Paris: a Parisian carpet beneath his feet, the fragile outline of a high-bred Parisienne, her exquisite charm, her disdain for the studied effects that spoil provincial women.

She had fair hair, dark eyes, and the pale complexion of blondes. She held her brow nobly, like a fallen angel grown proud. The way she gathered her thick hair into a crown of plaits above curving bandeaux added to the queenliness of her face; one could imagine a ducal coronet in the spiral threads of her golden hair. The courage of her house seemed to gleam in her brilliant eyes, a courage used to repel audacity yet full of tenderness for gentleness. Her small head, poised above a long white throat, her delicate features and the subtle curve of her lips formed a mobile face worn with discreet irony suggestive of craft and insolence. Yet those failings were softened by lines that appeared in her brow when she was not at rest, and by upward glances—pathetic tricks of manner—which told plainly of unhappiness, of a passion that had almost cost her life.

A woman sitting in the great, silent salon, cut off from the world in this remote valley, alone with memories of brilliant, impassioned youth and continual gaiety now replaced by a void—was there not something awe-inspiring? Consciousness of her own value and of her misfortune lurked in her smile. She was neither wife nor mother; she was an outlaw: she had lost the one heart that might have set her pulses beating without shame; she had nothing from without to support her reeling soul; she must draw strength from within and cherish no hope but forsaken love, which looks forward to Death. To feel destined for happiness and to die never having given or received it! A woman too! What pain! These thoughts flashed through M. de Nueil’s mind like lightning and left him humble before the greatest charm with which woman can be invested. The triple aureole of beauty, nobleness, and misfortune dazzled him; he stood in dreamy admiration of the Vicomtesse. But he found nothing to say.

Mme. de Beauseant, not displeased at his surprise, held out her hand with a kindly but imperious gesture; summoning a smile to her pale lips as if obeying a woman’s impulse to be gracious, she said:

“I have heard from M. de Champignelles of a message which you have kindly undertaken to deliver, monsieur. Can it be from—”

The unfinished phrase made Gaston understand, more clearly than before, his own ridiculous position and the bad taste and bad faith of his conduct toward a woman so noble and unfortunate. He reddened. Thoughts crowded upon him and showed in his troubled eyes; yet with the courage youth draws from a sense of its own wrongdoing, he gained confidence and interrupted humbly.

“Madame,” he faltered, “I do not deserve the happiness of seeing you. I have deceived you basely. However strong the motive, it can never excuse the pitiful subterfuge I used to gain my end. But, madame, if your goodness will permit me to tell you—”

The Vicomtesse glanced at him with haughty disdain, stretched toward the bell and rang it.

“Jacques,” she said, “light this gentleman to the door,” and looked with dignity at the visitor. She rose proudly, bowed, and stooped for the fallen volume. If her movements at his entrance had been caressingly dainty and gracious, her every gesture now was severely frigid. M. de Nueil rose but stood waiting. Mme. de Beauseant’s look seemed to say, Well, why do you not go?

There was such cutting irony in that look that Gaston grew white as if about to faint. Tears sprang to his eyes, but burning shame and despair dried them. He looked back at the Vicomtesse with a pride and consciousness of his own worth mingled with humility; she had a right to punish him, but ought she to exercise it? Then he went out.

As he crossed the ante-chamber, his head cleared and his wits, sharpened by passion, grasped the danger of his situation.

“If I leave this house, I can never come back to it again,” he said to himself. “The Vicomtesse will always think me a fool. It is impossible that a woman, and such a woman, should not guess the love she has called forth. Perhaps she feels a little vague regret for dismissing me so abruptly—but she could not recall her sentence. It rests with me to understand her.”

Gaston stopped on the stairs with an exclamation; he turned sharply, saying, “I have forgotten something,” and returned to the salon. The lackey, respectful of a baron and the rights of property, was deceived by the natural utterance and followed him. The Vicomtesse, thinking the intruder the servant, looked up and beheld M. de Nueil.

“Jacques lighted me to the door,” he said with a half-sad smile that dispelled any suspicion of jest, the tone going to the heart. Mme. de Beauseant was disarmed.

“Very well, take a seat,” she said.

Gaston eagerly took a chair. His eyes shone with happiness; the Vicomtesse, unable to endure the brilliant light in them, looked down at the book. She enjoyed the sensation; a man’s delight in her presence is an unfailing feminine instinct. Besides, he had divined her, and a woman is grateful to the man who masters the apparently capricious yet logical reasoning of her heart and can read the flashes of coquetry and self-revelation.

“Madame,” Gaston exclaimed in a low voice, “my blunder you know, but you do not know how much I am to blame. If you only knew what joy it was to—”

“Ah! take care,” she said, holding up a finger with an air of mystery as she put out her hand toward the bell.

The charming gesture, the gracious threat, called up some sad memory of the old happy time when she could be wholly gentle without afterthought; when the gladness of her heart justified caprice and put charm into every movement. Lines gathered between her brows and the expression of her face grew dark in the candlelight. Then, looking across at M. de Nueil gravely but not unkindly, she spoke like a woman who felt the weight of every word.

“This is all very ridiculous! Once upon a time, monsieur, when thoughtless high spirits were my privilege, I should have laughed fearlessly over your visit. But now my life is very much changed. I cannot do as I like; I am obliged to think. What brings you here? Is it curiosity? In that case I am paying dearly for a little fleeting pleasure. Have you fallen passionately in love already with a woman you have never seen, a woman whose name slander has, of course, besmirched? If so, your motive is disrespect, an error which accident has made notorious.”

She flung her book down scornfully, then, with a terrible look at Gaston, went on: “Because I once was weak, must it be supposed that I am always weak? This is horrible, degrading. Or have you come to pity me? You are very young to offer sympathy with heart troubles. Understand this clearly, sir: I would rather have scorn than pity. I will not endure compassion from anyone.”

A brief pause followed.

“Well, sir,” she continued, her face gentle and sad, “whatever motive induced this rash intrusion upon my solitude, it is very painful to me, you see. You are too young to be totally without good feeling, so surely you will feel that this behavior of yours is improper. I forgive you for it, and, as you see, I speak of it without bitterness. You will not come here again, will you? I entreat when I might command. If you come to see me again, neither you nor I can prevent the whole place from believing you my lover, and you would cause me great additional annoyance. You do not mean to do that, I think.”

She said no more, but looked at him with a great dignity which abased him.

“I have done wrong, madame,” he said, with deep feeling, “but it was through enthusiasm and thoughtlessness and eager desire of happiness, the qualities and defects of my age. Now I understand that I ought not to have tried to see you.” He spoke more to feeling than to intellect, describing the weariness of his forced exile and painting the portrait of a young man whose fires of life were burning low, a heart worthy of tender love that had never known it from a refined woman. He did not seek to justify his conduct but explained it: the joy of finding in her the ideal lady of a young man’s dream, the vision he had pursued in vain. He told of his morning prowlings under the walls of Courcelles and of the wild thoughts the first sight of the house had stirred, till he awakened that vague indulgence a woman can feel for the follies committed for her sake.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
An impassioned voice broke the chill solitude; the speaker brought a warm breath of youth and the charm of a cultivated mind. Mme. de Beauseant had not been so moved by delicately expressed feeling for a long time; it affected her strongly. In spite of herself she watched M. de Nueil’s expressive face and admired a noble countenance not yet hardened by the world’s cruel discipline. Gaston was in the flower of youth; he seemed full of something not yet aware of the great career before him. So both indulged dangerous reflections, and both strove to conceal them.

M. de Nueil saw in the Vicomtesse a rare type of woman: her graceful beauty was the least of her charms for those who knew the infinite feeling, thought, and goodness within her; a woman whose instinctive sense of beauty purified love and made it almost holy — that secret of womanhood Nature seldom bestows. The Vicomtesse, hearing the ring of sincerity in Gaston’s voice as he spoke of youthful troubles, began to understand what twenty-five-year-olds suffer from diffidence when hard work has kept them from corrupting intercourse with a sophistical world. Here was the ideal of a woman’s dream: a man unspoiled by family egoism or the narrow selfishness that blights the first impulses of honor, devotion, and self-sacrifice.

But whereas Gaston’s experiments were unconscious, Mme. de Beauseant’s remarks were purposeful; she used her natural and acquired subtlety to learn his opinions by advancing views opposite to her own. So witty and gracious, so utterly herself with this stranger she felt sure they would never meet again, that after some delicious epigram Gaston exclaimed unthinkingly, “Oh! madame, how could any man have left you?”

The Vicomtesse was silent; Gaston reddened, fearing he had offended her. She was not angry. The cry from a young man’s heart gave her back innocence in her own eyes, condemned the world, and laid blame upon the lover who had left her. That verdict restored to her the social absolution, heartfelt sympathy, and esteem so long refused — all her secret desires answered in that exclamation, sweetened by the flattering admiration women always relish. He understood her, and had offered, as if naturally, the opportunity of rising higher through her fall.

Gaston begged her not to punish him for his heedlessness; she smilingly parried, then spoke gravely. She explained why she could not see him again. He had too much magnanimity to understand that if any suspicion of a second offense attached to her everyone would condemn her as vulgar; she must live a pure and blameless life to set her character in relief. She was too proud not to remain apart, a victim in the eyes of society because of her marriage and of love. She had not the lofty virtue to remain with a man she did not love; she had snapped the bonds of marriage because they meant death to her — she meant to live. Perhaps, she said, if she had been a mother she could have endured a forced marriage of suitability. At eighteen we scarcely know what is done to us. She had broken the laws of the world and the world had punished her; she sought happiness, as is nature. She was young and beautiful; for a little while she had been loved.

She confessed she had thought no man could leave a woman in her position. Forsaken, she at first blamed another; now she was content to bear the whole blame, absolving the other of her first complaints. She lacked the art to keep him; fate had punished her for her lack of skill. She only knew how to love and was a slave when she should have been a tyrant. Those who knew her might condemn her, but would also respect her. Pain had taught her never to expose herself to that risk again. She spoke of the three years of solitude that had strengthened her enough to describe that time without falling apart. Such agony, she said, usually ends in death; she had endured the agony of death without a tomb.

She raised her eyes to the ceiling — the cornice, a conspiratorial confidant in the boudoir — and, eloquent and coquettish, justified herself and love in a way that stirred every sentiment in the man before her. The higher she set the goal, the more conspicuous it grew. At last, when her eyes lost their too-eloquent expression, she let them fall on Gaston and asked calmly if he acknowledged that she was bound to a solitary, self-contained life.

So sublime in her reasoning that M. de Nueil felt a wild longing to throw himself at her feet; but fear of ridicule and of failing to express himself kept him in check. He broke the silence in a faltering voice, confessing that she had set his heart swelling: he longed to make her forget her mortifications, to devote his life to her, to give her love for all who had wounded her. But he admitted this outpouring could not justify anything that day.

“Enough, monsieur,” said Mme. de Beauseant; “we have both of us gone too far. By giving you sad reasons for a refusal I meant to soften it and not to elicit homage. Coquetry only suits a happy woman. Believe me, we must remain strangers. At a later day you will know that ties which must inevitably be broken ought not to be formed at all.”

She sighed lightly. “How painful it is for a woman to be powerless to follow the man she loves through all the phases of his life! And if that man loves her truly, his heart must surely vibrate with pain at the deep trouble in hers. Are they not twice unhappy?” Then, smiling, she rose and observed that he had perhaps not expected a sermon when he came to Courcelles.

Gaston felt farther than before from this extraordinary woman. Had the charm of the hour been mere coquetry? Had she been anxious to display her wit? He bowed stiffly and left in desperation. On the way home he tried to detect the real character of a creature supple and hard as a steel spring, but her many phases confounded him. Her voice, gestures, and changing eyes grew more gracious in memory; her beauty shone again in the darkness. He fell into wandering musings where lucid thoughts become refractory and the soul passes briefly into frenzy. Youth only can understand those dithyrambic outpourings when, after stormy conflict between folly and common-sense, the heart yields to its latest passion.

At twenty-three diffidence often rules. A man is perplexed like a young girl’s shyness; he fears he will express his love ill and takes alarm. He would be bolder if he loved less, for he has no confidence in himself; with a growing sense of the cost of happiness comes the conviction that the woman he loves cannot easily be won. He worships in secret, and unless his love is guessed it dies away. Early loves often linger, bright with illusions, like children who die in the flower of childhood. Thus M. de Nueil came home the victim of a mood of desperate resolution. He felt Mme. de Beauseant one of the conditions of his existence; life without her seemed worse than death. He was to spend one of those stormy nights in which a young man’s thoughts race from happiness to suicide and back.

Too deeply in love to sleep, he rose to indite letters but burned them all. The next day he returned to Courcelles to pace her garden walls at night, afraid lest she see him. After painful hesitation he wrote her a letter — the familiar effusions of lovers — full of fervor and awkward phrases but sincere. He begged her not to throw the letter away and explained that his fate depended on her that day. He could not attract a woman’s love by artifice; he only knew how to love. He avowed himself unworthy of her, young, ignorant, shy, yet irresistibly drawn to her by the happiness she gave him. For him she was the only woman in the world; he could not imagine life without her. He had resolved to leave France and risk his life in some desperate enterprise if refused; how could he quell a limitless love but by opposing something as infinite? Yet, if she would allow him hope — not to be hers, but to win her friendship — he would stay. Let him come, he entreated, infrequently if necessary, to spend a few hours like those stolen yesterday. The keen delight of such brief happiness, cut short at the least over-ardent word, would enable him to endure the torrent in his veins. He asked, with humble submission, for an intercourse in which all the gain would be his alone. She could show the world that he was nothing to her; she was clever and proud enough. If he were refused he would go, without murmur; and if another woman entered his life later, she would be proved right; but if he remained faithful to death, perhaps she might feel some regret — the slighted heart’s sole revenge.

Only those who have suffered the excrescences of youth can realize the horrors that seized him when he supposed his ultimatum was in Mme. de Beauseant’s hands. He imagined the Vicomtesse untouched, laughing at his letter and his love like those who have ceased to believe in love. He wished to have the letter back, hating its stilted, pretentious phrases and crooked lines. He tried not to think, not to feel, but he felt and thought and was wretched. If he had been thirty he might have drunk, but the innocence of twenty-three knew nothing of opium nor of the expedients of more experienced men. Nor had he one of those Parisian friends who know how to say “Poète, non dolet!” by producing a bottle of champagne or dull the agony of suspense by carrying you off to drown it. Such capital fellows are often unreliable — low on funds when you need them, off to a watering-place when you look them up, with some bad bargain in horse-flesh to sell — yet in other respects the best of comrades, always ready to embark on misadventures where you lose time, soul, and life.

Thus they left him: alone with his feverish hopes and fears, the night stretching before him and the future uncertain, bound by the perilous charm of a woman whose truth and coquetry had both inspired and repelled him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At length M. de Nueil received a missive through Jacques, a letter sealed with the arms of Burgundy, written on vellum.

He locked himself away and read and reread her letter:—

“You are punishing me very severely, monsieur, both for the friendliness of my attempt to spare you a rebuff, and for the attraction which intellect always has for me. I put confidence in the generosity of youth, and you have disappointed me. And yet, if I did not speak unreservedly (which would have been perfectly ridiculous), at any rate I spoke frankly of my position, so that you might imagine that I was not to be touched by a young soul. My distress is the keener for my interest in you. I am naturally tender-hearted and kindly, but circumstances force me to act unkindly. Another woman would have flung your letter, unread, into the fire; I read it, and I am answering it. My answer will make it clear to you that while I am not untouched by the expression of this feeling which I have inspired, albeit unconsciously, I am still far from sharing it, and the step which I am about to take will show you still more plainly that I mean what I say. I wish besides, to use, for your welfare, that authority, as it were, which you give me over your life; and I desire to exercise it this once to draw aside the veil from your eyes.

“I am nearly thirty years old, monsieur; you are barely two-and-twenty. You yourself cannot know what your thoughts will be at my age. The vows that you make so lightly to-day may seem a very heavy burden to you then. I am quite willing to believe that at this moment you would give me your whole life without a regret, you would even be ready to die for a little brief happiness; but at the age of thirty experience will take from you the very power of making daily sacrifices for my sake, and I myself should feel deeply humiliated if I accepted them. A day would come when everything, even Nature, would bid you leave me, and I have already told you that death is preferable to desertion. Misfortune has taught me to calculate; as you see, I am arguing perfectly dispassionately. You force me to tell you that I have no love for you; I ought not to love, I cannot, and I will not. It is too late to yield, as women yield, to a blind unreasoning impulse of the heart, too late to be the mistress whom you seek. My consolations spring from God, not from earth. Ah, and besides, with the melancholy insight of disappointed love, I read hearts too clearly to accept your proffered friendship. It is only instinct. I forgive the boyish ruse, for which you are not responsible as yet. In the name of this passing fancy of yours, for the sake of your career and my own peace of mind, I bid you stay in your own country; you must not spoil a fair and honorable life for an illusion which, by its very nature, cannot last. At a later day, when you have accomplished your real destiny, in the fully developed manhood that awaits you, you will appreciate this answer of mine, though to-day it may be that you blame its hardness. You will turn with pleasure to an old woman whose friendship will certainly be sweet and precious to you then; a friendship untried by the extremes of passion and the disenchanting processes of life; a friendship which noble thoughts and thoughts of religion will keep pure and sacred. Farewell; do my bidding with the thought that your success will bring a gleam of pleasure into my solitude, and only think of me as we think of absent friends.”

Gaston de Nueil read the letter and wrote:—

“MADAME,—If I could cease to love you, to take the chances of becoming an ordinary man which you hold out to me, you must admit that I should thoroughly deserve my fate. No, I shall not do as you bid me; the oath of fidelity which I swear to you shall only be absolved by death. Ah! take my life, unless indeed you do not fear to carry a remorse all through your own—”

When the servant returned from his errand, the Baron asked with whom he had left the note.

“I gave it to Mme. la Vicomtesse herself, sir; she was in her carriage and just about to start.”

“For the town?”

“I don’t think so, sir. Mme. la Vicomtesse had post-horses.”

“Ah! then she is going away,” said the Baron.

“Yes, sir,” the man answered.

Gaston at once prepared to follow Mme. de Beauseant. She went as far as Geneva without suspecting he followed. Amid the many thoughts that assailed him during the journey, one question filled his mind: “Why did she go away?” Theories arose; he favored the one that flattered him—that if the Vicomtesse cared for him, a clever woman would choose Switzerland, where nobody knew either of them, rather than France with its critics. An impassioned lover of a certain stamp would not feel attracted to a woman clever enough to choose her own ground; such women are too clever. But there was no proof of Gaston’s supposition.

The Vicomtesse took a small house by the lake. As soon as she was installed, Gaston came one summer twilight. Jacques showed no surprise and announced M. le Baron de Nueil like a discreet domestic. At the sound of his name and sight of him, Mme. de Beauseant let her book fall; her surprise allowed him to come close and say in tones that sounded like music:

“What a joy it was to me to take the horses that brought you on this journey!”

To have the inmost desire of the heart so fulfilled—where is the woman who could resist such happiness? An Italian woman once remarked on French novels that she could not see why lovers took so long to come to terms, that the affair should be the work of a single morning. Why not, the remark implied, spare the reader excessive delay? Some passages of coquetry are pleasant; Mme. de Beauseant’s demurs and sweet delays might have been sketched rather than exhaustively narrated. M. de Nueil was at an age when a man is the dupe of such caprices, charmed by the fencing women prolong to dictate terms or savor their power. But these boudoir protocols are too small to be of consequence in the history of this passion.

For three years Mme. de Beauseant and M. de Nueil lived in the villa on Lake Geneva. They lived alone, received no visitors, caused no talk, rose late, went out on the lake together—knew, in short, the simple happiness of which we all dream. It was a little house, with green shutters, broad balconies shaded with awnings, a place contrived for lovers—white couches, soundless carpets, fresh hangings—everything reflecting their joy. Every window showed some new view of the lake; in the distance lay mountains, visions of changing color; above them the sunny sky; before them the broad sheet of water, never the same. All their surroundings seemed to dream for them; all things smiled upon them.

Weighty matters recalled M. de Nueil to France. His father and brother died, and he had to leave Geneva. The lovers bought the house; if they could they would have removed the hills and drawn off the lake, they said.

Mme. de Beauseant followed him. She realized her property and bought a considerable estate near Manerville, adjoining Gaston’s lands; here they lived together; Gaston graciously gave up Manerville to his mother for the present in consideration of the bachelor freedom she left him.

Her estate was close to a little town in a picturesque spot in the Auge. Here the lovers raised barriers between themselves and society, barriers no creature could overleap, and here the happy days of Switzerland were lived over again. For nine years they knew happiness which it serves no purpose to describe in detail; happiness to be divined by those who can comprehend poetry and prayer in their manifestations.

All this time Mme. de Beauseant’s husband, the present Marquis (his father and elder brother having died), enjoyed the soundest health. There is no better aid to life than a certain knowledge that our demise would confer a benefit on some fellow-creature. M. de Beauseant was one of those ironical and wayward beings who, like holders of life-annuities, wake with an extra relish for health. He was a man of the world, somewhat methodical and ceremonious, a calculator of consequences, who could make a declaration of love as quietly as a lackey announces “Madame is served.”

This brief notice explains why the Marquise could not marry M. de Nueil.

So, after a nine years’ lease of happiness—the sweetest agreement to which a woman ever put her hand—M. de Nueil and Mme. de Beauseant were still in a position as natural and as false as at the beginning. Yet they had reached a fatal crisis, which may be stated as clearly as any problem in mathematics.

Mme. la Comtesse de Nueil, Gaston’s mother, a strait-laced and virtuous person who had made the late Baron happy in strictly legal fashion, would never consent to meet Mme. de Beauseant. Mme. de Beauseant understood that the dowager must of necessity be her enemy and try to draw Gaston from his unhallowed life. The Marquise would willingly have sold her property and gone back to Geneva, but she could not bring herself to do it; it would mean distrusting M. de Nueil. Moreover, he had taken a great fancy to this Valleroy estate, making plantations and improvements. She would not deprive him of that small routine-work which women always wish for their husbands, and even for their lovers.

A Mlle. de la Rodiere, twenty-two, an heiress with a rent-roll of forty thousand livres, had come to live nearby. Gaston always met her at Manerville when obliged to go there. These personages stood to each other as terms in a proportion sum; the following letter will throw light on the appalling problem Mme. de Beauseant had been trying for the past month to solve:—

“My beloved angel, it seems like nonsense, does it not, to write to you when there is nothing to keep us apart, when a caress so often takes the place of words, and words too are caresses? Ah, well, no, love. There are some things a woman cannot say when she is face to face with the man she loves; at the thought of them her voice fails, and the blood goes back to her heart; she has no strength, no intelligence left. It hurts me to feel like this when you are near me, and it happens often. I feel that my heart should be wholly sincere for you; that I should disguise no thought, however transient, in my heart; and I love the sweet carelessness, which suits me so well, too much to endure this embarrassment and constraint any longer. So I will tell you about my anguish—yes, it is anguish. Listen to me! do not begin with the little “Tut, tut, tut” that you use to silence me, an impertinence that I love, because anything from you pleases me.

“Dear soul from heaven, wedded to mine, let me first tell you that you have effaced all memory of the pain that once was crushing the life out of me. I did not know what love was before I knew you. Only the candor of your beautiful young life, only the purity of that great soul of yours, could satisfy the requirements of an exacting woman’s heart. Dear love, how very often I have thrilled with joy to think that in these nine long, swift years, my jealousy has not been once awakened. All the flowers of your soul have been mine, all your thoughts. There has not been the faintest cloud in our heaven; we have not known what sacrifice is; we have always acted on the impulses of our hearts. I have known happiness, infinite for a woman. Will the tears that drench this sheet tell you all my gratitude? I could wish that I had knelt to write the words!

“Well, out of this felicity has arisen torture more terrible than the pain of desertion. Dear, there are very deep recesses in a woman’s heart; how deep in my own heart I did not know until to-day, as I did not know the whole extent of love. The greatest misery which could overwhelm us is a light burden compared with the mere thought of harm for him whom we love. And how if we cause the harm, is it not enough to make one die? This is the thought that weighs upon me. But it brings in its train another thought that is heavier far, a thought that tarnishes the glory of love, and slays it, and turns it into a humiliation which sullies life as long as it lasts. You are thirty years old; I am forty. What dread this difference calls up in a woman who loves! It is possible that, first unconsciously, afterwards in earnest, you have felt the sacrifices you made by renouncing all in the world for me. Perhaps you have thought of your future from the social point of view, of the marriage which would increase your fortune, give you avowed happiness and children who would inherit your wealth; perhaps you have thought of reappearing in the world and filling your place there honorably. And then, if so, you must have repressed those thoughts, and felt glad to sacrifice heiress and fortune and a fair future to me without my knowledge. In your young man’s generosity, you must have resolved to be faithful to the vows which bind us each to each in the sight of God. My past pain has risen up before your mind, and the misery from which you rescued me has been my protection. To owe your love to your pity! The thought is even more painful to me than the fear of spoiling your life for you. The man who can bring himself to stab his mistress is very charitable if he gives her her deathblow while she is happy and ignorant of evil, while illusions are in full blossom.... Yes, death is preferable to the two thoughts which have secretly saddened the hours for several days.

“To-day, when you asked ‘What ails you?’ so tenderly, the sound of your voice made me shiver. I thought that, after your wont, you were reading my very soul, and I waited for your confidence to come, thinking that my presentiments had come true, and that I had guessed all that was going on in your mind. Then I began to think over certain little things that you always do for me, and I thought I could see in you the sort of affection by which a man betrays a consciousness that his loyalty is becoming a burden. And in that moment I paid very dear for my happiness. I felt that Nature always demands the price for the treasure called love. Briefly, has not fate separated us? Can you have said, ‘Sooner or later I must leave poor Claire; why not separate in time?’ I read that thought in the depths of your eyes, and went away to cry by myself. Hiding my tears from you—the first tears I have shed for sorrow for these ten years; I am too proud to let you see them, but I did not reproach you.

“Yes, you are right. I ought not to be so selfish as to bind your long and brilliant career to my so-soon out-worn life.... And yet—how if I have been mistaken? How if I have taken your love melancholy for a deliberation? Oh, my love, do not leave me in suspense; punish this jealous wife of yours, but give her back the sense of her love and yours; the whole woman lies in that—that consciousness sanctifies everything.

“Since your mother came, since you paid a visit to Mlle. de Rodiere, I have been gnawed by doubts dishonoring to us both. Make me suffer for this, but do not deceive me; I want to know everything that your mother said and that you think! If you have hesitated between some alternative and me, I give you back your liberty.... I will not let you know what happens to me; I will not shed tears for you to see; only—I will not see you again.... Ah! I cannot go on, my heart is breaking..................
I have been sitting benumbed and stupid for some moments. Dear love, I do not find that any feeling of pride rises against you; you are so kind-hearted, so open; you would find it impossible to hurt me or to deceive me; and you will tell me the truth, however cruel it may be. Do you wish me to encourage your confession? Well, then, heart of mine, I shall find comfort in a woman’s thought. Has not the youth of your being been mine, your sensitive, wholly gracious, beautiful, and delicate youth? No woman shall find henceforth the Gaston whom I have known, nor the delicious happiness that he has given me.... No; you will never love again as you have loved, as you love me now; no, I shall never have a rival, it is impossible. There will be no bitterness in my memories of our love, and I shall think of nothing else. It is out of your power to enchant any woman henceforth by the childish provocations, the charming ways of a young heart, the soul’s winning charm, the body’s grace, the swift communion of rapture, the whole divine cortege of young love, in fine.

“Oh, you are a man now, you will obey your destiny, weighing and considering all things. You will have cares, anxieties, ambitions, and concerns that will rob her of the unchanging smile that made your lips fair for me. The tones that were always so sweet for me will be troubled at times; and your eyes that lighted up with radiance from heaven at the sight of me will often be lustreless for her. And besides, as it is impossible to love you as I love you, you will never care for that woman as you have cared for me. She will never keep a constant watch over herself as I have done; she will never study your happiness at every moment with an intuition which has never failed me. Ah, yes, the man, the heart and soul, which I shall have known will exist no longer. I shall bury him deep in my memory, that I may have the joy of him still; I shall live happy in that fair past life of ours, a life hidden from all but our inmost selves.

“Dear treasure of mine, if all the while no least thought of liberty has risen in your mind, if my love is no burden on you, if my fears are chimerical, if I am still your Eve—the one woman in the world for you—come to me as soon as you have read this letter, come quickly! Ah, in one moment I will love you more than I have ever loved you, I think, in these nine years. After enduring the needless torture of these doubts of which I am accusing myself, every added day of love, yes, every single day, will be a whole lifetime of bliss. So speak, and speak openly; do not deceive me, it would be a crime. Tell me, do you wish for your liberty? Have you thought of all that a man’s life means? Is there any regret in your mind? That I should cause you a regret! I should die of it. I have said it: I love you enough to set your happiness above mine, your life before my own. Leave on one side, if you can, the wealth of memories of our nine years’ happiness, that they may not influence your decision, but speak! I submit myself to you as to God, the one Consoler who remains if you forsake me.”

When Mme. de Beauseant knew that her letter was in M. de Nueil’s hands, she sank into utter prostration; the pressure of many thoughts so numbed her faculties that she seemed drowsy. She suffered a pain not always proportioned to a woman’s strength, a pain women alone know. And while the unhappy Marquise awaited her doom, M. de Nueil, reading her letter, felt that he was “in a very difficult position,” to use the phrase young men apply to such a crisis.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By this time he had nearly yielded to his mother’s insistence and to the attractions of Mlle. de la Rodiere, a somewhat insignificant, pale young woman whose silence matched the conventional view of a marriageable girl; her forty thousand livres of rent-roll spoke for her. Mme. de Nueil, with maternal devotion, tried to draw her son into a respectable life. She urged him that it was flattering to be preferred by a young lady who had refused many great matches; reminded him to think of his future, that he might one day have eighty thousand livres of income from land; argued that money made everything bearable; and declared that if Mme. de Beauseant loved him truly she should be the first to urge him to marry. Using every feminine argument on the masculine mind, she had brought her son into a wavering state.

Mme. de Beauseant’s letter arrived just as Gaston’s love for her persisted against the temptations of a conventional life. That letter decided him: he resolved to break with the Marquise and to marry. “One must live a man’s life,” he told himself. He foresaw the pain this would cause Mme. de Beauseant; vanity and the lover’s conscience exaggerated that pain, and sincere pity seized him. He imagined a charitable plan: to deaden the blow, to reconcile her gradually to separation; to sacrifice Mlle. de la Rodiere at first as a shadowy third, only to impose her later; to marry later, in obedience to Mme. de Beauseant’s expressed wish; even to enlist the Marquise’s nobility and pride to help him in this compassionate design. He would write a letter at once to allay her suspicions.

For a woman of acute perception and the intuition of passionate love, a letter could be a sentence of death.

So when Jacques came and brought Mme. de Beauseant a sheet of paper folded in a triangle, she trembled like a snared swallow. A cold fear wrapped her. If he did not rush to her feet, if he did not come to her in tears, pale and like a lover, she knew all was lost. Yet a woman who loves keeps hope, and is only killed by repeated wounds.

“Does madame need anything?” Jacques asked gently as he went away.

“No,” she said.

“Poor fellow!” she thought, brushing away a tear; “he guesses my feelings, servant though he is!”

She read: “My beloved, you are inventing idle terrors for yourself...” The Marquise gazed at the words and a mist spread before her eyes. A voice in her heart cried, “He lies!” She glanced down and found at the foot, “Nothing has been decided as yet...” Turning the page she saw the writer’s mind through the intricacies of the wording; this was no spontaneous outburst of love. She crushed the paper in her fingers, tore it with her teeth, flung it on the fire, and cried aloud, “Ah! base that he is! I was his, and he had ceased to love me!”

She sank half dead upon the couch.

M. de Nueil went out as soon as he had written his letter. When he came back, Jacques met him at the threshold with a note. “Madame la Marquise has left the château,” the man said.

M. de Nueil, amazed, broke the seal and read:

“MADAME,—If I could cease to love you, to take the chances of becoming an ordinary man which you hold out to me, you must admit that I should thoroughly deserve my fate. No, I shall not do as you bid me; the oath of fidelity which I swear to you shall only be absolved by death. Ah! take my life, unless indeed you do not fear to carry a remorse all through your own...”

It proved to be his own letter—one he had written to the Marquise as she set out for Geneva nine years before. At its foot Claire de Bourgogne had written, “Monsieur, you are free.”

M. de Nueil went to his mother at Manerville. In less than three weeks he married Mlle. Stéphanie de la Rodiere.

If this commonplace story ended here, it might seem a mystification. But the fame of the catastrophe and the memories it awakens in anyone who has known the divine delights of consuming passion—and lost them by their own deed or the cruelty of fate—may shelter the tale from easy criticism.

Mme. la Marquise de Beauseant never left Valleroy after parting from M. de Nueil. After his marriage she continued to live there, for some inscrutable woman’s reason; any woman may assign the one that most appeals. Claire de Bourgogne lived in complete retirement so that none of the servants, save Jacques and her own woman, ever saw their mistress. She required absolute silence and only left her room to go to the chapel on the estate, where a neighboring priest said mass every morning.

The Comte de Nueil sank, a few days after his marriage, into something like conjugal apathy that might be read as happiness or unhappiness. “My son is perfectly happy,” his mother said everywhere. Mme. Gaston de Nueil was, like many young wives, a rather colorless character—sweet and passive. A month after her marriage she expected to become a mother, which fitted ordinary views. M. de Nueil was very nice to her; but two months after his separation from the Marquise he grew notably thoughtful and abstracted. Then he always had been serious, his mother said.

After seven months of this tepid happiness, a small incident occurred—one of those trifles that betray profound inner trouble. One day, after shooting over Manerville and Valleroy, M. de Nueil crossed Mme. de Beauseant’s park on his way home, summoned Jacques, and asked whether the Marquise still liked game. Jacques answered yes. Gaston offered him a good sum, with plausible reasoning, for a small service: would he set aside for the Marquise the game the Count would bring? It mattered little to Jacques whether the partridge on which his mistress dined had been shot by her keeper or by M. de Nueil, especially since the latter particularly wished that the Marquise should know nothing about it.

“It was killed on her land,” said the Count, and for some days Jacques lent himself to the harmless deceit. Day after day M. de Nueil went shooting and came back at dinner-time with an empty bag. A whole week passed. Gaston grew bold enough to write a long letter to the Marquise and had it conveyed to her. It was returned to him unopened. The Marquise’s servant brought it back about nightfall. The Count, sitting in the drawing-room while his wife at the piano mangled a Caprice of Hérold, suddenly sprang up and rushed out to the Marquise as if flying to an assignation. He dashed through a known gap into the park, and went slowly along the avenues, stopping now and again to still the loud beating of his heart.

Smothered sounds as he neared the château told him the servants were at supper, and he went straight to Mme. de Beauseant’s room.

Mme. de Beauseant never left her bedroom. M. de Nueil could reach the doorway without making a sound. By two candles he saw the thin, white Marquise in a great armchair; her head bowed, hands hanging listlessly, her eyes fixed as if on something unseen. Her whole attitude spoke hopeless pain. There was a vague something like hope in her bearing, but it was impossible to say whether she looked forward to the tomb or back into the past. Perhaps Gaston’s tears glittered in the shadows; perhaps his breathing sounded; perhaps unconsciously he trembled—however it was, Mme. de Beauseant slowly turned her face toward the doorway and beheld her old lover. Then Gaston stepped forward a few paces.

“If you come any further, sir,” exclaimed the Marquise, growing paler, “I shall fling myself out of the window!”

She sprang to the window, flung it open, stood with one foot on the ledge, her hand on the iron balustrade, her face turned toward Gaston.

“Go out! go out!” she cried, “or I will throw myself over.”

At that dreadful cry the servants stirred, and M. de Nueil fled like a criminal.

When he reached home he wrote a few lines and gave them to his own man, telling him to put the letter himself into Mme. de Beauseant’s hands and to say it was a matter of life and death for his master. The messenger went. M. de Nueil returned to the drawing-room where his wife was still at the piano and sat down to await an answer. An hour later the Caprice ended and husband and wife sat in silence on opposite sides of the hearth. The man came back from Valleroy and gave his master his own letter unopened.

M. de Nueil went into a small room beyond the drawing-room where he had left his rifle, and shot himself.

The swift and fatal end of the drama, contrary to the habits of young France, is only what might have been expected. Those who have closely observed, or known by delicious experience, the perfect union of two beings will understand Gaston de Nueil’s suicide. A woman does not bend and form herself in a day to the caprices of passion. The pleasure of loving, like a rare flower, needs cultivation. Time alone, and two souls attuned to each other, discover its resources and create the tender delights for which we are steeped in superstitions, imagining them inherent in the loving heart. It is this response of one nature to another, this religious certainty of peculiar happiness in the presence of the beloved, that accounts for perdurable attachments and long-lived passion. If a man who has once possessed this love shuts himself from it by his own act and sinks into a loveless marriage; if, by some incident in married life, the woman with whom he hoped to know the same felicity makes it clear it will never be revived; if, with the sweetness of divine love still on his lips, he has dealt a deadly wound to the woman he forsook for a social chimera—then he must either die or adopt a materialistic, selfish, heartless philosophy, from which impassioned souls recoil.

As for Mme. de Beauseant, she doubtless did not imagine her friend’s despair would drive him to suicide, though he had drunk deep of love for nine years. Possibly she thought she alone was to suffer. At any rate she rightly refused the most humiliating of positions; a wife may stoop for weighty social reasons to a compromise which a mistress must hold in abhorrence, for in the purity of her passion lies all its justification.

ANGOULEME, September 1832.
