################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
MIDDLE OF THE STORY — PIVOTAL TURN.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

La Grenadiere is a little house on the right bank of the Loire, about a mile below the bridge of Tours. Here the river, broad as a lake and dotted with green islands, runs between two lines of cliff where white-stone country houses stand among gardens and vineyards. The finest fruit ripens on these south-facing slopes. Patient toil over generations has cut terraces in the cliff so the rock absorbs the sun and hot-climate produce can be grown outdoors in an artificially warm exposure.

A church spire rising from a shallower dip marks the little village of Saint-Cyr, to which the scattered houses belong; a little further the Choisille flows into the Loire through a fertile valley cut in the low downs.

La Grenadiere itself, halfway up the hillside and about a hundred paces from the church, is one of those old-fashioned houses two or three hundred years old that you find in every picturesque spot in Touraine. A fissure in the rock gives space for a flight of steps descending to the “dike,” the embankment at the foot of the cliffs that keeps the Loire in its bed and serves as causeway for the Paris–Nantes road. At the top of the steps a gate opens on a narrow stony footpath between two terraces: soil banked up, walls built against landslips, crowned with trellises and espaliers so that the steep path below the upper wall is almost hidden by the trees on the lower. At every step the view of the river widens as you climb to the house.

At the end you come to a second gateway, a Gothic archway with simple ornament, crumbling and overgrown with moss, ivy, wallflowers and pellitory. Every stone wall on the hillside is decked with this persistent plant-life, which sprouts anew each season along the cracks.

The worm-eaten gate opens onto a little garden: a strip of turf, a few trees and a wilderness of flowers and rose bushes—garden won from the rock on the highest terrace, with a dark old balustrade along its edge. Opposite the gateway a wooden summer-house leans against the neighboring wall; its posts are cloaked in jessamine, honeysuckle, vines and clematis.

The house stands in the middle of this highest garden, above a vine-covered flight of steps; beneath an arched doorway lie vast cellars hollowed in the rock. All about the dwelling trellised vines and pomegranate trees—the grenadiers that give the place its name—grow in the open. The front has two large windows on either side of a rustic door and three dormers in a slate roof with two gables, the roof disproportionately high compared with the low ground floor. The walls are yellowwashed; the door, first-floor shutters and Venetian attic shutters are painted green.

Inside is a little lobby with a crooked wooden staircase straight ahead—an old structure whose spiral balusters are brown with age and whose steps shift angle at every turn. On the right is the great old-fashioned paneled dining-room, floored with square white tiles from Chateau-Regnault; to the left a sitting-room, equally large, its walls not paneled but covered with saffron-colored paper bordered with green. Walnut rafters are visible, the spaces between filled with white plaster.

The first story has two large whitewashed bedrooms with stone chimneypieces, less elaborately carved than those below. Every door and window faces south, save a single north door contrived behind the staircase to give access to the vineyard. Against the western wall leans a supplementary timber-framed structure, its woodwork fledged with slates so the walls are checkered with bluish lines; this shed is the kitchen. You can pass from it into the house without going outside, but it has its own entrance and a short flight of steps down to a deep well and a rustical pump half hidden by water-plants, savin bushes and tall grasses. The kitchen is a modern addition, proving La Grenadiere began as a simple vendangeoir—a vintage-house belonging to townsfolk in Tours, who came over for the day or the harvest, sending provisions across in the morning and seldom sleeping there except at vintage-time. When the English settled in Touraine, La Grenadiere had to be completed to find tenants; luckily this recent appendage is hidden from view by the first two trees of a lime-tree avenue planted in a gully below the vineyards.

There are at most two acres of vineyard; the ground rises so steeply at the back that it is no easy matter to scramble among the vines. The slope, covered with green trailing shoots, ends within about five feet of the house wall in a ditch-like passage always damp and cold, full of vigorous green things fed by the drainage of the highly cultivated ground above—rain washes manure down into the terrace garden. A vinedresser’s cottage leans against the western gable and is in some sort a continuation of the kitchen. Stone walls and espaliers surround the property and fruit trees are planted among the vines; not an inch of the precious soil is wasted. If man overlooks some dry crevice, Nature plants a fig-tree, or wildflowers and strawberries take root in sheltered nooks among the stones.

Nowhere else will you find a dwelling so humble and yet so imposing, so rich in fruit and fragrant scents, and with such wide views of country. It is a miniature Touraine in the heart of Touraine: all its flowers, fruits and characteristic beauty are represented. Here are grapes of every district, figs, peaches and pears of every kind; melons grow outdoors as easily as licorice plants; Spanish broom, Italian oleanders and jessamines from the Azores flourish. The Loire lies at your feet; from the terrace you look down upon the ever-changing river almost two hundred feet below, and in the evening the breeze brings a faint scent of the sea mingled with far-off flowers gathered on its way.

A wandering cloud across the pure blue can alter every detail of the widespread landscape in a thousand ways from every point of view. The eye first embraces the south bank of the Loire stretching away to Amboise, then Tours with its suburbs and buildings, and the Plessis rising out of the fertile plain. Further off, between Vouvray and Saint-Symphorien, a crescent of gray cliff is full of sunny vineyards; beyond, the low rich hills along the Cher form a bluish horizon broken by many a chateau and the wooded masses of many a park. To the west the river widens into an immense basin where vessels come and go, spreading white sails to the winds that seldom fail in the Loire.

A prince might build a summer palace at La Grenadiere, but it will always be a poet’s desire and the sweetest retreat for two young lovers. This vintage-house, belonging to a substantial burgess of Tours, charms every imagination—from the humblest and dullest to the most impassioned and lofty. No one can dwell here without feeling that happiness is in the air, without a glimpse of what a peaceful life without care or ambition means. There is something in the air and in the sound of the river that makes you dream; the sands have a language, joyous or dreary, golden or wan. The owner of the vineyard may sit motionless amid perennial flowers and tempting fruit and yet feel all the stir of the world about him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
If an Englishman takes the house for the summer, he is asked a thousand francs for six months, the produce of the vineyard not included. If the tenant wishes for the orchard fruit, the rent is doubled; for the vintage, it is doubled again. What can La Grenadiere be worth, you wonder; La Grenadiere, with its stone staircase, its beaten path and triple terrace, its two acres of vineyard, its flowering roses about the balustrades, its worn steps, well-head, rampant clematis, and cosmopolitan trees? It is idle to make a bid! La Grenadiere will never be in the market; it was brought once and sold, but that was in 1690; and the owner parted with it for forty thousand francs, reluctant as any Arab of the desert to relinquish a favorite horse. Since then it has remained in the same family, its pride, its patrimonial jewel, its Regent diamond. “While you behold, you have and hold,” says the bard. And from La Grenadiere you behold three valleys of Touraine and the cathedral towers aloft in air like a bit of filigree work. How can one pay for such treasures? Could one ever pay for the health recovered there under the linden-trees?

In the spring of one of the brightest years of the Restoration, a lady with her housekeeper and her two children (the oldest a boy thirteen years old, the youngest apparently about eight) came to Tours to look for a house. She saw La Grenadiere and took it. Perhaps the distance from the town was an inducement to live there.

She made a bedroom of the drawing-room, gave the children the two rooms above, and the housekeeper slept in a closet behind the kitchen. The dining-room was sitting-room and drawing-room all in one for the little family. The house was furnished very simply but tastefully; there was nothing superfluous in it, and no trace of luxury. The walnut-wood furniture chosen by the stranger lady was perfectly plain, and the whole charm of the house consisted in its neatness and harmony with its surroundings.

It was rather difficult, therefore, to say whether the strange lady (Mme. Willemsens, as she styled herself) belonged to the upper middle or higher classes, or to an equivocal, unclassified feminine species. Her plain dress gave rise to the most contradictory suppositions, but her manners might be held to confirm those favorable to her. She had not lived at Saint-Cyr, moreover, for very long before her reserve excited the curiosity of idle people, who always, and especially in the country, watch anybody or anything that promises to bring some interest into their narrow lives.

Mme. Willemsens was rather tall; she was thin and slender, but delicately shaped. She had pretty feet, more remarkable for the grace of her instep and ankle than for the more ordinary merit of slenderness; her gloved hands, too, were shapely. There were flitting patches of deep red in a pale face, which must have been fresh and softly colored once. Premature wrinkles had withered the delicately modeled forehead beneath the coronet of soft, well-set chestnut hair, invariably wound about her head in two plaits, a girlish coiffure which suited the melancholy face. There was a deceptive look of calm in the dark eyes, with the hollow, shadowy circles about them; sometimes, when she was off her guard, their expression told of secret anguish. The oval of her face was somewhat long; but happiness and health had perhaps filled and perfected the outlines. A forced smile, full of quiet sadness, hovered continually on her pale lips; but when the children, who were always with her, looked up at their mother, or asked one of the incessant idle questions which convey so much to a mother’s ears, then the smile brightened, and expressed the joys of a mother’s love. Her gait was slow and dignified. Her dress never varied; evidently she had made up her mind to think no more of her toilette, and to forget a world by which she meant no doubt to be forgotten. She wore a long, black gown, confined at the waist by a watered-silk ribbon, and by way of scarf a lawn handkerchief with a broad hem, the two ends passed carelessly through her waistband. The instinct of dress showed itself in that she was daintily shod, and gray silk stockings carried out the suggestion of mourning in this unvarying costume. Lastly, she always wore a bonnet after the English fashion, always of the same shape and the same gray material, and a black veil. Her health apparently was extremely weak; she looked very ill. On fine evenings she would take her only walk, down to the bridge of Tours, bringing the two children with her to breathe the fresh, cool air along the Loire, and to watch the sunset effects on a landscape as wide as the Bay of Naples or the Lake of Geneva.

During the whole time of her stay at La Grenadiere she went but twice into Tours; once to call on the headmaster of the school, to ask him to give her the names of the best masters of Latin, drawing, and mathematics; and a second time to make arrangements for the children’s lessons. But her appearance on the bridge of an evening, once or twice a week, was quite enough to excite the interest of almost all the inhabitants of Tours, who make a regular promenade of the bridge. Still, in spite of a kind of spy system, by which no harm is meant, a provincial habit bred of want of occupation and the restless inquisitiveness of the principal society, nothing was known for certain of the newcomer’s rank, fortune, or real condition. Only, the owner of La Grenadiere told one or two of his friends that the name under which the stranger had signed the lease (her real name, therefore, in all probability) was Augusta Willemsens, Countess of Brandon. This, of course, must be her husband’s name. Events, which will be narrated in their place, confirmed this revelation; but it went no further than the little world of men of business known to the landlord.

So Madame Willemsens was a continual mystery to people of condition. Hers was no ordinary nature; her manners were simple and delightfully natural, the tones of her voice were divinely sweet,--this was all that she suffered others to discover. In her complete seclusion, her sadness, her beauty so passionately obscured, nay, almost blighted, there was so much to charm, that several young gentlemen fell in love; but the more sincere the lover, the more timid he became; and besides, the lady inspired awe, and it was a difficult matter to find enough courage to speak to her. Finally, if a few of the bolder sort wrote to her, their letters must have been burned unread. It was Mme. Willemsens’ practice to throw all the letters which she received into the fire, as if she meant that the time spent in Touraine should be untroubled by any outside cares even of the slightest. She might have come to the enchanting retreat to give herself up wholly to the joy of living.

The three masters whose presence was allowed at La Grenadiere spoke with something like admiring reverence of the touching picture that they saw there of the close, unclouded intimacy of the life led by this woman and the children.

The two little boys also aroused no small interest. Mothers could not see them without a feeling of envy. Both children were like Mme. Willemsens, who was, in fact, their mother. They had the transparent complexion and bright color, the clear, liquid eyes, the long lashes, the fresh outlines, the dazzling characteristics of childish beauty.

The elder, Louis-Gaston, had dark hair and fearless eyes. Everything about him spoke as plainly of robust, physical health as his broad, high brow, with its gracious curves, spoke of energy of character. He was quick and alert in his movements, and strong of limb, without a trace of awkwardness. Nothing took him unawares, and he seemed to think about everything that he saw.

Marie-Gaston, the other child, had hair that was almost golden, though a lock here and there had deepened to the mother’s chestnut tint. Marie-Gaston was slender; he had the delicate features and the subtle grace so charming in Mme. Willemsens. He did not look strong. There was a gentle look in his gray eyes; his face was pale, there was something feminine about the child. He still wore his hair in long, wavy curls, and his mother would not have him give up embroidered collars, and little jackets fastened with frogs and spindle-shaped buttons; evidently she took a thoroughly feminine pleasure in the costume, a source of as much interest to the mother as to the child. The elder boy’s plain white collar, turned down over a closely fitting jacket, made a contrast with his brother’s clothing, but the color and material were the same; the two brothers were otherwise dressed alike, and looked alike.

No one could see them without feeling touched by the way in which Louis took care of Marie. There was an almost fatherly look in the older boy’s eyes; and Marie, child though he was, seemed to be full of gratitude to Louis. They were like two buds, scarcely separated from the stem that bore them, swayed by the same breeze, lying in the same ray of sunlight; but the one was a brightly colored flower, the other somewhat bleached and pale. At a glance, a word, an inflection in their mother’s voice, they grew heedful, turned to look at her and listened, and did at once what they were bidden, or asked, or recommended to do. Mme. Willemsens had so accustomed them to understand her wishes and desires, that the three seemed to have their thoughts in common. When they went for a walk, and the children, absorbed in their play, ran away to gather a flower or to look at some insect, she watched them with such deep tenderness in her eyes, that the most indifferent passer-by would feel moved, and stop and smile at the children, and give the mother a glance of friendly greeting. Who would not have admired the dainty neatness of their dress, their sweet, childish voices, the grace of their movements, the promise in their faces, the innate something that told of careful training from the cradle? They seemed as if they had never shed tears nor wailed like other children. Their mother knew, as it were, by electrically swift intuition, the desires and the pains which she anticipated and relieved. She seemed to dread a complaint from one of them more than the loss of her soul. Everything in her children did honor to their mother’s training. Their threefold life, seemingly one life, called up vague, fond thoughts; it was like a vision of the dreamed-of bliss of a better world. And the three, so attuned to each other, lived in truth such a life as one might picture for them at first sight--the ordered, simple, and regular life best suited for a child’s education.

Both children rose an hour after daybreak and repeated a short prayer, a habit learned in their babyhood. For seven years the sincere petition had been put up every morning on their mother’s bed, and begun and ended by a kiss. Then the two brothers went through their morning toilet as scrupulously as any pretty woman; doubtless they had been trained in habits of minute attention to the person, so necessary to health of body and mind, habits in some sort conducive to a sense of wellbeing. Conscientiously they went through their duties, so afraid were they lest their mother should say when she kissed them at breakfast-time, “My darling children, where can you have been to have such black finger-nails already?” Then the two went out into the garden and shook off the dreams of the night in the morning air and dew, until sweeping and dusting operations were completed, and they could learn their lessons in the sitting-room until their mother joined them. But although it was understood that they must not go to their mother’s room before a certain hour, they peeped in at the door continually; and these morning inroads, made in defiance of the original compact, were delicious moments for all three. Marie sprang upon the bed to put his arms around his idolized mother, and Louis, kneeling by the pillow, took her hand in his. Then came inquiries, anxious as a lover’s, followed by angelic laughter, passionate childish kisses, eloquent silences, lisping words, and the little ones’ stories interrupted and resumed by a kiss, stories seldom finished, though the listener’s interest never failed.

“Have you been industrious?” their mother would ask, but in tones so sweet and so kindly that she seemed ready to pity laziness as a misfortune, and to glance through tears at the child who was satisfied with himself.

She knew that the thought of pleasing her put energy into the children’s work; and they knew that their mother lived for them, and that all her thoughts and her time were given to them. A wonderful instinct, neither selfishness nor reason, perhaps the first innocent beginnings of sentiment teaches children to know whether or not they are the first and sole thought, to find out those who love to think of them and for them. If you really love children, the dear little ones, with open hearts and unerring sense of justice, are marvelously ready to respond to love. Their love knows passion and jealousy and the most gracious delicacy of feeling; they find the tenderest words of expression; they trust you--put an entire belief in you. Perhaps there are no undutiful children without undutiful mothers, for a child’s affection is always in proportion to the affection that it receives--in early care, in the first words that it hears, in the response of the eyes to which a child first looks for love and life. All these things draw them closer to the mother or drive them apart. God lays the child under the mother’s heart, that she may learn that for a long time to come her heart must be its home. And yet--there are mothers cruelly slighted, mothers whose sublime, pathetic tenderness meets only a harsh return, a hideous ingratitude which shows how difficult it is to lay down hard-and-fast rules in matters of feeling.

Here, not one of all the thousand heart ties that bind child and mother had been broken. The three were alone in the world; they lived one life, a life of close sympathy. If Mme. Willemsens was silent in the morning, Louis and Marie would not speak, respecting everything in her, even those thoughts which they did not share. But the older boy, with a precocious power of thought, would not rest satisfied with his mother’s assertion that she was perfectly well. He scanned her face with uneasy forebodings; the exact danger he did not know, but dimly he felt it threatening in those purple rings about her eyes, in the deepening hollows under them, and the feverish red that deepened in her face. If Marie’s play began to tire her, his sensitive tact was quick to discover this, and he would call to his brother:

“Come, Marie! let us run in to breakfast, I am hungry!”

But when they reached the door, he would look back to catch the expression on his mother’s face. She still could find a smile for him, nay, often there were tears in her eyes when some little thing revealed her child’s exquisite feeling, a too early comprehension of sorrow.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mme. Willemsens dressed during the children’s early breakfast and play; coquettish for her darlings, she wished to please them, a gracious vision with an irresistible charm.

She was always ready to hear their lessons from ten till three, with a noon lunch taken together in the summer-house. After lunch the children played for an hour while she lay on a long sofa there, placed so she could look out over the soft, ever-changing Touraine countryside, a landscape renewed with every shift of light and season.

The children scampered through the orchard, over the terraces, chased lizards, explored flowers, seeds, and insects, continually bringing questions to their mother and running between garden and summer-house. In the country they needed no toys; everything amused them.

During lessons Mme. Willemsens sat at her embroidery. She neither spoke nor looked at masters or pupils, but followed all that was said, striving to grasp the sense of the lessons and Louis’ progress. If Louis asked a puzzling question, her eyes would light up and she would smile at him with hope. Of Marie she inquired little; her desire was fixed on her eldest son. She treated him with respectful tact, aiming to awaken in him a spirit of high endeavor, to teach him to think himself capable of great things. This had a secret purpose Louis was to understand in time; indeed he already did.

After lessons she accompanied the master to the gate and asked strict accounts of Louis’ progress. So winning was her manner that tutors told her the truth, pointing out weaknesses she might help remedy. Then came dinner, play, a walk, and lessons till bedtime.

Their days were uniform yet full; work and amusements left no dull hour. Discouragement and quarrelling were impossible. The mother’s boundless love smoothed everything. She taught moderation by refusing nothing, and submission by making them see Necessity in many forms; she bolstered them with timely praise, nurturing all that was best as a good fairy might. Tears sometimes rose to her eyes as she watched them play, thinking they had never vexed her. Such complete happiness brings tears because it represents the dim imaginings of Heaven.

Those were delicious hours on the sofa in the garden-house, looking over river and landscape, listening to their laughter and small quarrels that showed their unity of heart, Louis’ paternal care for Marie, and the love both bore their mother. They spoke English and French equally well—an English nurse had raised them—so their mother spoke both, guiding their minds skillfully, allowing no fallacious reasoning or bad principle. She ruled by kindness, concealing nothing, explaining everything. If Louis wanted books, she gave him interesting but accurate ones—biographies, lives of seamen, captains, and famous men—since small incidents in their histories offered countless openings to explain the world. She pointed out how truly great men rose from obscurity and achieved noble destinies through themselves.

These readings, important among Louis’ lessons, took place while little Marie slept on her mother’s knee, the Loire reflecting the sky; but when they ended the woman’s sadness seemed doubled; she would fall silent, motionless and pensive, her eyes filling with tears.

“Mother, why are you crying?” Louis asked one balmy June evening as twilight succeeded a hot day.

Deeply moved, she put her arm about him and drew him close.

“Because, my boy, the lot of Jameray Duval, the poor and friendless lad who succeeded at last, will be yours, yours and your brother’s, and I have brought it upon you. Before very long, dear child, you will be alone in the world, with no one to help or befriend you. While you are children, I shall leave you, and yet, if only I could wait till you are big enough and know enough to be Marie’s guardian! But I shall not live so long. I love you so much that it makes me very unhappy to think of it. Dear children, if only you do not curse me some day!—”

“But why should I curse you some day, mother?”

“Some day,” she said, kissing his forehead, “you will find out that I have wronged you. I am going to leave you here, without money, without”—she hesitated—“without a father,” and at the word burst into tears and gently pushed him away. A sort of intuition told Louis she wished to be alone, and he carried off Marie, now half awake. An hour later, when Marie was in bed, he stole down to the summer-house where his mother sat.

“Louis! come here.”

The words were delicious to his heart. He sprang into her arms and they held each other in an almost convulsive embrace.

“Cherie,” he said—the name he often used—“cherie, why are you afraid that you are going to die?”

“I am ill, my poor darling; every day I am losing strength, and there is no cure; I know that.”

“What is the matter with you?”

“Something you must never know. You must not know what caused my death.”

The boy fell silent, glancing at her as she watched the clouds. It was a sad, sweet moment. Louis could not believe she would die soon, but he felt a trouble he could not name. If older, he might have read happy memories and repentance there—the careless childhood, loveless marriage, a terrible passion, flowers born of storm then struck down into an abyss.

“Darling mother,” Louis said at last, “why do you hide your pain from me?”

“My boy, we ought to hide troubles from strangers,” she replied; “we should smile, never speak of ourselves to them, nor think of ourselves; these rules, practiced in family life, conduce to happiness. You will have much to bear one day! Ah me! then think of your poor mother who died smiling before your eyes, hiding her suffering, and you will take courage to endure life’s ills.”

She choked back tears and tried to explain existence’s mechanism: the value of money, the standing and consideration it brings, and their bearing on social position; honorable means of gaining a livelihood, and the necessity of training. She told him that chief among her sadness was the thought that, after her death, he and Marie would be almost without resources, with but a slender sum and no friend but God.

“How quick I must be about learning!” cried Louis, giving her a piteous look.

“Oh! how happy I am!” she said, showering kisses and tears. “He understands me!—Louis,” she went on, “you will be your brother’s guardian, will you not? You are no longer a child!”

“Yes, I promise,” he said; “but you are not going to die yet—say you are not going to die!”

“Poor little ones!” she replied. “Love for you keeps life in me. And this country is so sunny, the air so bracing, perhaps—”

“You make me love Touraine more than ever,” he said.

From that day, with Mme. Willemsens anticipating death and speaking to Louis of his future, he concentrated on his work, growing more industrious and less inclined to play. He coaxed Marie to read and abandon boisterous games; the hollow pathways, gardens and terraced walks of La Grenadiere grew quieter. They adapted their lives to their mother’s melancholy. Day by day her face grew paler; hollows gaped in her temples, and the lines in her forehead deepened.

August came. Five months at La Grenadiere had altered their life. The old servant Annette grew anxious and gloomy as she watched the mistress’s slow decline. Mme. Willemsens, beautiful still, was more careful of her appearance than ever; she adorned her wasted self and painted her cheeks. Often while walking the upper terrace with the children, Annette’s wrinkled face would peer from the savin trees by the pump; she would forget her work, stand with wet linen in her hands, scarcely able to hold back tears at seeing Mme. Willemsens so unlike the enchanting woman she had been.

The pretty house, once gay and bright, looked melancholy; it was very quiet, and the family seldom left it—the walk to the bridge was too much for Mme. Willemsens. Louis had almost identified himself with his mother; his growing imagination saw the weariness and exhaustion beneath the rouge, and he often found reasons for shorter walks.

Happy couples coming to Saint-Cyr, the Petite Courtille of Tours, and knots of evening strollers along the “dike” saw a pale, thin figure in black, a woman with a worn yet bright face, gliding like a shadow along the terraces. Great suffering cannot be concealed. The vinedresser’s household had grown quiet too. Sometimes the laborer and his family sat at the cottage door while Annette washed at the well, and Mme. Willemsens and the children sat in the summer-house in complete silence. Unknown to her, all eyes grew pitiful; she was so good, thoughtful, and dignified.

As autumn came with sun and grapes and healthful influences that might seem to prolong life despite disease, she saw no one but her children, taking from each hour as if it were her last.

Louis had worked at night without his mother’s knowledge and made immense progress from June to September. In algebra he reached equations with two unknowns; he studied descriptive geometry and drew admirably; he was prepared to sit for the École Polytechnique entrance exam.

Sometimes in the evening he went to the bridge of Tours. There he met a lieutenant on half-pay, an Imperial naval officer whose manly face, medal, and gait impressed the boy; the officer liked the lad’s lively eyes. The sailor had a friend in a colonel struck off the rolls too; Louis learned from them what life in camp or aboard a man-of-war was like. He plied these veterans with questions; resolved to face the hardships of their callings, he asked his mother’s leave for country walks as amusement. Mme. Willemsens, delighted, consented—the masters had warned she worked him too hard. So Louis took long walks, inuring himself to fatigue: he climbed tallest trees with incredible quickness, learned to swim, and kept watch through the night. He was no longer the same boy but already a young man, sunburned, with an expression of deep purpose.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When October came, Mme. Willemsens could only rise at noon. The reflected sunshine of the Loire warmed the air like the Bay of Naples, and physicians had recommended the place. At midday she sat beneath green leaves with her two boys, who never now left her. Lessons were over; mother and children wished to live heart to heart, free from outside cares. There were no outward outcries, only the elder boy lying in the grass beside her, basking in her eyes and kissing her feet, while restless little Marie gathered flowers and stood on tiptoe to press a girlish kiss to her lips. The pale woman, with great tired eyes and languid movements, smiled upon her healthy children—a sad, tender picture among yellow leaves and softened sunlight.

At last the doctor forbade Mme. Willemsens to leave her room. Every day flowers brightened it, and her children were always with her. One November day she sat at the piano for the last time. A Swiss landscape hung above the instrument; at the window she saw her children with their heads close together. She looked from them to the landscape, her face flushing; her fingers flew with passionate feeling over the ivory keys. It was her last great day, a private festival of memories. When the doctor came he ordered her to stay in bed. The alarming command was received in bewildered silence.

After he left she turned to the older boy. “Louis,” she said, “take me out on the terrace, so that I may see my country once more.” He gave her his arm and brought her out; but her eyes turned, perhaps unconsciously, to heaven rather than earth, and it was hard to say whether heaven or earth was fairer, for clouds traced shadowy outlines against the sky. Mme. Willemsens’ brows contracted violently; anguish and remorse showed in her face. She caught the children’s hands and clutched them to a heavily throbbing heart.

“‘Parentage unknown!’” she cried, with a look that pierced them. “Poor angels, what will become of you? And when you are twenty, what strict account may you not require of my life and your own?”

She pushed them away, leaned her arms on the balustrade, and stood hiding her face, fearful of all eyes. When she recovered she saw Louis and Marie kneeling on either side, watching her and smiling lovingly.

“If only I could take that smile with me!” she said, drying her eyes.

Then she went in and took to the bed she would leave only for her coffin.

A week passed, each day like the last. Old Annette and Louis sat with her in turn, never taking their eyes from the invalid. It was that dreadful time of listening to every breath lest it be the last, a dark stretch prolonged over days. On the fifth day the doctor interdicted flowers in the room: the illusions of life were going, one by one.

Marie and her brother felt their mother’s lips hot as fire beneath their kisses; and at last, on Saturday evening, she was too ill to bear the slightest sound, and her room was left in disorder. This neglect—so strange for a woman of refined taste—marked the beginning of the death-agony. After this, Louis refused to leave her. On Sunday night, in the deepest silence, when Louis thought she had grown drowsy, he saw a white, moist hand move the curtain in the lamplight.

“My son!” she said. Her tone was so solemn that the reaction in the boy was violent; an intense heat passed through his marrow.

“What is it, mother?”

“Listen! Tomorrow all will be over for me. We shall see each other no more. Tomorrow you will be a man, my child. So I must make arrangements, which must remain secret, known only to us. Take the key of my little table. That is it. Open the drawer. You will find two sealed papers to the left. There is the name of LOUIS on one, and on the other MARIE.”

“Here they are, mother.”

“Those are your certificates of birth, darling; you will want them. Give them to our poor old Annette to keep; ask her for them when you need them. Now,” she continued, “is there not another paper as well, something in my handwriting?”

“Yes, mother,” and Louis began to read, “Marie Willemsens, born at—”

“That is enough,” she broke in quickly, “do not go on. When I am dead, give that paper, too, to Annette, and tell her to send it to the registrar at Saint-Cyr; it will be needed if my death certificate is to be made out in due form. Now find writing materials for a letter which I will dictate.”

When he was ready the words came quietly:

“Monsieur le Comte, your wife, Lady Brandon, died at Saint-Cyr, near Tours, in the department of Indre-et-Loire. She forgave you.”

“Sign yourself—” she stopped, hesitating and perturbed.

“Are you feeling worse?” asked Louis.

“Put ‘Louis-Gaston,’” she went on.

She sighed, then continued. “Seal the letter, and direct it. To Lord Brandon, Brandon Square, Hyde Park, London, Angleterre.—That is right. When I am dead, post the letter in Tours, and prepay the postage.—Now,” she added after a pause, “take the little pocketbook you know, and come here, my dear child.... There are twelve thousand francs in it,” she said when Louis returned. “That is all your own. Oh me! you would have been better off if your father—”

“My father,” cried the boy, “where is he?”

“He is dead,” she said, placing a finger on her lips; “he died to save my honor and my life.”

She looked upward. If any tears remained, she would have wept from pain.

“Louis,” she continued, “swear to me, as I lie here, that you will forget all that you have written, all that I have told you.”

“Yes, mother.”

“Kiss me, dear angel.”

She was silent long; she seemed to draw strength from God and to measure her words by the life that remained.

“Listen,” she began. “Those twelve thousand francs are all you have. You must keep the money on you, because when I am dead the lawyers will come and seal everything. Nothing will be yours then, not even your mother. All that remains will be to go out, poor orphan children, God knows where. I have made Annette’s future secure. She will have an annuity of a hundred crowns, and she will stay at Tours no doubt. But what will you and your brother do?”

She raised herself and looked at the brave child standing by her bedside. Drops of perspiration stood on his forehead; he was pale with emotion and his eyes dim with tears.

“I have thought it over, mother,” he answered in a deep voice. “I will take Marie to the school here in Tours. I will give ten thousand francs to our old Annette and ask her to look after Marie. Then, with the remaining two thousand francs, I will go to Brest and go to sea as an apprentice. While Marie is at school, I will rise to be a lieutenant on board a man-of-war. There, after all, die in peace, my mother; I shall come back a rich man, and our little one shall go to the Ecole Polytechnique, and I will find a career to suit his bent.”

A gleam of joy shone in the dying woman’s eyes. Two tears fell on her fevered cheeks; a deep sigh escaped. The sudden joy of finding the father’s spirit in the son, who in an instant had become a man, almost killed her.

“Angel of heaven,” she cried, weeping, “by one word you have effaced all my sorrows. Ah! I can bear them.—This is my son,” she said, “I bore, I reared this man,” and she raised her hands as if in ecstasy, then lay back.

“Mother, your face is growing pale!” cried the lad.

“Someone must go for a priest,” she answered, with a dying voice.

Louis woke Annette, and the terrified old woman hurried to the parsonage at Saint-Cyr.

In the morning Mme. Willemsens received the sacrament amid touching surroundings. Her children knelt with Annette and the vinedresser’s family, simple folk already part of the household. The silver crucifix, carried by a chorister—a peasant child from the village—was lifted up, and the dying mother received the Viaticum from an aged priest. “This woman has suffered greatly!” the old cure said simply.

Marie heard no voices now, but her eyes remained fixed upon her children. Those about her listened in terror to each breath; it already came more slowly, though at intervals a deep sigh told them she still struggled; then at last it ceased. Everyone burst into tears except Marie, too young to understand death.

Annette and the vinedresser’s wife closed the eyes of the beloved woman, whose beauty shone in death. The women took possession of the chamber, removed furniture, wrapped the dead in her winding-sheet, and laid her upon the couch. They lit tapers and arranged the crucifix, sprigs of box, and holy-water stoup after the countryside custom, bolting shutters and drawing curtains. Later the curate came to pass the night in prayer with Louis, who refused to leave his mother. On Tuesday morning an old woman, two children, and a vinedresser’s wife followed the dead to her grave. These were the only mourners. Yet this was a woman whose wit, beauty, and charm had won European reputation; her funeral, had it been in London, would have been recorded in pompous paragraphs as an aristocratic rite—if she had not committed the sweetest of crimes, a crime always expiated in this world so that the pardoned spirit may enter heaven. Marie cried when they threw earth on his mother’s coffin; he understood that he should see her no more.

A simple wooden cross set over her grave bore this inscription, due to the cure of Saint-Cyr:

HERE LIES
AN UNHAPPY WOMAN,
WHO DIED AT THE AGE OF THIRTY-SIX.
KNOWN IN HEAVEN BY THE NAME OF AUGUSTA.
Pray for her!

When all was over, the children returned to La Grenadiere for a last look at home; then, hand in hand, they set out with Annette, leaving the vinedresser in charge, with directions to hand everything to the proper authorities.

At this moment Annette called Louis from the kitchen steps and took him aside. “Here is madame’s ring, Monsieur Louis.”

Seeing that vivid remembrance moved him so deeply he wept. He had not thought of this supreme piety, and flung his arms about the old woman’s neck. Then the three set out down the path, down the stone staircase, and so to Tours, without turning back.

“Mamma used to come there!” Marie said when they reached the bridge.

Annette had a relative, a retired dressmaker on the Rue de la Guerche, who took the children in. But Louis told her his plans, gave Marie’s certificate and ten thousand francs into her keeping, and the two went the next morning to take Marie to school.

Louis briefly explained his position to the headmaster and went. Marie accompanied him as far as the gateway. There Louis gave tender, solemn parting counsel, telling Marie he would now be alone in the world. He looked at his brother, put his arms about him, took one more long look, brushed a tear, and left, turning again and again until the last to see his brother standing in the school gateway.

A month later Louis-Gaston, now an apprentice on a man-of-war, left Rochefort harbor. Leaning over the bulwarks of the corvette Iris, he watched the coast of France recede until it became the faint blue of the horizon. In a little while he felt truly alone, lost on the wide ocean and in life.

“There is no need to cry, lad; there is a God for us all,” said an old sailor, with rough kindness.

The boy thanked him with pride. Then he bowed his head and resigned himself to a sailor’s life. He was a father.

ANGOULEME, August, 1832.

################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY #####
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

I have longed to tell a simple true story that should strike terror into two young lovers and drive them to take refuge in each other's hearts, as children cling at the sight of a snake. At the risk of appearing a coxcomb, I state my intention at the outset.

I myself played a part in this almost commonplace tragedy; so if it fails to interest you, the fault will be partly mine, partly due to historical veracity. Many things in real life are uninteresting; it is half of art to select from realities those with poetic possibilities.

In 1819 I was traveling from Paris to Moulins. My finances obliged me to take an outside place. For the first few miles I found many reasons to agree with Englishmen who prefer those airy perches. A young fellow, evidently better off, chose the seat beside me and listened with inoffensive smiles. Near age, similar ways of thinking, a shared love of fresh air and the rich landscape through which the coach lumbered, and an indefinable magnetism drew us before long into one of those short-lived travellers' intimacies in which we unbend because the intercourse is transient and makes no demands upon the future.

We had not gone thirty leagues before we were talking of women and love. Then, with the circumspection such matters require, we naturally proceeded to our lady-loves. Young as we both were, we still admired "the woman of a certain age," that is, the woman between thirty-five and forty. Oh! any poet who should have listened to our talk, for how many stages beyond Montargis, would have reaped a harvest of flaming epithets, rapturous description, and tender confidences. Our bashful fears, our silent interjections, our blushes, as we met each other's eyes, were eloquent with a boyish charm I have ceased to feel. One must remain young, no doubt, to understand youth.

We understood one another admirably on all the essential points of passion. We had laid it down as an axiom from the outset that there was no such driveling nonsense as a certificate of birth; that many women were younger at forty than girls of twenty; and, to come to the point, that a woman is no older than she looks.

This theory set no limits to the age of love, so we struck into a boundless sea. When we had portrayed our mistresses as young, charming, and devoted—women of rank, taste, intellect; endowed them with tiny feet and delicate, fragrant skin—then came the admission: on his part that Madame Such-an-one was thirty-eight, and on mine that I worshiped a woman of forty. Thereupon, as if released from some vague fear, our confidences came thick and fast when we found that we were in the same confraternity of love. It was which of us should overtop the other in sentiment.

One of us had traveled six hundred miles to see his mistress for an hour. The other, at the risk of being shot for a wolf, had prowled about her park one night. Out came all our follies in fact. If it is pleasant to remember past dangers, is it not at least as pleasant to recall past delights? We live through the joy a second time. We told each other everything—our perils, our great joys, our little pleasures, and even the humors of the situation. My friend's countess had lighted a cigar for him; mine made chocolate for me and wrote to me every day when we did not meet; his lady had come to spend three days with him at the risk of ruin to her reputation; mine had done even better, or worse, if you will. Our countesses, moreover, were adored by their husbands; these gentlemen were enslaved by the charm possessed by every woman who loves; and, with almost supererogatory simplicity, afforded us that sufficient spice of danger which increases pleasure. Ah! how quickly the wind swept away our talk and our happy laughter!

When we reached Pouilly, I scanned my new friend with much interest; it was not difficult to imagine him the hero of a very serious love affair. Picture a young man of middle height, well proportioned, a bright expressive face, dark hair, blue eyes, moist lips, and even white teeth. A certain not unbecoming pallor spread his delicately cut features, and faint dark circles about his eyes as if recovering from illness. Add that he had white, shapely hands, which he tended as a pretty woman should; that he seemed very well informed and decidedly clever, and you will see he was more than worthy of a countess. Indeed, many a girl might have wished for such a husband; he was a Vicomte with an income of twelve or fifteen thousand livres, "to say nothing of expectations."

About a league out of Pouilly the coach was overturned. My luckless comrade, thinking to save himself, jumped to the edge of a newly-ploughed field instead of following the vehicle and clinging tightly to the roof, as I did. He either miscalculated or slipped; how it happened I do not know, but the coach fell upon him, and he was crushed under it.

We carried him into a peasant's cottage, and there amid moans wrung from him by horrible sufferings he contrived to give me a commission—a sacred task, since it was laid on me by a dying man's last wish. Poor boy, all through his agony he tortured himself with the thought of the shock his mistress would feel when she read of his death in a newspaper. He begged me to go and break the news to her. He bade me look for a key he wore on a ribbon about his neck. I found it half buried in the flesh, but the dying boy did not utter a sound as I extricated it as gently as possible from the wound it had made. He had scarcely given me the necessary directions—I was to go to his home at La Charite-sur-Loire for his mistress' love-letters, which he conjured me to return to her—when he grew speechless in the middle of a sentence; but from his last gesture I understood the fatal key would be my passport in his mother's house. It troubled him that he was powerless to utter a single word to thank me, for he had no doubt of my wish to serve him. He looked wistfully at me, then his eyelids drooped in token of farewell, his head sank, and he died. His death was the only fatal accident caused by the overturn.

"But it was partly his own fault," the coachman said to me.

At La Charite I executed the poor fellow's dying wishes. His mother was away, which in a manner was fortunate for me. Nevertheless, I had to assuage the grief of an old woman-servant, who staggered back at the tidings of her young master's death and sank half-dead into a chair when she saw the blood-stained key. But I had another and more dreadful sorrow to think of—the sorrow of a woman who had lost her last love; so I left the old woman to her prosopopeia and carried off the precious correspondence, carefully sealed by my friend of the day.

The Countess' chateau lay some eight leagues beyond Moulins, and then there was distance to walk across country. So it was not exactly easy to deliver my message. For divers reasons into which I need not enter, I had barely money to take me to Moulins. However, youthful enthusiasm determined me to hasten thither on foot as fast as possible. Bad news travels swiftly, and I wished to be first at the chateau. I asked for the shortest way and hurried through the field paths of the Bourbonnais, bearing, as it were, a dead man on my back. The nearer I came to the Chateau de Montpersan, the more aghast I felt at my strange self-imposed pilgrimage. Vast numbers of romantic fancies ran through my head. I imagined all kinds of situations in which I might find this Comtesse de Montpersan, or, to observe the laws of romance, this Juliette, so passionately beloved of my traveling companion. I sketched ingenious answers to the questions she might put. At every turn of a wood, in every beaten pathway, I rehearsed a modern version of the scene in which Sosie describes the battle to his lantern. To my shame be it said, I had thought at first of nothing but the part I was to play, of my own cleverness, of how I should demean myself; but now that I was in the country, an ominous thought flashed through my soul like a thunderbolt tearing through gray cloud.

What an awful piece of news for a woman whose whole thoughts were full of her young lover, who was looking forward hour by hour to a joy which no words can express, a woman who had been at a world of pains to invent plausible pretexts to draw him to her side. Yet, after all, it was a cruel deed of charity to be the messenger of death! So I hurried on, splashing and bemiring myself in the byways of the Bourbonnais.

Before very long I reached a great chestnut avenue with a pile of buildings at the further end—the Chateau of Montpersan stood out against the sky like a mass of brown cloud, with sharp fantastic outlines. All the doors of the chateau stood open. This in itself disconcerted me, and routed all my plans; but I went in boldly, and in a moment found myself between a couple of dogs, barking as your true country-bred animals can bark. The sound brought out a hurrying servant-maid, who, when informed that I wished to speak to Mme. la Comtesse, waved a hand towards the masses of trees in the English park which wound about the chateau with "Madame is out there—"

"Many thanks," said I ironically. I might have wandered for a couple of hours in the park with her "out there" to guide me.

In the meantime, a pretty little girl, with curling hair, dressed in a white frock, a rose-colored sash, and a broad frill at the throat, had overheard or guessed the question and its answer. She gave me a glance and vanished, calling in shrill childish tones:

"Mother, here is a gentleman who wishes to speak to you!"

And, along the winding alleys, I followed the skipping, dancing white frill, a sort of will-o'-the-wisp that showed me the way among the trees.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
I must confess. I paused behind the last shrub in the avenue, adjusted my collar, brushed my shabby hat and trousers with my cuffs, dusted my coat and smoothed it once more. I buttoned my coat to show the least worn side, folded my trousers over my grass-stained boots. With this Gascon toilette I hoped the lady would not mistake me for the local rate collector; now I sometimes laugh at my precautions.

Suddenly, at a turning in the green walk among flowers lit by a ray of sunlight, I saw Juliette—Juliette and her husband. The little girl held her mother’s hand; the lady quickened her pace at the child’s ambiguous phrase. Taken aback, I bowed coolly, masking the disappointment I felt in a faint pout. My polished phrases fled me.

This hesitation allowed the husband to approach. A thousand thoughts flitted through my mind. To steady myself I made feeble inquiries—were they really M. le Comte and Mme. la Comtesse de Montpersan? These imbecilities bought time to form my conclusions at a glance and, with a perspicacity rare at that age, to study the pair whose solitude I was about to disturb.

The husband was a typical provincial nobleman. I noticed his big shoes first—more striking than a seedy coat, threadbare trousers, a flabby cravat, or a crumpled collar. There was something of a magistrate about him: the self-importance of a local mayor, the sour temper of an unsuccessful candidate. His face was wizened, wrinkled and sunburned; his scant hair sleekly gray. His character mixed homely sense with sheer silliness, a rich man’s overbearing ways and a lack of manners; led by his wife yet fancying himself master—domineering about trifles, careless of weightier matters.

But the Countess—what a contrast! Small, flat and graceful; so fragile you would fear to bruise her. She wore white muslin, a rose sash, and rose ribbons in her cap; her chemisette moulded to her shoulders and rounded contours, awakening an irresistible desire of possession. Her eyes were dark and expressive, her movements graceful, her foot charming. An experienced man of pleasure would not have given her more than thirty; her forehead was girlish. In character she recalled certain types fresh in any young man who had read Louvet.

In a moment I saw how things stood and took a diplomatic course creditable to an old ambassador. For once—perhaps the only time then—I used tact, showing the special skill of courtiers and men of the world. I had so many battles to fight later in life that such care for daily proprieties rarely had space in me.

“M. le Comte,” I said with an air of mystery, “I should like a few words with you,” and fell back a pace.

He followed. Juliette left us, going away unconcernedly, like a wife who knows she can learn her husband’s secrets whenever she chooses.

I told the Count briefly of the death of my traveling companion. His reaction convinced me his affection for his young collaborator was sincere, and this emboldened me.

“My wife will be in despair,” he cried; “I shall be obliged to break the news of this unhappy event with great caution.”

“Monsieur,” I said, “I addressed you first as duty required. I could not, without informing you, deliver a message to Mme. la Comtesse entrusted to me by a stranger; this commission is sacred, a secret I cannot dispose of. From the high idea he gave me of your character, I felt sure you would not oppose fulfilling a dying request. Mme. la Comtesse will be free to break the silence imposed on me.”

At this eulogy the Count inclined his head amiably, responded with a somewhat involved compliment, and left me a free field. We returned to the house. The bell rang, and I was invited to dinner. As we approached, Juliette stole a glance at us. Surprised to find her husband contriving a frivolous excuse to leave us together, she gave me that look only women can give—curiosity, disdain, involuntary tremors and alarms, the thought that an unexpected guest was tiresome when she had hoped for solitude with her love. I understood that mute eloquence and answered with a sad smile. I thought of her standing in the sunny alley with flowers on either hand; the picture rose before my eyes and I could not repress a sigh.

“Alas, madame, I have just made a very arduous journey—undertaken solely on your account.”

“Sir!”

“Oh! it is on behalf of one who calls you Juliette that I am come,” I continued. Her face grew white.

“You will not see him today.”

“Is he ill?” she asked, in a lower voice.

“Yes. But for pity’s sake, control yourself… He intrusted me with secrets that concern you, and you may be sure that never messenger could be more discreet nor more devoted than I.”

“What is the matter with him?”

“How if he loved you no longer?”

“Oh! that is impossible!” she cried; a faint smile broke over her face. Then a shudder ran through her; she reddened and asked with a wild glance, “Is he alive?”

Great God! What a terrible phrase. I was too young for that tone; I made no reply, only looked at the unhappy woman in helpless bewilderment.

“Monsieur, monsieur, give me an answer!” she cried.

“Yes, madame.”

“Is it true? Oh! tell me the truth; I can hear the truth. Tell me the truth! Any pain would be less keen than this suspense.”

I answered with two tears wrung from me by that voice. She leaned against a tree with a faint, sharp cry.

“Madame, here comes your husband!”

“Have I a husband?” and with those words she fled out of sight.

“Well,” cried the Count, “dinner is growing cold.—Come, monsieur.”

I followed him into the dining-room. Dinner was served with all the Parisian luxury. Five covers were laid: three for the Count and Countess and their child; mine, which should have been his; and another for the canon of Saint-Denis, who said grace and then asked, “Why, where can our dear Countess be?”

“Oh! she will be here directly,” said the Count. He helped himself to soup with portentous speed.

“Oh! nephew,” exclaimed the canon, “if your wife were here, you would behave more rationally.”

“Papa will make himself ill!” said the child, mischievous.

Shortly after, as the Count eagerly helped himself to venison, a housemaid entered: “We cannot find madame anywhere, sir!”

I sprang up; the dread showed plainly in my face, and the old canon followed me into the garden. The Count, for appearances’ sake, came partway, calling “Don't go, don't go! Don't trouble yourselves in the least,” but he did not offer to accompany us.

We three—the canon, the housemaid, and I—hurried through the garden and over the bowling-green, shouting, listening, growing more uneasy. As we hurried, I told the story of the fatal accident, and discovered how attached the maid was to her mistress—she took my dread more seriously than the canon. We searched by the pools and all over the park, but found no sign. At last we turned back, and under the walls of some outbuildings I heard a smothered, wailing cry, barely audible. It seemed to come from what might have been a granary. I went in at all risks, and there we found Juliette. With the instinct of despair she had buried herself deep in the hay, hiding her face to deaden her cries—pudency stronger than grief. She was sobbing and crying like a child, but with a more poignant, piteous sound. There was nothing left in the world for her. The maid pulled the hay from her; her mistress lay supine and listless as a dying animal. The maid could find nothing to say but, “There! madame; there, there—”

“What is the matter with her? What is it, niece?” the canon kept exclaiming.

With the girl's help I carried Juliette to her room, ordered that she not be disturbed and that everyone be told she had a sick headache. Then the canon and I came back to the dining-room.

Some time had passed; I had scarcely thought of the Count since we left him; his indifference had surprised me, and I was more amazed to find him seated philosophically at table. He had eaten almost the whole dinner, to his little daughter’s delight; the child smiled at her father’s flagrant breach of the Countess’ rules. His indifference was explained by a mild altercation with the canon: the Count was on a severe medical regimen and the ferocious hunger of a convalescent had overpowered all human sensibilities. In that short time I had seen human nature under two different aspects, with a grotesque element in the heart of a terrible tragedy.

The evening that followed was dreary. I was tired. The canon racked his brains for reasons for his niece’s tears. The Count silently digested his dinner; apparently content with the maid’s vague feminine explanation. We went early to bed.

As I passed the Countess’ door on the way to my room, I asked a servant timidly for news. She heard my voice and would have me come in, trying to speak, but could not utter a sound. She bowed her head, and I withdrew. Exhausted from my forced march and agitation, I slept.

Late in the night curtain rings grated. There sat the Countess at the foot of my bed; the lamp’s light fell full upon her face.

“Is it really true, monsieur, quite true?” she asked. “I do not know how I can live after that awful blow; but I feel calm now. I want to know everything.”

What calm! Her face was ghastly against her brown hair; those hours had bleached her; she had lost all womanly color. Her eyes were red and swollen; nothing of their beauty remained—only bitter, exceeding grief; it was as if a gray cloud covered where the sun had shone.

I told her the accident in a few words, without harrowing details. I spoke of our first day's journey, filled with recollections of her and of love. She listened eagerly, without tears, leaning toward me like a zealous doctor watching a patient’s change. When she seemed to have opened her whole heart to pain, deliberately plunging into misery with the frenzy of despair, I seized the moment and told her of the fears that had troubled the dying man and why he had given me that fatal message. Then her tears were dried by fires burning in the dark depths within her; she grew paler. When I drew the letters from beneath my pillow and offered them, she took them mechanically; then, trembling, she said in a hollow voice:

“And I burned all his letters!—I have nothing of him left!—Nothing! nothing!”

She struck her hand to her forehead.

“Madame—” I began.

She glanced at me in the convulsion of grief. “I cut this from his head, this lock of his hair.”

I gave her that last imperishable token that had been part of him she loved. If you had felt, as I did then, her burning tears on your hands, you would know what gratitude is when it follows so closely upon a kindness. Her eyes shone with a feverish glitter; a faint gleam of happiness lit her suffering as she grasped my hands and, choking, said:

“Ah! you love! May you be happy always. May you never lose her whom you love.”

She broke off and fled with her treasure.

Next morning that night-scene seemed like a dream; to make sure of the truth I looked under my pillow for the packet of letters and found nothing. There is no need to tell you how the next day went. I spent hours with Juliette, and in her lightest words and gestures I saw proof of the nobleness and delicacy of soul that made her one of those beloved, self-sacrificing natures so rare on earth.

In the evening the Comte de Montpersan came as far as Moulins with me. Embarrassed, he said:

“Monsieur, if it is not abusing your good-nature… would you, as you are going to Paris, have the goodness to remit a sum of money to M. de——, in the Rue du Sentier? I owe him an amount and he asked me to send it as soon as possible.”

“Willingly,” said I. In my innocence I took charge of a rouleau of twenty-five louis d’or, which paid my journey back to Paris; only when I arrived at the address to repay the amount did I understand the delicate art with which Juliette had obliged me. Was not all the genius of a loving woman revealed in such a way of lending, in her reticence about a poverty easily guessed?

And what rapture to have this adventure to tell to a woman who clung to you more closely in dread, saying, “Oh, my dear, not you! You must not die!”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

DEDICATION

To M. le Baron Barchou de Penhoen.

Of all our fellow pupils from the Oratorian school at Vendôme, I think we are the only two who have since met in mid-career as men of letters. When we met, you were engaged on German philosophy and I on this study. Neither of us has missed his vocation; and you, seeing your name here, will feel as much pleasure as I in my inscribing this work to you. Your old schoolfellow, 1840 De Balzac.

GOBSECK

It was one o’clock on a winter night in 1829–30 in the Vicomtesse de Grandlieu’s salon; two guests remained. A young man took his leave; as his carriage died away, the Vicomtesse turned to her daughter Camille, who pretended to watch a fire-screen while listening.

“Camille,” she said, “if you continue to treat young Comte de Restaud as you have this evening, I shall have to prevent him from visiting. Trust my love and let me guide you. At seventeen one cannot judge the past or the future or social standings. M. de Restaud has a mother, Mlle. Goriot, who squandered her father’s fortune and is of no birth. Though he behaves admirably toward her and his siblings, any family would hesitate to entrust their daughter’s future to him while she lives.”

Derville, a family friend who had overheard, interposed: “I have won, M. le Comte. I shall assist your niece.” On being challenged, he said he had read the Vicomtesse’s looks and settled by the fire. Camille’s uncle approached and the Vicomtesse sat between them.

“It’s time for a story that may change your view of Ernest de Restaud’s prospects,” Derville said.

The tale was meant for the Vicomtesse. By birth and fortune she led the Faubourg Saint-Germain; that an attorney could be intimate with her seemed odd until the facts were known. After returning from exile with the Royal family, the Vicomtesse had been reduced to a pension from Louis XVIII, intolerable to her pride. Her Hôtel de Grandlieu had been sold during the Revolution. Derville discovered defects in the title, pursued legal action, and reclaimed it. He obtained judgments restoring significant property and indemnities, raising her income to about sixty thousand francs. He did this without exploiting his new popularity: a man of integrity, he refused preferment to keep his practice and her confidence, becoming a close family adviser.

His management of her affairs won him esteem and many aristocratic clients, yet he declined political preferment. Since Ernest de Restaud’s arrival—and especially as Camille showed interest—Derville had visited the Hôtel de Grandlieu more often.

At a recent ball Derville had taunted Camille: “It’s a pity that young man doesn’t have two or three million francs, isn’t it?”

“Is it a pity? I don’t think so,” she replied. “M. de Restaud is capable and well educated. He will have as much money as he wishes when he comes into power.”

“But what if he were already rich?” Derville persisted.

“Rich already?” Camille blushed. “All the girls would be vying for him,” she said.

“Then Mlle. de Grandlieu might not be the one he favors,” he teased. Her blush betrayed her. Camille rose and left. Derville thought, “She loves him.”

Since that evening Camille had been unusually attentive to Derville, who approved of her feelings. Though grateful for his services, she showed respect rather than warmth; gratitude often weighs on the next generation, which may seek to escape it.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“This adventure,” Derville began, “recalls the one romantic episode of my life. It may surprise you that an attorney would speak of romance, yet at twenty-five, after seeing strange things, I met a man I shall describe—a usurer you have not known.

Imagine a sallow, wan face—what I would call lunar. His iron-gray hair was sleek and carefully combed; his features were impassive, his little yellow eyes without lashes peering from under a shabby cap as if afraid of light. Thin lips, a gimlet nose, and a low, suave voice that never rose made him look either prematurely aged or unnaturally preserved. His room matched him—clean but threadbare. In winter the fire smoldered in ashes, never flame. He moved with pendulum regularity, hoarding every sensation; his life flowed soundlessly like sands in an hourglass. Yet toward evening his money seemed to turn to a heart: when he finished his day’s business he would rub his hands; inward glee creased his face into a silent laugh. Even then he spoke in monosyllables with an inscrutable expression.

He was my neighbor in the Rue de Grès while I was a second clerk finishing my legal studies. The house was damp and dark; its gloom smothered even the liveliest spirits before they reached his door. He and his lodging were alike; the oyster resembled its rock. I was the only one he spoke to—borrowing a light or a book, sometimes permitting me to visit in the evenings. These small courtesies had grown out of four years of neighborly contact and my sober conduct. I lived nearly like him through lack of funds. No one knew if he had relatives or friends, whether he was rich or poor; I never saw money in his room—his capital was in the Bank. He collected debts himself, running all over Paris on skinny legs. Once, when gold fell from his pocket, another lodger returned it.

“That isn’t mine!” he cried, surprised. “If I were rich, would I live as I do?”

He made coffee on a cast-iron chafing dish; his dinners came from a cookshop, and the porter’s wife tidied his room faithfully. A whimsical chance named him Gobseck. Later, when I did business for him, I learned he was about seventy-six when we met—born about 1740 near Antwerp, son of a Dutch father and a Jewish mother. His full name was Jean-Esther Van Gobseck. When I mentioned the murder trial of La belle Hollandaise, he said without interest, “She is my grandniece.” That was the only remark he made about the death of his sole surviving relative, Sara Van Gobseck; when I asked why his grandniece bore his name, he smiled, “Women never marry in our family.” He had never cared to trace relatives beyond four generations of the female line. The thought of heirs disgusted him; the idea of his wealth passing to others after his death was intolerable.

Sent away at ten as a cabin boy to the Dutch Straits, he spent twenty years abroad. The inscrutable lines on his brow concealed adventures, panics, sudden luck, and squandered fortunes. He had known Admiral Simeuse, M. de Lally, Tippoo Sahib, notorious pirates in St. Thomas, had hunted treasure in Buenos Ayres, and observed the American War of Independence. Yet when he spoke of the Indies or America, he did so reluctantly, as if indiscreet. If sociability is a religion, Gobseck was an infidel; despite my efforts, his nature remained opaque.

Was he faithful to his mother’s religion? Had he converted? Did he view Gentiles as prey? I never learned his religious opinions; he appeared indifferent rather than unbelieving.

One evening I visited him. He sat motionless in his armchair, staring at the mantel as if reading his accounts. A lamp burned behind him, making his face paler. He gestured to a chair but said nothing. What could such a being think? Did he know God, feeling, women, happiness? I felt pity for him, like pity for a diseased creature. Yet I knew that while millions were at his command, he possessed the world in idea—having explored, ransacked, appraised, and exploited it.

“Good day, Daddy Gobseck,” I said.

He turned, contracting his bushy black eyebrows in an expression as expressive as a Southern smile. “You look as gloomy as when you heard that bookseller you admired had failed, though you were one of his victims.”

“One of his victims?” he repeated.

“Yes. You refused composition at the creditors’ meeting until he promised to pay you in full. He gave you bills accepted by the bankrupt firm. When he resumed business, he paid you the dividend on those bills.”

“He was sharp, but I got it out of him.”

“Do you have bills to protest? Today is the 30th, I believe.”

It was the first time I’d mentioned money to him. He looked at me ironically, then said blandly, “I am amusing myself.”

“So you amuse yourself now and again?”

“Do you think the only poets are those who print their verses?” he replied, pityingly.

I, knowing little of his life, thought, “Poetry in that head!” Then he surprised me.

“What life can be as glorious as mine?” he asked, eyes lighting. “You are young; your visions are colored by blood. You see women’s faces in the fire; I see coals. You have beliefs; I have none. Keep your illusions if you can. I’ll show you life with the discount taken off. You can go wherever you like or stay with your wife, but eventually you settle into a groove. Happiness consists in applying your faculties to realities. Anything more is false. My principles have varied with different men and latitudes. What we admire in Europe is punishable in Asia; a vice in Paris becomes a necessity elsewhere. There are no hard-and-fast rules, only conventions adapted to climate. Throw a man into various social melting pots and convictions become meaningless. The only constant is self-interest. If you lived as long as I have, you would know the only concrete reality worth caring about is GOLD. Gold means every form of human power. I’ve traveled and found hills or plains everywhere; place is irrelevant. Manners are universal; the battle between poor and rich is inevitable. It is better to exploit than be exploited. Pleasures are the same, and when sensations are exhausted, only Vanity remains. Vanity is satisfied only by gold. Our dreams need time and means to be realized; gold contains everything in embryo; it realizes all things for us.

“Only fools find pleasure in shuffling cards for a few pence. Only dupes think they’re useful making political rules amid unpredictable events. Only simpletons delight in discussing actors and repeating their sayings. What is all this but Parisian life? Let us seek a higher outlook: happiness in strong emotions that exhaust us or in methodical occupation that makes existence clockwork. A higher happiness is noble curiosity to learn Nature’s secrets, or to imitate Nature. In two words: Science and Art, or passion and calm. Every human passion, heightened by the struggle for existence, parades before me while I live in calm. As for scientific curiosity, I replace it with insight into the springs of action in man and woman. In short, the world is mine without effort and holds no claim on me. Listen,’ he continued, ‘I will tell you about my morning, and you’ll see my pleasures.”

He rose, bolted the door, drew a tapestry across it, and sat again.

“This morning,” he said, “I had only two amounts to collect; the rest of the bills due I gave away rather than cash yesterday. So much saved—when I discount a bill I always deduct two francs for a hired brougham; it’s absurd for my clients to make me trudge all over Paris for a few francs of discount when no man is my master, and I only pay seven francs in taxes.

“The first bill for a thousand francs was presented by a dandy—spangled waistcoat, eyeglass, tilbury, English horse. The bill bore the signature of one of the prettiest women in Paris, the wife of a Count, a great landowner. How did that Countess come to sign a bill of exchange, legally worthless but practically sound? Such women, poor things, fear the scandal of a protested bill and would rather pay than fail. I wanted to know what that bill truly meant—stupidity, imprudence, love, or charity?

“The second bill, signed ‘Fanny Malvaut,’ came from a linen-draper near bankruptcy. No one with bank credit comes to me. The first step to my door means despair; the news of failure will soon break, and he has been everywhere else first. When I see the stag at bay, a pack of creditors follows. The Countess lived in the Rue du Helder, and my Fanny in the Rue Montmartre. I made conjectures as I set out. If these two women could not pay, they would have more respect than their fathers. What tricks would the Countess try for a thousand francs? She would be so nice to me, speaking in that ingratiating tone of endorsers, pouring out coaxing words, while I...’ (he turned his pale eyes on me)—‘and I would remain unmoved, inexorable! So much for hypotheses. I reached the house.

“‘Madame la Comtesse is asleep,’ said the maid.

“‘When can I see her?’

“‘At twelve o’clock.’

“‘Is the Comtesse ill?’

“‘No, sir, but she returned home at three o’clock this morning from a ball.’

“‘My name is Gobseck; tell her I’ll call again at twelve,” I said, leaving muddy footprints on the carpet. I like leaving mud on a rich man’s carpet—not spitefully, but as a reminder of Necessity.
