The parish priest, connected to the Cruchots and allied with Monsieur de Bonfons, had been urged to speak to her about marriage; consumed by unhappiness, Eugénie told him she wished to withdraw from the world and live only for God. He warned against such a drastic retreat, reminding her of duties to society and the poor.

Madame des Grassins arrived, driven by vengeance, to reveal Charles’s return and engagement. Eugénie masked her shock as Madame des Grassins produced a letter detailing Charles’s situation and the marriage’s consequences. Tormented, Eugénie asked the priest whether remaining a virgin after marriage would be a sin, exposing her inner conflict. She secluded herself in her father’s secret room, skipped dinner, and avoided guests. Later she asked Monsieur de Bonfons—the president—to stay, an act taken as a pledge of marriage.

Privately she told the president she could give only friendship and asked him to settle her uncle’s debts in exchange for her hand. He eagerly accepted, hurried to Paris, met creditors, and arranged payment with interest. Carrying a letter from Eugénie to Charles explaining her decision and conditions, he informed Charles, who was at first excited but then shocked when he learned of Eugénie’s fortune; Madame d’Aubrion remained unaware.

The president returned to Saumur and announced his marriage to Eugénie. Though wealthy, she lived simply and devoted herself to charity. Her husband entered politics but died soon after being elected deputy. Widowed, Eugénie remained pious, dedicating her fortune to the community and rejecting worldly desires. Rumors linked her to the Marquis de Froidfond, but she stayed independent, supported by loyal servants Nanon and Cornoiller. Her story reveals the tension between wealth and personal fulfillment, showing her struggle to meet social expectations while staying true to her values.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
MIDDLE OF THE STORY — the central narrative section; the core middle prominently emphasized. MIDDLE OF THE STORY: central, repeated and emphasized again.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

For souls given to effusiveness there is a delightful hour between day and night; twilight softens and mingles light and shadow, favoring a dreamy mood that artists especially cherish. In that hour skill and technique dissolve into illusion: a painting seems alive—figures speak and move, flesh breathes, fabrics change sheen—and the artist, withdrawn from work, surrenders to meanings that flash upon the inner eye. Illusion, a kind of mental night peopled with dreams, lifts the soul above ordinary cares.

It was at this magic hour that a young painter, who saw art as an end in itself, perched on a step-ladder to finish a large canvas. Lost in meditative judgment and self-admiration, he lingered long. Night fell; whether by misstep or believing himself on the floor, he fell, struck his head on a footstool, and lay unconscious for an indeterminate time.

A sweet voice at last roused him. Blinded by a bright lamp, he heard whispering and felt timid hands support his head. As the mist cleared he saw, by the light of an old Argand lamp, the most charming face he had ever seen—an ideal beauty corresponding to the artist’s taste: features of delicate refinement, a virginal innocence like Prudhon and Girodet’s ideals; a slight, graceful figure and a modest, neat dress giving no clue of wealth or want. A handkerchief pressed to his forehead smelled faintly of ether, used to revive him; an elderly woman held the lamp and advised the girl.

“Monsieur,” the younger woman said when he questioned them while still dazed, “my mother and I heard a noise and thought we heard a groan. After the crash the silence alarmed us. Finding the key in the latch we entered and found you motionless. My mother fetched what was needed to bathe your head and revive you. You have cut your forehead—there. Do you feel it?”

“Yes, I do now,” he replied.

“Oh, it will be nothing,” said the elder woman. “Happily your head rested against this lay-figure.”

“I feel infinitely better,” the painter answered. “I need only a hackney cab to take me home. The porter’s wife will go for one.”

He tried to thank them, but the older lady interrupted with medical admonitions: “Tomorrow, monsieur, be careful—put on leeches, or be bled, and drink something healing. A fall may be dangerous.”

The young girl stole glances at him and at the pictures; she showed perfect propriety, a curiosity tempered by reserve, and an innocent interest in another’s suffering. They seemed to forget the canvases in his mishap, and when reassured they left, looking at him with anxious kindness—neither insistent nor familiar, free from indiscreet questions or invitations. Their noble simplicity made a quiet impression on the painter; later he would be struck by those details.

On the landing the old lady gently observed, “Adelaide, you left the door open.”

“That was to hear me fall,” he replied with a grateful smile.

“You came down just now, mother,” said the young girl, blushing.

“Would you like us to accompany you downstairs? The stairs are dark.”

“No, thank you, madame; I am much better.”

“Hold tightly by the rail.”

They stayed to light him to the stairhead and listened to his footsteps.

To appreciate the effect of this scene, it helps to know that only a few days earlier the painter, Hippolyte Schinner, had set up his studio in the attics of this house in the murkier part of the Rue de Suresnes, near the Rue des Champs-Élysées and almost opposite the Madeleine. Fame had recently come to him: at the last exhibition he was hailed as an unknown genius, acknowledged by artists and rewarded with the Cross of the Legion of Honor; dealers began to pay him well. He had spared himself long walks to cheaper outskirts studios and was now enjoying his “last privations.”

Hippolyte was attractive but reserved; he did not invite acquaintances easily and guarded his personal story. He had been raised by a needy mother, Mademoiselle Schinner, a farmer’s daughter from Alsace who had never married. Her gentleness had been crushed long ago by a rich man who betrayed her; she refused his alms, renounced society, and devoted herself to maternal love, working and saving a little treasure for her son. The sacrifices of those years were amply repaid when his talent brought him honor and income. Eager to restore to his mother the pleasures denied her, Hippolyte lived for her, selecting friends among the honorable and distinguished to raise his standing still further. His boyhood solitude had left him with delicate, poetic beliefs and shy, virginal hopes that marked his character and manners: polite, modest, with a voice that stirred noble sentiments and a presence that attracted without being explainable.

These traits explain why, when he later sent the porter to fetch a cab, Hippolyte did not press the porter’s wife for information about the two women who had tended him. He answered her chatter but let it go. “They were, no doubt, Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, who have lived here four years,” she said. “We do not know exactly what they do; in the mornings an old deaf woman helps them. In the evenings two or three elderly gentlemen with ribbon loops come to see them and stay late. One arrives by carriage with servants; they say he has sixty thousand francs a year. However, they are quiet—economical—and pay promptly. It’s queer the mother’s name is not the same as the daughter’s. When they go to the Tuileries mademoiselle is very smart, always followed by young men, though she shuts the door in their faces.” The cab came; Hippolyte went home.

His mother dressed his wound and forbade his return to the studio the next day. During three enforced days at home his memory retrieved the scene of his fainting: the young girl’s profile against the dark, the mother’s faded face, Adelaide’s touch, a graceful gesture, a tone of voice—details rising like objects from deep water. When he could work again he went early to his studio, but his real motive was to visit his neighbors. Passion, when it first awakens, gives a certain immediate delight: he climbed to the fourth-floor door of Mademoiselle Leseigneur slowly, his heart beating at the thought of calling. He felt a similarity between their positions and imagined misfortunes akin to his own. After thinking, dining in his studio, he went to call at seven.

Parisian life often shows dissonances between appearance and comfort: fresh toilettes and brilliant women housed in rooms that betray doubtful means. Hip-polyte’s neighbors’ rooms influenced his feelings and hopes.

The house belonged to a proprietor who hated repairs: chimneys were left faulty, woodwork grimy and stained. Hippolyte, who had seen such frugality before, expected these signs and saw their poetical aspect. Mademoiselle Leseigneur opened the door. Recognizing him she bowed and, with Parisian tact, turned to shut a glass partition that hid laundry and a cramped household: a camp-bed, irons, a filter, kitchen crockery—utensils of a simple domestic life. Muslin curtains screened the clutter from a window overlooking a yard.

With an artist’s eye Hippolyte took in the arrangement. The room visible was divided: an ante-room/dining-room hung with patched salmon-rose paper and a faded flock border; in the center, a massive mahogany table worn at the edges; a small stove and a blocked hearth by a cupboard; carved chairs with red morocco seats, gilt nails showing former splendor. The room was a museum of the odd mingling of luxury and penury: a finely finished telescope hung over a discolored glass; a painted sideboard mimicked mahogany; slippery red quarry tiles and shabby rugs; everything hard-cleaned in a way that emphasized its age. An indescribable odor rose from the lumber-room and kitchen, while the partly open window let in dusty street air.

Adelaide opened an inner door and received him with obvious pleasure. Hippolyte, whose memories of his mother’s poverty were vivid, entered with sympathy. The old lady rose from an antique armchair and offered him a seat.

“Well, monsieur, I hope you no longer feel the effects of your fall,” she said.

“No, madame. I have come to thank you for the kind care you gave me, and above all mademoiselle, who heard me fall.”

As Hippolyte spoke with the awkwardness of first love he looked at the girl. Adelaide lit the Argand lamp, replacing a tallow candle, placed the lamp on the chimney shelf, and sat a little behind him to look at him at ease while pretending interest in the lamp. The flame sputtered in the damp chimney. A mirror on the chimney-piece reflected her, and she and the painter exchanged restrained glances, each embarrassed by the stratagem.

While talking with Madame Leseigneur, he observed the room more closely. Egyptian figures on iron fire-dogs were barely visible under cinders; two brands struggled before a sham log; a faded Aubusson carpet left the tiled floor cold. The walls were papered in a reddish imitation of figured silk; a crack outlined double doors closing a recess where Madame Leseigneur probably slept, hidden by a sofa. Above a chest of drawers hung a dim portrait of an officer; a malachite tray with a dozen painted coffee cups—perhaps Sèvres—stood on the chest. On the chimney-shelf an Empire clock—an equestrian charioteer with four horses—marked the hours; tall candlesticks bore smoke-yellowed tapers; dusty artificial flowers crowned porcelain vases.

A card-table with new packs at the center suggested either these women were industrious and honest or supported by intrigue and gambling. But Adelaide’s pure demeanor persuaded Schinner of her innocence; he preferred to ascribe the furniture’s dissonance to honorable causes.

“My dear,” the old lady said to the young one, “I am cold; make a little fire, and give me my shawl.”

Adelaide wrapped a once-costly, now-faded and darned cashmere around her mother and fetched small bundles of wood from the lumber-room to revive the fire.

Their conversation that followed was tactful and cautious. Hippolyte, mindful that pity can embarrass, avoided blunt inquiries. Women are skilled at easing the awkwardness of a first call: they asked about painting, prompting him to speak—and as they talked, his reflections and habits came out. The old lady’s face, worn by trouble but retaining an outline of refined grace and shrewd eyes, suggested the old court’s genteel bearing; she could hint either at artifice or delicacy. A woman’s face always conceals the line between frankness and duplicity; only intuition discerns it. At Schinner’s age the first impulse is to believe in goodness. Looking at Adelaide’s noble brow and soulful eyes, he sensed modest virtue. He seized the opportunity to discuss portraits and inspect a faded pastel whose chalk had faded.

“You are attached to that picture for the likeness, no doubt, mesdames, for the drawing is poor?” he said.

“It was done at Calcutta, in great haste,” replied the mother, agitated.

She looked upon the formless sketch with the absorption of one who cherishes a memory; regret showed in her face. The painter read in her attitude the mark of a once-happy life and sat down beside her.

“Madame,” he said, “in a short time the colors of that pastel will disappear. The portrait will survive only in your memory. May I reproduce the likeness on canvas? It will endure. Grant me, as a neighborly favor, the pleasure of doing you this service. Sometimes an artist enjoys a respite from greater undertakings by doing work of less lofty pretension; it will be a recreation.”

The old lady flushed at the offer; Adelaide’s glance showed deep feeling. Hippolyte wanted a tie to these neighbors, a right to mingle in their life. His proposal appealed to maternal affection, gratified his pride as an artist, and could not offend. Madame Leseigneur accepted with the composed dignity of a noble soul, aware of the delicate nature of such an obligation.

“I fancy,” the painter continued, “that the uniform is that of a naval officer.”

“Yes,” she said. “That was a captain in command. Monsieur de Rouville—my husband—died at Batavia from a wound received in a fight with an English ship off the Asiatic coast. He commanded a frigate of fifty-six guns; the Revenge carried ninety-six. The struggle was unequal, but he defended his ship bravely, held out till nightfall and got away. When I returned to France Bonaparte had not yet come to power, and I was refused a pension. When I applied again, quite lately, I was sternly told that if the Baron de Rouville had emigrated I should not have lost him; that by this time he would have been a rear-admiral; and finally his Excellency quoted degrees of forfeiture. I took the step, urged by friends, only for Adelaide’s sake. I have always hated holding out my hand as a beggar in the name of a grief that silences a woman’s voice and strength. I do not like this money valuation for blood irreparably spilt—”

“Dear mother, this subject always does you harm,” Adelaide interrupted, and the Baronne bowed in silence.

“Monsieur,” the young girl observed softly, “I had supposed a painter’s work was generally fairly quiet?” Schinner colored, remembering the noise he had made.

Their conversation was interrupted by the sound of a carriage stopping at the door. Adelaide went into her room and returned with a pair of tall gilt candlesticks with partly burnt wax candles, which she lit. Setting the lamp down, she opened the outer door. The sound of a kiss exchanged touched Hippolyte’s heart; he was impatient to see the man who treated Adelaide familiarly. The newcomers conversed in undertone with her.

At last Mademoiselle de Rouville returned, followed by two men whose costumes and appearance suggested tales of their own. The first, about sixty, wore one of those coats of the Louis XVIII. period—neither fully civil nor military—embroidered with fleurs-de-lis and gilt buttons; shoulder straps hinted at epaulettes, and his powdered hair showed no hat-crease. He might be taken for fifty and seemed robust. His frank, loyal émigré expression hinted also at libertine habits, a reckless gallantry like that of the Musketeers. His gestures and manner showed no intention of renouncing royalism, religion, or gallantry.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A really fantastic figure stood behind this specimen of “Louis XIV.’s light infantry”—a nickname the Bonapartists gave these venerable survivors of the Monarchy. To do it justice it ought to be the principal object in the picture, and yet it remained only an accessory. Imagine a lean, dry man, dressed like the first, but seeming only his reflection or his shadow. The coat, new on the one, on the other was old; the powder in his hair looked less white, the fleurs-de-lis less bright, the shoulder straps more hopeless and dog’s-eared; his intellect seemed feebler, his life nearer the end. In short, he realized Rivarol’s witticism on Champcenetz—“He is the moonlight of me.” He was simply the double of the old gallant, paler and poorer, the difference between first and last impressions of a lithograph.

This speechless old man—the Chevalier—was a mystery to the painter and always remained one. He did not speak; nobody spoke to him. Was he friend, poor relation, or the elderly companion who followed a gallant like a lady follows an older woman? Did he fill a place midway between dog, parrot, and friend? Had he saved his patron’s fortune or only his life? Was he a Trim to another Captain Toby? At the Baronne de Rouville’s he provoked curiosity without satisfying it. Who, after the Restoration, could recall the attachment that had once bound this man to his friend’s wife, twenty years dead?

The leader, least dilapidated of these wrecks, came gallantly to Madame de Rouville, kissed her hand, and sat by her. The other bowed and placed himself not far from his model, two chairs apart. Adelaide leaned her elbows on the back of the old gentleman’s armchair, unconsciously imitating the pose Guerin gave Dido’s sister.

Though the gentleman’s familiarity was paternal, his freedom seemed for a moment to annoy the young girl.

“What, are you sulky with me?” he said.

Then he shot at Schinner one of those side-glances full of shrewdness and discreet curiosity that ask, when they see a stranger, “Is he one of us?”

“This is our neighbor,” said the old lady, pointing to Hippolyte. “Monsieur is a celebrated painter; his name must be known to you despite your indifference to the arts.”

The old man read the Baronne’s mischievous intent in suppressing the name and bowed.

“Certainly,” he said. “I heard much about his pictures at the last Salon. Talent has immense privileges,” he added, observing the artist’s red ribbon. “That distinction, which we must earn by our service and blood, you gain in youth; but all glory is of the kindred,” he said, laying his hand on his Cross of Saint-Louis.

Hippolyte murmured acknowledgment and fell silent again, satisfied to admire the beautiful girl’s head that charmed him. He soon forgot the misery of the dwelling; to him Adelaide’s face stood out against a luminous atmosphere. He replied briefly to questions, as if some double consciousness allowed him to listen without attending; his mind overflowed with hope and visions of happiness.

After a short time he perceived the old lady and her daughter playing cards with the old gentleman. The silent Chevalier stood behind his friend’s chair, watching the game, answering mute inquiries by approving nods and repeating gestures.

“Du Halga, I always lose,” said the gentleman.

“You discard badly,” replied the Baronne.

“For three months now I have never won a single game,” he persisted.

“Have you the aces?” asked the old lady.

“Yes, one more to mark,” he said.

“Shall I come and advise you?” Adelaide offered.

“No, no. Stay where I can see you. By Gad, it would be losing too much not to have you to look at!”

At last the game ended. The gentleman threw two louis d’or on the table, not without temper. “Forty francs,” he cried. “Deuce take it! It is eleven o’clock.”

“It is eleven o’clock,” repeated the silent figure, looking at the painter. Hearing this, Hippolyte thought it time to retire. He found a few commonplace remarks, took leave of the Baroness, her daughter, and the two strangers, and went away wholly possessed by the first raptures of true love, without attempting to analyze the evening’s little incidents.

Next day the painter ardently desired to see Adelaide. He waited until afternoon, then blushing like a girl went downstairs to ask Mademoiselle Leseigneur to let him have the portrait of the Baron. Adelaide, who had heard him come down from the studio, summoned him in.

He followed, bashful and out of countenance; mere proximity to her was ecstasy. The old lady was not in the drawing-room. Adelaide brought a chair to stand on to take down the picture but found she could not unhook it without setting her foot on the chest of drawers; blushing, she asked Hippolyte, “I am not tall enough. Will you get it down?”—a modesty that meant more than the request. He read her soul and gave her a look of understanding that was the sweetest language of love. Adelaide dropped her eyes in maiden reserve. Hippolyte, shy, took down the picture, examined it, carried it to the window, and went away, saying only, “I will return it soon.”

In that brief moment they underwent one of those storms of agitation whose effects are like a stone flung into a deep lake—reveries that rise and follow each other, titillating the heart with repeated ripples.

Hippolyte returned to his studio bearing the portrait. His easel was ready with a fresh canvas. Till dinner he worked with the ardor artists put into whims. He went again that evening and remained from nine till eleven. The two old men arrived at the same hour, the same piquet was played, the same speeches made, Adelaide’s friend lost as much as before; Hippolyte, a little bolder, chatted with the young girl.

A week passed. In it the painter’s and Adelaide’s feelings underwent slow, delightful transformations that brought their souls to perfect understanding. Each day the look with which she welcomed him grew more intimate, more confiding, gayer; her voice and manner more eager and familiar. They laughed and talked, telling their thoughts with the artless trust of two children who have been friends for years. Schinner wished to be taught piquet, made blunders, and, like the old man, lost every game. Without speaking of love, the lovers knew they were all in all to each other. Hippolyte enjoyed his power over his gentle friend; Adelaide, timid and devoted, surrendered in small ways—false fits of temper and caprices that lovers and guileless girls play—so that familiarity between the girl and the old Count soon ceased. She understood the painter’s melancholy from the abrupt tone of his few words when the old man kissed her hands or throat.

Mademoiselle Leseigneur, for her part, expected a full account of the painter’s actions; she scolded him for absences and the painter had to give up seeing other friends. Adelaide’s natural jealousy showed when she heard he sometimes, at eleven, had visits in brilliant drawing-rooms. She told him such a life could not be proof of warm affection. Thus he came, as much from the tyranny of his passion as from the demands of a girl in love, to live almost exclusively in the little apartment where everything attracted him.

Never was there purer love. On both sides the same trustfulness and delicacy increased their passion without the usual sacrifices people use to prove affection. Between them a spontaneous affinity united their souls; two months after meeting their lives were one. From morning Adelaide knew when his steps were overhead. When he dined at his mother’s he still looked in on his neighbors; in the evening he flew there with lover’s punctuality. Adelaide tasted unmixed happiness, seeing the realization of the ideal natural to her age.

The old gentleman now came more rarely; Hippolyte, once jealous, had taken his place at the green table, sharing his constant ill luck. Yet, in the midst of happiness thoughts of Madame de Rouville’s disastrous poverty haunted him. Several times he mused, “Strange! twenty francs every evening?” and dared not confess to himself his ugly suspicions.

He spent two months on the portrait; when finished and framed, he considered it one of his best works. Madame la Baronne de Rouville had never spoken of it again. Was this indifference or pride? The painter would not analyze her silence. He plotted with Adelaide to hang the picture when the Baronne was out. One day during her usual walk in the Tuileries Adelaide came to the studio to see the portrait in its good light. She stood speechless, in ecstatic contemplation; a woman’s admiration for the man she loves suffused her. When the painter leaned forward, she held out her hand; two tears fell. Hippolyte took her hand, covered it with kisses; for a minute they looked at each other in silence, both longing to confess but not daring. The painter kept her hand, their hearts beating wildly. Adelaide, too agitated, gently drew away and said simply, “You will make my mother very happy.”

“What, only your mother?” he asked.

“Oh, I am too happy.”

The painter bent his head, frightened by the vehemence of feelings her tones stirred. Both, aware of perils, went downstairs and hung the picture in its place. Hippolyte dined for the first time with the Baroness, who, overcome and in tears, embraced him.

In the evening the old emigre, the Baron de Rouville’s comrade, paid a visit to announce he had been promoted to vice-admiral; his land voyages were counted as naval campaigns. Seeing the portrait he shook the painter’s hand, “By Gad! though my old hulk does not deserve to be perpetuated, I would give five hundred pistoles to see myself as like that is to my dear old Rouville.”

At the hint the Baroness glanced at her young friend and smiled with sudden gratitude. Hippolyte suspected the admiral meant to offer the price of both portraits while paying for his own. Pride and jealousy offended, and he replied, “Monsieur, if I were a portrait-painter I should not have done this one.”

The admiral bit his lip and sat down to cards. The painter remained near Adelaide, who proposed a dozen hands of piquet, to which he agreed. As he played he observed an excitement in Madame de Rouville unusual for her—an ardent desire to win, a keen joy in fingering the old gentleman’s gold. Evil suspicions returned: could Madame de Rouville live by gambling? Was she playing to pay a debt or rent? The old man seemed shrewd, keeping his money from being taken. What interest attracted him, who was rich, to this fourth-floor house where he lost some forty francs every evening? Why had he given up the liberties he once enjoyed with Adelaide?

These reflections made Hippolyte watch the old man and the Baroness; their looks toward Adelaide displeased him. “Am I being duped?” he thought—horrible. He resolved to stay after the old men left to confirm or dispel his suspicions. He drew his purse to pay Adelaide but, carried away by tormenting thoughts, laid it on the table and fell into reverie. Ashamed of his silence, he rose, answered a commonplace question, and moved closer to the Baronne to examine her withered features while he talked.

On leaving he felt dizzy; he had gone only a few steps when he turned back for the forgotten purse. “I left my purse here!” he said.

“No,” Adelaide said, reddening.

“I thought it was there,” he said, pointing to the card-table. Not finding it, in shame for Adelaide and the Baroness, he looked at them with amazement that made them laugh. He felt in his pockets: not there. He searched himself but did not find the purse. His memory retraced the fatal truth: he remembered the purse on the green cloth. The theft was blatant and denied with effrontery; Hippolyte no longer felt doubt as to his neighbors’ morals. His knees shook, he found himself unable to walk, struggling with the shock caused by the destruction of all his hopes. He recalled many trifling observations that now corroborated his suspicions; these suggested the character and life of the women.

Had they waited till the portrait was given them before robbing him? If so, the theft was odious. He thought perhaps the admiral had reasons for not marrying Adelaide, and the Baroness had tried to make gains—then checked himself. “If the Baroness hoped to get me to marry her daughter, they would not have robbed me,” he thought.

Clinging to illusions, he tried to find a justification in accident: “The purse must have fallen; perhaps I left it on my chair.” He searched his person but did not find it. He blamed Adelaide in his heart, yet could not admit she was base. The wretched rooms rose before him, cleansed of the poetry of love; they appeared dirty and faded, emblematic of an inner life devoid of honor, idle and vicious. Our feelings, he thought, are written upon the things about us.

Next morning he rose without having slept. Heartache had made rapid inroads. To lose a future one has dreamed of is keener than losing known happiness; hope is better than memory. The painter walked under the Tuileries’ fresh shade, absorbed.

There he met Francois Souchet, a schoolfellow and sculptor who had won the first prize and was bound for Italy.

“Why, Hippolyte, what ails you?” asked Souchet.

“I am most unhappy,” replied Hippolyte gravely.

“Nothing but a love affair can cause you grief. Money, glory, respect—you lack nothing.”

Led into confidence, Hippolyte told of his love. On hearing the Rue de Suresnes and the fourth-floor girl, Souchet laughed. “Stop. A little girl I see every morning at the Church of the Assumption; we all know her. The mother is a Baroness. Do you really believe a Baroness lives up four flights of stairs? Brrr! You are a relic of the golden age! Her face, her appearance tell everything. Have you not known her for what she is by the way she holds her bag?”

Others joined them. The sculptor repeated Hippolyte’s adventure, and jests followed—mockery common among artists. The painter’s secret, he felt, was raked through with careless laughter. Reticence made him uncomfortable as his passion was lightly handled. He pretended contradiction and asked for proofs; the jokes resumed.

“But, my dear boy, have you seen the Baroness’ shawl?” said Souchet. “Have you followed the girl to church?” asked Bridau. “The mother has a certain gray gown,” Bixiou observed.

“Come at four o’clock and study their walk,” Souchet advised. “If then you still have doubts—well, you would marry your porter’s daughter.” Torn, Hippolyte parted from his friends. He thought Adelaide and her mother must be superior to these accusations and felt remorse for having suspected them. Passing their door, he felt a pain no man can misread. He loved Adelaide so passionately that, despite the theft, he still worshiped her. He felt like Des Grieux, ready to hold his mistress pure even on the cart that bears ruined women to prison. “Why should not my love keep her the purest of women? Why abandon her to evil without trying to rescue her?”

The idea of such a mission pleased him. Love makes gains of everything; nothing tempts a young man more than to act as a good genius to a woman. There is romance in this devotion—it is the loftiest stretch of submission and service.

Hippolyte sat in his studio gazing at his picture through tears, holding his brush but touching nothing. Night fell; he met the old admiral on the stairs, looked darkly at his bow, and fled. He had intended to call on the ladies, but the sight of Adelaide’s protector froze him. For the hundredth time he wondered why this prodigal, with eighty thousand francs a year, came to this fourth story to lose about forty francs every evening; he thought he could guess.

The next days he threw himself into work to conquer passion by sudden composition. He half succeeded; study consoled him but could not smother memories of tender hours.

One evening, as he left his studio, the ladies’ door lay half open. Somebody stood in the window’s recess and the staircase made it impossible for the painter to pass unseen. He bowed coldly, with supreme indifference—but imagining the bitterness such scorn would cause in a loving heart, he shuddered. To crown a feast of mutual delight with eight days of disdain would be tragic. Perhaps the purse had been found, perhaps Adelaide had looked for him nightly. This simple thought filled him with remorse; ashamed of resisting his heart for a week, almost criminal, he called that evening.

All his suspicions vanished at the sight of Adelaide, who had grown pale and thin.

“Good heavens! what is the matter?” he asked after greeting the Baroness.

Adelaide made no reply, but gave a look of deep melancholy that pained him.

“You have, no doubt, been working hard,” said the old lady. “You are altered. We are the cause of your seclusion. That portrait delayed some pictures essential to your reputation.”

Hippolyte welcomed the excuse. “Yes. I have been busy, but I have been suffering—” he began.

At the words Adelaide raised her head; her anxious eyes showed no reproach.

“You must have thought us quite indifferent to any good or ill that may befall you?” asked the Baroness.

“I was wrong,” he replied. “Still, there are forms of pain we know not how to confide, even to a friendship older perhaps than yours.”

“The sincerity and strength of friendship are not measured by time. I have seen old friends who had not a tear to bestow on misfortune,” said the Baronne sadly.

“But you—what ails you?” Hippolyte asked Adelaide.

“Oh, nothing,” the Baroness answered. “Adelaide has sat up late finishing some little piece of woman’s work; she would not listen to me when I said a day more or less did not matter—”

Hippolyte was not listening. Seeing their noble, calm faces he blushed for his suspicions and ascribed the purse’s loss to some unknown accident. The evening was delicious. There are secrets young souls understand; Adelaide read Hippolyte’s thoughts though he did not confess. He returned more in love and affectionate, seeking tacit forgiveness. This concord was disturbed, however, by a little remark of the Baronne.

“Let us have our little game,” she said, “for my old friend Kergarouet will not let me off.”

These words revived the painter’s fears; he colored at the Baronne’s look, but saw only frank good-nature; nothing perfidious clouded it.

He sat at the card-table; Adelaide took his side, saying he did not know piquet and needed a partner. Throughout the game Madame de Rouville and her daughter exchanged looks of intelligence, which alarmed Hippolyte the more because he was winning; at last a final hand left the lovers in the old lady’s debt.

Feeling in his pocket for money Hippolyte took his hands off the table and saw before him a purse Adelaide had slipped in front of him without his noticing. The poor child had the old one in her hand, and—looking into it for the money to pay her mother—held it to him. The blood rushed to his heart; he nearly fainted.

The new purse, substituted for his own and containing his fifteen gold louis, was worked with gilt beads. The rings and tassels showed Adelaide’s taste; she had no doubt spent her little hoard on ornamenting this pretty work. It was a delicate way to say that the painter’s gift could only be repaid by some proof of affection.

Overcome with happiness, Hippolyte turned to look at Adelaide and her mother; they trembled with delight at their little ruse. He felt mean and foolish; tears rose. By an irresistible impulse he sprang up, clasped Adelaide, pressed her to his heart, and stole a kiss; then, with an artist’s hearty frankness, “I ask for her for my wife!” he exclaimed to the Baroness.

Adelaide looked at him with half-wrathful eyes; Madame de Rouville, surprised, was considering her reply when the bell rang. The old vice-admiral entered, followed by his shadow and Madame Schinner—Hippolyte’s mother. Having guessed the cause of the grief her son vainly concealed, Madame Schinner, alarmed by calumnies about Adelaide, sought out the admiral. He declared he would crop all scoundrels’ ears for slandering her.

Prompted by indignation, he explained to Madame Schinner the secret of his losing intentionally at cards; the Baronne’s pride left him no other ingenious means of assisting her.

After Madame Schinner paid her respects, the Baroness looked round—at the Comte de Kergarouet, the Chevalier du Halga, at Hippolyte and Adelaide—and, with the simple grace that comes from the heart, said, “So we are a family party this evening.”

PARIS, May 1832

To Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:

Dear Child,—You, the joy of the household, whose pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of Wierzschovnia, how can I dedicate such a melancholy story to you? Yet sorrows should be spoken of to a young girl like you, since the day may come when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them. It is difficult to find a subject worthy of your eyes, so I have no choice; perhaps you will feel fortunate when you read this story sent to you by your old friend, De Balzac.

PIERRETTE

I. THE LORRAINS

On an October morning in 1827 a sixteen-year-old boy in modest clothes stood in a small square of Lower Provins, watching mills and simple bourgeois houses under linden trees. He noticed a recently restored white-stone dwelling with gray blinds and yellow iron balconies—a neatness that spoke of a retired petty shopkeeper.

As the sun lit a calico curtain in a garret window, the boy began a Breton ditty for newlyweds. A girl, Pierrette, signaled from the window; he offered a yellow broom-flower. He was Brigaut, her childhood companion, and told her he planned to settle nearby. Alarmed, Pierrette urged him to flee when a grotesque old maid below—sharp-eyed and prone to imagining scandal—appeared.

Overcome by Brigaut’s visit, Pierrette reflected on her past. Seated in a shabby armchair, frail and thoughtful, she recalled childhood pleasures shaded by illness and uncertainty. Her mother, a Demoiselle Auffray from a tangled family—the father had married twice and left a complicated inheritance—later married Major Lorrain, a Breton officer who died in battle, leaving them impoverished; the Lorrains could not provide for Pierrette.

She was eventually sent to live with her uncle and aunt, the Rogrons, in Provins. Unaware of all the intricacies of her lineage and her parents’ fortunes and losses, Pierrette’s fate becomes increasingly shaped by these relatives—the Rogrons’ control setting the stage for the drama to follow.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Père Rogron, an indulgent, ruddy-faced innkeeper in Provins, and his unattractive, selfish wife scraped by on their land and inn until inheriting a dilapidated house from old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandmother. Their children, Sylvie and Jérôme-Denis, were neglected and ill-bred; Rogron sent them to Paris to work in shops with little guidance.

In Paris Sylvie apprenticed on rue Saint-Denis and became a forewoman; Jérôme rose to head-clerk. They rarely saw each other but pooled savings to buy the retail “Family Sister,” achieving modest success. Severe and competitive, Sylvie ran the business shrewdly though plainly; Jérôme proved ill-suited to broader trade. Obsessed with the shop, stingy and harsh to clerks, they were pleasant to customers but sour at home.

While mourning their father they learned of cousin Pierrette’s inheritance. To protect their wealth they refused to take Pierrette in, delayed replying, and returned to Provins four years before Brigaut arrived.

Back in Provins their Parisian airs quickly faded as provincial norms reasserted themselves. The city’s vibrancy yielded to local stillness. With new means they took up travel, politics, hunting, and remodeled their house to mimic wealthier families, trying to join the town’s circle led by the Julliards, Guepins, and Guenées. Despite lavish dinners and ostentation—Madame Tiphaine promoted her husband—the Rogrons lacked polish. Invitations dwindled and ridicule spread. Sylvie’s belief that money ensured status collapsed; the siblings sank into ennui, nostalgic for business life. Bringing Pierrette back exposed their neglect; bitter and sidelined, they befriended lower bourgeois figures like Colonel Gouraud and lawyer Vinet, who exploited their wealth. The story shows provincial hierarchies and social codes ultimately trump money.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
After her grandparents were hospitalized, Pierrette—ashamed to live on charity—was relieved when wealthier relatives agreed to take her in. Brigaut, a childhood friend and her mother’s friend’s son, gave her sixty francs for the trip to Paris; she accepted without thanks. Penniless, a conductor briefly paid for a hotel but treated her like baggage; four days later a kinder conductor took her to Mademoiselle Rogron’s, where her cousins received her with astonishment.

Dressed in Breton clothes and carrying a small bundle, Pierrette was shy and spoke little while Sylvie and Rogron questioned her. Shown a modest room that seemed luxurious to her, she quickly became the focus of Sylvie’s anxiety over her lack of proper clothes; Sylvie resolved to buy her a wardrobe. The welcome revealed a chilling preoccupation with appearances. Where Pierrette had known affection and freedom in Brittany, the Rogrons prized possessions and outward respectability. Reproached the next morning for dirty shoes, she found their constant fault-finding and concern for material matters sharply at odds with the love she expected. Her innocence made their disdain especially painful; she began to stifle her joy and grow passive to avoid scorn.

The Rogrons’ obsession with appearances treated her as a burden and suffocated her spirit. When she tore a dress playing, she braced for stern rebuke. Life in Provins worsened as Sylvie forbade visits to friends and pursued social ambitions with Vinet and the colonel. As they plotted for status—dining, alliances, and local influence—Pierrette was increasingly confined and instructed; she was transformed from a lively child into a pale, silent shadow, enduring self-interested care that was not love.

One night, when Pierrette went to say goodnight, Sylvie turned away coldly and made her cry. Pierrette protested that her rich cousin was not as kind as her poor grandmother; Sylvie retorted that the grandmother had taken money while the cousin would leave her some—Pierrette wanted affection, not wealth. Accused of mischief, disobedience, and breaking a watch, she left ashamed. Rogron demanded punishment; Madame Vinet suggested school; the colonel hoped instruction might keep her quiet. Vinet called her a rough diamond to be polished and hired a writing master. The lessons produced ink stains and scattered papers blamed on Pierrette, and she was sharply told she must earn her keep. Rogron’s severity and Sylvie’s indifference deepened her isolation; complaints of pains were dismissed.

Only Adèle, the servant, showed kindness, secretly warming Pierrette’s bed. Meanwhile the colonel and Vinet blamed her Breton nature and pursued local prestige—investing in a newspaper—while Pierrette’s situation worsened. She considered escaping to her grandparents, but a death and Rogron’s appointment as guardian prevented it; Vinet hinted at an inheritance and Rogron secured a promise of eight thousand francs from her grandmother.

Preparations for Pierrette’s first communion altered the household. Sylvie insisted on the zealous priest Monsieur Habert, whose influence gave Pierrette solace; she abandoned escape plans and embraced the Church’s teachings, a change even Sylvie imitated. Habert’s sister offered to take her to school, but the Rogrons refused, fearing a convent education. Later, with the arrival of the Chargeboeufs and the alluring Bathilde, Sylvie forced Pierrette into domestic tasks. Without Adèle’s protection she was treated worse than the servants—scolded for trifles, denied praise—and over the years her health and spirit faded into a silent, overlooked shadow.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Before recounting the domestic drama Jacques Brigaut’s arrival would provoke in the Rogron family, it is necessary to explain how he came to Provins, since he plays a significant but passive role.

Distressed by Pierrette’s change from lively to pale and sorrowful, Brigaut left the Rogron house and watched her from the Upper town. After a short apprenticeship in Paris he returned to Provins as a journeyman cabinetmaker so he could stay near her, concealing his feelings while learning about the family. Madame Frappier revealed the Rogrons’ dubious acquisition of Pierrette’s grandfather’s property. Brigaut often glimpsed Pierrette at market and church; her frailty and furtive signals for him to remain hidden deepened his concern.

Pierrette fretted about changes since Brigaut’s return but did not grasp the Rogrons’ internal strife, especially between Sylvie and Mademoiselle Habert (Céleste), who maneuvered for advantageous marriages. Sylvie, forty-two, feared aging alone; Céleste, ten years younger and urged on by her priest brother, actively sought a husband. A recent political shift had emboldened local Liberals and intensified competition for social standing in Provins.

Sylvie consulted a doctor about marrying at her age; his discouraging prognosis heightened her anxiety. Céleste urged delay to remove a rival. Sylvie grew jealous of the colonel’s attention to Pierrette. Vinet, a lawyer with designs on Bathilde, exploited Sylvie: he plotted to separate the colonel from Pierrette and divert the Rogron fortune to Bathilde, persuading the colonel to court Pierrette while secretly scheming for Bathilde’s benefit. After overhearing Brigaut serenade Pierrette, Sylvie concluded a secret admirer was involved and, one tense morning, accused Pierrette of concealment. Pierrette’s silence and the household pressure worsened her health. Brigaut offered brief comfort, but Sylvie’s relentless scrutiny deepened Pierrette’s isolation, entwining love, jealousy, and ambition and setting the stage for the tragedy to follow.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For a month Rogron had stopped bringing the Constitutionnel to Gouraud, so Gouraud began coming himself to gossip and walk with Sylvie. Eager to see him, Sylvie dressed her best; when he arrived Rogron excused himself, leaving them alone.

Gouraud asked after Sylvie’s cousin; she said the cousin was unwell. He grew uneasy: a half-pay officer, he feared Vinet’s rising influence with the Rogrons would sideline him. He suspected Vinet controlled their political plans and that Rogron’s infatuation with Bathilde de Chargeboeuf threatened his prospects. He even worried Vinet might steer him toward Pierrette as a ploy to benefit Vinet and Bathilde.

Alone with Sylvie he sensed her unease; when she tentatively mentioned Pierrette he dismissed any affection for the girl, claiming he preferred a more mature partner. Relieved, Sylvie concealed her intentions and hinted her brother had once considered Pierrette a match. Sensing her interest, Gouraud declared he would marry Sylvie, delighting Rogron. Sylvie began planning their home but remained wary of Pierrette’s interactions with the colonel.

As dinner neared Sylvie scolded Pierrette for a stain but still ordered her to dress nicely—suspicious of collusion. The Chargeboeuf ladies arrived; Bathilde, spectacularly dressed at Vinet’s urging, treated marriage to Rogron as a stepping stone to influence, using beauty and connections for independence.

The evening grew tense: Bathilde’s condescension and Vinet’s remarks sharpened rivalries. At cards Pierrette’s innocent comments provoked Sylvie, and Pierrette left in tears. That night Sylvie brooded on men’s duplicity and her precarious position. The next day Pierrette suffered a painful fall; Sylvie blamed disobedience and showed no sympathy, widening the rift.

The following week was relentless torment for Pierrette under Sylvie’s escalating cruelty. She concealed Brigaut’s arrival to avoid anger, but longed for him. Determined to communicate, Brigaut arranged a nightly note exchange: he slipped a message while pretending to buy fruit and asked her to lower a string from her window.

Pierrette brought back his heartfelt letter and, despite difficulty obtaining paper without waking Sylvie, replied. Their hopeful correspondence continued even as Pierrette’s health declined. One evening she fainted; in a struggle over her letters she was hurt and distressed. At Brigaut’s appeals Madame Lorrain arrived, witnessed the violence, summoned medical aid, and promised protection. As Pierrette recovered, her bond with Madame Lorrain deepened and the prospect of a future with her grandmother and Brigaut offered a hopeful glimmer. Pierrette remained determined to reclaim her life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At nine o’clock Monsieur Martener told Monsieur Tiphaine about Pierrette’s mistreatment by the Rogrons. Tiphaine brought in Auffray, a notary relative, and long-standing enmities between the Vinet and Tiphaine factions—heightened by damaging truths about Madame Tiphaine’s family—gave the matter political weight.

Tiphaine consulted the public prosecutor; he, Auffray, and Martener arranged to petition the courts to make Auffray Pierrette’s guardian so a Family Council, supported by medical testimony, could remove her from the Rogrons’ control. By midday the town buzzed as neighbors heard Pierrette’s cries and rumors spread. Doctors Bianchon and Brigaut examined her and confirmed abuse. Vinet, the Rogrons’ lawyer, warned of criminal exposure; Sylvie, initially evasive, later admitted jealousy and the truth.

To stifle scandal and serve the Rogrons’ political aims, Vinet arranged a marriage contract, then filed a petition accusing Brigaut and the widow Lorrain of abducting Pierrette to portray his clients as victims. The hearing was postponed, increasing public interest. The Tiphaines rallied support; Pierrette, unaware of politics, was cared for by her grandmother and Brigaut. The doctors, appalled by her condition, unanimously condemned the Rogrons’ treatment.

At the reconvened hearing, Auffray presented the physicians’ report to justify protecting Pierrette. Vinet countered, but the judge ordered she be kept from the Rogrons until the Family Council met. The Council removed Rogron as guardian and appointed Auffray; evidence produced a civil suit for neglect and abuse.

As the legal battle continued, Pierrette’s health declined. Martener and the Auffrays grew fond of her; her grandmother’s care and Brigaut’s visits comforted her. A critical operation in March 1828 offered little hope; she forgave her cousins and soon died. Her funeral filled the town; the Rogrons, fearing outrage, stayed away. Community sympathy hindered their maneuvers and Vinet faced backlash, yet they largely retained political standing. Only a few—Martener, Brigaut, and like‑minded friends—remembered the reality of Pierrette’s suffering, leaving justice and accountability unresolved.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

Dedication: To David, Sculptor—Balzac thanks sculpture’s permanence compared with other precarious fame.

In autumn 1826 Abbé Birotteau, short, corpulent, about sixty and prone to gout, was caught in a rainstorm returning from a friend’s house. He hurried across the deserted Cloister behind Saint‑Gatien cathedral, careless of wet shoes after winning at cards with Madame de Listomère and buoyed by hopes of a long‑desired canonry; so preoccupied he even neglected his umbrella.

The Cloister, once home to Chapter dignitaries, had become the narrow Rue de la Psalette. On its left stood a single house long let to priests and owned by the devout, discreet Mademoiselle Gamard, who preserved the property through the Restoration by boarding clerics. She could shelter only two lodgers; for twelve years she had housed Abbé Troubert and the late Abbé Chapeloud. When Chapeloud died, Birotteau—who had long admired the apartment’s furnishings during visits—took his place.

Chapeloud had been a close friend; his death left Birotteau both bereaved and relieved. He inherited Chapeloud’s tasteful library and furniture, comforts he had envied and viewed as markers of the status he sought. Becoming Gamard’s boarder and securing the canonry seemed to promise those comforts. Installed in the rooms he had coveted, he imagined attentions and domestic ease, but gratitude soon gave way to renewed obsession with preferment.

One rainy evening he returned to find the house door locked and had to ring repeatedly; his servant Marianne explained Gamard assumed he was already in. He noticed other petty omissions—no slippers, no fire—and felt slighted. These trivial neglects pricked his vanity and suggested intentional disrespect. Innocent and lacking worldly tact, he could not perceive that the lapses were deliberate.

Mademoiselle Gamard’s discontent had grown during Birotteau’s eighteen months’ tenancy. She prized social recognition and longed for a salon, ambitions frustrated by his indifference and evenings with friends. As he distanced himself she nursed a quiet vengeance. Absorbed by hopes of preferment, Birotteau confronted symptoms, not causes, turning small domestic grievances into a deeper conflict of pride, naïveté, and provincial intrigue—a prologue to the wider tale.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The next morning Birotteau woke hopeful about a canonry, briefly forgetting the previous night’s ill omens. He rang for Marianne; before she returned, Abbé Troubert, on his regular visit, arrived. Troubert noticed the absent fire, called for Marianne, and asked after Birotteau’s health and the canonry. Unaware Troubert resented Madame de Listomère for not inviting him to her house, Birotteau described his efforts to obtain the position.

The two abbés contrasted sharply. Troubert was tall, thin, serious, with a wrinkled, sarcastic face and a calm exterior that masked a dulled intellect some blamed on subservience to Mademoiselle Gamard; Birotteau was plump, round-faced, and jovial, his amiability making him popular. Neither seemed likely to rise far—Troubert, fifty and ailing, might at best become vicar-general; Birotteau’s good nature kept him comfortable. Though Abbé Chapeloud quietly opposed Troubert’s promotion, Troubert treated him respectfully despite Chapeloud’s skepticism.

Troubert asked to borrow the Register of Bishops, which Birotteau lent. Carrying heavy books downstairs, they met Mademoiselle Gamard’s sharp disapproval; she scolded Birotteau for cluttering her dining room, stinging him with ill temper—a jarring contrast to his usual cheerfulness. Breakfast was tense; weighed down by her displeasure, Birotteau could not engage. Gamard’s bitter demeanor, traced to social frustration and resentment as an unmarried woman, showed in sharp features and abrupt movements. Feeling oppressed, Birotteau left for the garden, found Gamard and Troubert watching him, escaped into church duties, and on returning discovered he had missed dinner—Gamard had set the clock ahead to avoid waiting, a petty manipulation protecting her propriety at his expense.

Eight days later Birotteau discovered Gamard’s hostility—visible since the candlestick-and-clock incidents—was part of a six-month plot against him. Previously oblivious, he now felt suffocated by her malice as she savored revenge while he, absorbed by minor worries, had confided in no one. His anxiety worsened his health, so he sought Troubert’s help. Embarrassed to complain, he described Gamard’s provocations; Troubert listened with restrained amusement and advised understanding her, attributing it to ill health.

Desperate, Birotteau hoped a stay at Madame de Listomère’s country house would ease tensions, not realizing Gamard could exploit his absence. After ten days, Monsieur Caron, Gamard’s lawyer, reported she was alarmed by his prolonged leave and wanted clarity to avoid disputes. Back among friends, speculation followed: Madame de Listomère suggested Troubert coveted Birotteau’s apartment; Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix warned the vicar-general’s illness might put appointment power in Troubert’s hands.

Madame de Listomère urged Birotteau to pursue the canonry. Friends proposed yielding to Gamard and Troubert if it aided advancement. But the struggle intensified. Caron returned asking Birotteau to sign a document relinquishing his residence; pressed by friends, he signed. He then learned he had signed away possessions Gamard claimed as compensation for alleged board differences. Confronted, she coldly cited the agreement. Devastated, he fled to Madame de Listomère and found his belongings packed. Mademoiselle Salomon, fearing for his sanity, took him back to the Alouette, where he confided in friends.

Monsieur de Bourbonne examined the deed, found it exploitative, and urged legal action. The group debated suing to recover property versus compromising to avoid scandal; they resolved to support Birotteau, though Bourbonne warned many would desert him if the fight grew bitter. An expert appraisal exposed the true value of his goods and Gamard’s fraud. As legal proceedings opened, town gossip and political divisions intensified; the outcome remained uncertain against the alliance of Gamard and Troubert.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
After Birotteau’s quarrel, Baron de Listomere was warned of retirement and dismissed. The minister thought he had offended Abbé Troubert by siding with Radicals. The family placated Troubert. The baroness forced Birotteau to withdraw his suit against Gamard; he complied, asking for the portrait. Gamard fell ill and gossip blamed the suit. She presented his withdrawal; Troubert feigned indifference, then returned with Gamard’s refusal to hand over the portrait and accepted peace. On Gamard’s death she left her fortune to Troubert, worsening the Listomeres’ plight. At the funeral Troubert eulogized; Birotteau mourned alone. Troubert became Bishop of Troyes; her death left him an annuity, and his rise threatened the family as Birotteau was sent to a distant parish, grieving.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

DEDICATION

To Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy.

Dear Nodier, this book exposes actions hidden behind domestic doors where, too often, divine intervention replaces human justice. Its morals concern family and motherhood and the harms that follow when paternal authority—the traditional tribunal for domestic wrongs—is weakened. A loving mother cannot substitute for patriarchal power. The story defends indissoluble marriage and warns against unchecked selfishness in a society that exalts wealth and success while the law proves impotent. I hope such a society will return to Catholic moral guidance through different education.

In “Scenes from Military Life” I will portray noble self-sacrifice and also show war’s corrupting influence on those at home who live as if on a battlefield. You have observed our times keenly; I entrust this tale to your name as a shield from criticism.

Your sincere admirer,  
De Balzac  
Paris, November 1842.

THE TWO BROTHERS

CHAPTER I

In 1792 the people of Issoudun disliked Dr. Rouget; gossip blamed him for his beautiful wife’s unhappiness, though the household’s true nature remained secret. Madame Rouget, née Descoings, had been frail from youth. They had a son, Jean-Jacques, and, ten years later, a daughter, Agathe.

Rouget’s insistence on masculine firmness caused much female suffering. The Descoings family—once wealthy wool merchants who invested in forfeited estates—were rich and miserly. Descoings, Rouget’s brother-in-law, left Issoudun for Paris, failed as a grocer, and later sought a wealthy widow. Expecting his wife’s death, Rouget sent Agathe to Paris to secure Descoings’ favor and a share of any inheritance.

Agathe, admired in town, provoked a quarrel between Rouget and their friend Lousteau, who then left. Madame Rouget, learning her daughter had gone, predicted she would never see Agathe again—a prophecy fulfilled as her health declined, worsened by her son’s neglect; Jean-Jacques offered little comfort.

Agathe reached Paris as Descoings was imprisoned for imprudent political remarks during the famine and was later executed alongside the poet André Chénier—an event that silenced local grocers and left a sharper mark on the community than posterity would note.

Agathe married Bridau, a ministry secretary, with Rouget’s approval; Rouget hoped to profit from any Descoings inheritance. Bridau, captivated by Agathe, supported Napoleon, earned promotion in 1804, and worked relentlessly. In 1808 he died of overwork just as another promotion—intended by the Emperor—arrived too late.

After Bridau’s death Agathe received no letters from Issoudun. Her mother died shortly before the birth of her second son; her father died around the Emperor’s coronation, an event that distracted Bridau. Jean-Jacques remained absent. Madame Hochon, Agathe’s godmother, warned she would inherit nothing unless she granted power of attorney to her brother; Agathe hesitated. Bridau’s savings provided only a modest income; Agathe and Madame Descoings lived on twelve thousand francs.

By 1809 Madame Descoings, sixty-five but fond of theater and fine food, was secretly gambling and hiding mounting lottery debts while concealing family scandals about her son Bixiou. Devoted and refusing to remarry, Agathe took on maternal and household duties. Her limited judgment and blind trust in Madame Descoings proved disastrous when the debts emerged; Agathe sold possessions and liquidated investments to settle them—a decisive turning point in their lives.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One of the bleakest stretches of Paris was rue Mazarin between rue Guenegard and rue de Seine, shadowed by college walls and Cardinal Mazarin’s library. On the third floor of a damp, cold house there lived the poor widow Madame Bridau (Agathe). From her windows she watched the Institute opposite and the students’ uproar at examinations, and to brighten the room she defied police rules by setting boxes of earth on the sill for little gardens.

The shallow house, reached by a winding staircase, held three rooms—a dining room, a small salon, and a bedroom—plus a kitchen and two single rooms. Agathe chose it because it was cheap (four hundred francs a year), near her sons’ school and in a familiar neighborhood. The interior matched the building’s dreariness: yellowed wallpaper, unglazed tiles, and only essential furniture. The salon held an Aubusson carpet given to her and a mahogany sofa bearing a pastel portrait of her late husband, Bridau, which captured his noble essence; a Napoleon hung above a card table.

After Bridau’s death Agathe’s life narrowed. She filled the room with two large birdcages—canaries and Java sparrows—and millet, chickweed and cat hair littered the floor and furniture, outward signs of grief and neglected personal care. She preserved everything belonging to her husband, even wrapping his last pen with a note. Grief seemed to erode her sense of womanhood; she devoted herself entirely to her children and to managing the precarious household.

Agathe lived for her sons and worried ceaselessly over their finances. The Emperor provided three hundred francs a year for each child, with scholarships supplementing this. Her aunt, Madame Descoings, on the second floor, assigned three thousand francs from her annuity to help Agathe; the Descoings themselves now lived modestly on twelve hundred francs a year and shared dinners with the two widows. Madame Descoings doted on Philippe and Joseph, indulging them with treats and small sums. Distrustful of the future, Agathe scrimped and saved—she set aside three thousand francs from her pension—hoping to secure some inheritance for her boys. Thus both widows had passed from hollow opulence to voluntary poverty, each for different reasons.

The house, the students’ uproar, and her husband’s portrait formed the setting that would shape her youngest son, Joseph Bridau, into a notable painter of the modern French school. Philippe, the elder, resembled his mother in looks but had a bold demeanor often mistaken for courage; at fifteen his charm reinforced Agathe’s hopes. Joseph, three years younger, took after his father in less favorable ways: untidy, often scolded, and plainly less favored by his mother.

Joseph’s artistic bent was sparked by a visit to the Tuileries, where a caricature on an Institute wall captivated him and made him want to draw. He began sneaking into the Institute courtyard, enduring teasing from older students until his persistence won their respect. He later visited the sculptor Chaudet’s atelier; Chaudet recognized and encouraged his talent. Agathe feared art meant hardship and tried to dissuade him, but Chaudet defended the vocation as a divine calling, insisting true talent must be followed.

Meanwhile Philippe, enchanted by military life, secretly petitioned the Emperor to enlist. He was sent to the Imperial Lyceum at Saint-Cyr and rose rapidly, becoming a captain by nineteen. After his scholarship was withdrawn, Joseph tried to earn a living by art with Madame Descoings’s encouragement. In 1816 Joseph persuaded his mother to let him convert the adjoining garret into an atelier—partly financed by Madame Descoings—installing a skylight and continuing studies under Gros and Schinner. Unlike the two widows, who treated painting as a trade, Joseph embraced it passionately.

Political unrest simmered in France. Philippe was implicated in conspiracies, briefly arrested, then released for lack of evidence, though his military pay was cut and Agathe feared for him. Friends urged the widows to send him abroad for safety; though she resisted the idea of him serving a foreign power, Philippe joined General Lallemand in the United States to help found the Champ d’Asile—an enterprise that failed. Agathe had earlier equipped him with ten thousand francs; by late 1817 the household had adjusted to reduced means and Joseph lived frugally to support the family while his talent developed.

In 1818 the notary Roguin absconded with funds intended for Madame Descoings. Soon after, Philippe sent a bill for a thousand francs, having lost everything in the Champ d’Asile. Agathe, distraught, blamed herself for urging his departure. Despite her own difficulties, Madame Descoings continued to support them. Desperate, Joseph appealed to Gérard, who arranged for him to paint copies of a portrait of Louis XVIII; Joseph worked tirelessly, selling panels to cover Philippe’s debt.

When Philippe returned, Agathe welcomed him warmly but failed to notice how America had hardened him. He became self-centered—drinking, gambling and swaggering—and lived off others, disappearing for days in cafés and gaming halls. He refused to serve the Bourbons, neglected family duties, and sought to impress Mariette; a proposal to turn his experiences into journalism and a cashier’s post at a newspaper, secured by Agathe and Madame Descoings, brought temporary relief but little reform. Joseph felt increasingly overshadowed and unappreciated. Philippe’s return and altered behavior strained relationships and finances, exposing the consequences of choices, the limits of maternal devotion, and the fragile balance between ambition and family duty that governed the Bridau household.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Three months on, the colonel—who “ate for four”—still complained about the food while the widows, reluctant to press him, kept paying though he gave nothing. His mother and Madame Descoings avoided reminding him of his promise; months passed without the “tigers with five claws,” as Léon Gozlan joked, migrating from Philippe’s pocket into the household purse. The colonel eased his conscience by seldom dining at home. “Well, he is happy,” his mother said, “he has a place.”

Prompted by a feuilleton edited by Vernou, Mariette debuted at the Porte-Saint-Martin and triumphed with Begrand. A wealthy general, enchanted and connected to Philippe and Giroudeau, became her impresario and arranged the debut—an instance of solidarity among admirers. Bixiou soon told his grandmother and Agathe that Philippe, the cashier, was in love with Mariette, the celebrated ballet-dancer. The widows were appalled; Agathe deemed stage women morally suspect and both feared extravagant spending. “Do you think my brother will spend on Mariette? Such women ruin rich men,” Joseph protested. Bixiou suggested Mariette might be stolen by higher-ranking admirers; he’d even heard an ambassador was infatuated.

Partly for financial reasons, Philippe proposed; Mariette declined, perhaps suspecting his intent or valuing freedom, hoping instead for a Grand Opera engagement. Over the next year Philippe visited his mother only twice a month, spending days at the office, the theatre, or with Mariette, enjoying pleasures and mixing with Tullia and other entertainers. The carnival lasted eighteen months but grew stormy. After her Opera debut in January 1821 Mariette attracted a distinguished duke. Philippe, driven by passion, began dipping into the funds he managed—taking eleven hundred francs by May. Mariette left for London to seek patrons while the temporary opera house was prepared. Despite her infidelities and using him as a stepping stone, Philippe remained deeply in love.

Forced to relinquish Mariette, Philippe returned to his mother’s apartment, unable to imagine life without recent pleasures. Suicide crossed his mind—not just over the deficit but because he could not bear life without Mariette. He entered Joseph’s studio, where Joseph was copying a picture for a dealer and discussing earnings. Pale and subdued, Philippe asked how long his portrait would take, saying he wanted to leave his mother a likeness. When Joseph asked if he was leaving again, Philippe said he was going never to return and, pressed, confessed he had taken money and could not live with the dishonor.

Joseph tried to reassure him that repayment would save his honor, but Philippe, despairing, fled downstairs, leaving Joseph anxious. Joseph told Madame Descoings, who was horrified. Agathe was devastated, crying at the thought of Philippe’s dishonor. That day Philippe calmly submitted his accounts, claiming he’d retained the money for safekeeping. He then went to a gambling house—winning at first, then losing everything. Returning home, he found his family’s concern and affection, which briefly comforted him. “Promise not to kill yourself, and all shall be forgotten,” his mother pleaded. Philippe, sensing the plea had worked, feigned gratitude and affection; they shared dinner and he felt momentarily relieved.

The next day Agathe seemed older and more anxious, aware the family’s finances were precarious. Madame Descoings suggested consulting Desroches to manage the affair and preserve their honor. Desroches, diplomatically, advised Agathe to sign a transfer of funds and petition for Philippe’s reinstatement in the army, assuring that he would regain his rank and easing some fear. Yet Agathe’s faith was shaken and she lived in constant dread of his downfall.

Philippe returned to a life of leisure—gambling, drinking, looking disheveled and indifferent to the family’s plight. His love for Mariette had hardened him; he treated women with contempt. Joseph, focusing on painting, gained recognition and Madame Descoings’s support; she pinned her hopes on his future. Noticing discrepancies, Joseph began to suspect Philippe and confided in Madame Descoings. When Joseph discovered Philippe had indeed taken money from him, he felt betrayed; despite anger he still struggled to confront his brother. Madame Descoings urged sympathy for Philippe’s struggles, but Joseph remained wary of his influence on the family’s future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Despite Philippe’s careful nightly gambling, he was sometimes “cleaned out.” Needing his usual ten francs, he began stealing from family—pilfering from his brother and whatever Madame Descoings or Agathe left about. After a dream in which Philippe robbed her, Agathe recognized his decline and begged him to ask for money instead of stealing. Within two weeks her savings were gone; she took worsted work for twenty sous a day, endured sleepless nights waiting for Philippe, and watched his increasing drunkenness and mood swings.

When Madame Descoings suggested new clothes, Philippe retorted that his mother had no money and that he had mortgaged his pension. He admitted borrowing from Giroudeau, calling it a “debt of honor.” Agathe excused him as out of his element, while Joseph criticized his neglect of appearance and duty. Philippe sank further into debauchery and gambling, losing the steadiness and good looks he once had.

One day Philippe asked Joseph for money to pay a tobacconist; Joseph refused. Philippe then guessed where Madame kept her savings and, expecting she’d be out buying Christmas treats, broke into her room. He found twenty napoleons hidden in the mattress and took the money to gamble.

At the gambling house Philippe won substantial sums over several sessions and, intoxicated by success, amassed over one hundred fifty thousand francs by night’s end. Pressed by greed, he continued and lost everything. The next morning he awoke in despair, knowing he had ruined his family.

Agathe and Madame Descoings waited for Philippe at Christmas dinner; when he failed to show they worried. Madame had pinned hopes on a lottery stake, but upon checking her savings she found the money gone and fainted. The concierge confirmed Philippe had been in the apartment. Agathe returned pale and shaken; the family confronted Philippe. He dismissed their accusations, claiming he’d taken what was owed him. A fight erupted between Joseph and Philippe, and Madame Descoings suffered a stroke amid the chaos.

Joseph offered his mother money afterward, but she refused, clinging to lottery hope. When the winning numbers were announced it was clear she’d missed her chance; she died shortly after. Philippe, recovering from illness, was told by Agathe that their finances had collapsed and urged to rejoin the army. Indifferent and feeling abandoned, he accepted the money she reluctantly offered and left to seek the company of those he called true friends. Agathe, heartbroken, prayed for her son and resigned herself to the loss of the child she once loved.

By February 1822 Madame Bridau had been moved into the attic above the kitchen of her former apartment, recently occupied by her son Philippe. Seeing his mother reduced, Joseph set out to make her comfortable: he rearranged the garret, added better furnishings, and took Agathe daily to a table d’hôte that cost him nearly a hundred francs a month. Alarmed by the expense and urged by her confessor, Agathe accepted a seven-hundred-franc-a-year post at a lottery office that demanded long hours.

She adapted to the routine—preparing Joseph’s breakfasts, mending his clothes, keeping the studio tidy—and aided his work despite having no artistic training. Their bond deepened through these small acts of tenderness. They spoke often of Philippe—Agathe feared his reckless life, but Joseph dismissed her worries, convinced Philippe was prospering in New York.

One afternoon Joseph visited Philippe, who worked at a newspaper. Philippe invited Joseph to dine; Joseph declined to stay with his mother. When he described Philippe afterward, Agathe felt hopeful. She decorated Joseph’s studio and prepared an elaborate breakfast for a sitting for Philippe’s portrait; overwhelmed, she hid and watched the two sons together. After the sitting Philippe invited them to a sumptuous dinner, which Agathe eagerly accepted while Joseph felt uneasy at the ostentation. At another sitting she learned more of Philippe’s circle—journalists and actresses—and heard boastful plans, though he remained vague about money.

Later Joseph postponed a session for a commission. Philippe, mistaking a valuable copy of a Rubens for an original, took it and sold it for a fraction of its worth. Discovering the theft devastated Joseph; Agathe was heartbroken, feeling she had lost her son to vice.

Two months after, an old officer told Agathe Philippe was ill and needed money. Working at the lottery, she had little to spare and at first refused to help the son she felt she had banished from her heart; Joseph insisted Philippe go to the hospital. When she later found Philippe destitute on the street, she gave what she could and despaired.

Agathe then learned Philippe had been implicated in a conspiracy and was arrested; she fainted. Joseph consulted Desroches, who urged them to secure the family inheritance and gather evidence of undue influence; he planned to visit Issoudun at the holidays to help. Agathe’s letter to her godmother brought a discouraging reply about an unhelpful brother. Agathe and Joseph set off for Issoudun, leaving Philippe to face the consequences and Agathe to confront their deep estrangement.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Armand Peremet’s excavations in Issoudun uncovered a fifth‑century basilica rebuilt with stones from a Roman temple to Isis; the town’s name—Isis + dun—suggests Celtic origins.
